summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Interim determination of proceedings involving three children: E (11 years old), F (7 years old), and G (4 years old).

  • Father seeks a change in living arrangements for children if parents no longer share a house.

  • Mother seeks dismissal of Father's application.

  • Litigation history includes previous living arrangements with Father having children four nights per week.

  • Current orders allow children to live with Mother if parents cease to share a residence.

  • Father filed an Application in a Case in June 2018 and relied on affidavits for his case.

  • Mother filed a response to the Application in a Case and relied on her own affidavit.

  • Father's application was based on a change to 50-50 time split for children if parents no longer share a residence.

  • Father critiqued a single expert report by Dr. C, highlighting deficiencies and contentious facts.

  • Father argued for a revisiting of orders based on new material not available during the previous hearing.

  • Court considered the controversial findings of Dr. C regarding Father's alleged abusive behavior and parenting deficiencies.

  • Court balanced the risks posed by Father against the potential psychological harm to children.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • E (11 years old)

  • F (7 years old)

  • G (4 years old)

### Expert Evidence

  • Single expert report by Dr. C critiqued by Father.

### Orders

  • Father's application for 50-50 time split dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • Court balanced risks posed by Father against potential harm to children.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['Interim decision-making limitations due to resource constraints and prolonged interim orders.']
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • Interim decision-making constraints due to limited factual conclusions.

  • Resource constraints impact the ability to safeguard children's best interests effectively.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • Court balanced risks posed by Father against potential harm to children.

  • Acknowledged limitations of interim decision-making due to resource constraints.

  • Emphasized the importance of prompt and accurate justice for children's best interests.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final hearing on competing parenting applications over three days in July 2018.

  • ICL seeks equal contribution from father and mother for costs estimated at $33,000.

  • Father filed submissions opposing costs, citing financial hardship and previous expenses.

  • Mother did not file any submissions regarding costs.

  • ICL appointed in 2017, estimated costs from May 2017 including trials and interim hearings.

  • Father's submissions highlighted equal time orders, financial constraints, expert report costs, and failed negotiations by mother.

  • Father argued against bearing any responsibility for ICL's costs based on history of offers and litigation.

  • ICL funded by Legal Aid, father paid for Court expert exceeding $10,000.

  • Judge dismissed ICL's application for costs against both parents.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The judge dismissed the ICL's application for costs against both parents, considering financial circumstances and lack of submissions from the mother."]
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The judge evaluated the financial positions of both parents and the lack of submissions from the mother.

  • Consideration of financial hardship and previous expenses led to the dismissal of the ICL's costs application.

  • Overall assessment indicated that neither parent should bear responsibility for the ICL's costs.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge found that circumstances did not justify either parent paying a proportion of the ICL's costs.

  • Financial constraints, previous expenses, and lack of submissions influenced the decision.

  • ICL's application for costs was dismissed based on the judge's assessment of the situation.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The Mother, born in 1980, moved to Australia in 2006 and holds a permanent residence visa.

  • The Father, born in 1985, is a British national residing in C Town, United Kingdom.

  • The parties agreed the child, B, would be born in Australia to assist future immigration prospects.

  • The Mother and her older children moved to Australia in June 2016; the Father joined them in July 2016 on a three-month visitor visa.

  • The child, B, was born in Australia in August 2016.

  • The Father returned to the UK in October 2016; the Mother and children followed on 9 November 2016 on return tickets dated for April 2017.

  • The child entered the UK on a British Holiday Visa valid until April 2017.

  • The parental relationship ended on 3 December 2016 following allegations of domestic conflict.

  • On 20 December 2016, a UK Court in City L refused the Father's ex parte application for a passport order, finding the child was habitually resident in Australia.

  • The Mother returned to Australia with the child on 1 February 2017.

  • The Director-General of the Department of Communities, Child Safety and Disability Services initially filed the return application but later withdrew, leaving the Father to prosecute the matter.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, born in August 2016 (approximately 1 year old at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of the Mother smacking the child in November 2016 (denied).

  • Allegations of the Mother assaulting the Father in December 2016 (admitted as a 'lash out' during breastfeeding).

  • Concerns raised by UK Social Services regarding the Father's mental health and controlling behavior.

### Expert Evidence

  • C Town County Council Children and Young Peoples’ Services Assessment (Exhibit 5) found no concerns regarding the Mother's care or mental health.

  • Evidence from a pharmacist, Ms N, regarding a conversation about a 'two-week holiday' was contested.

### Best Interests

  • The court focused on the jurisdictional issue of habitual residence rather than a full best-interests welfare analysis, as per the Hague Convention framework.

### Orders

  • The Application for a return order filed on 26 May 2017 is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The child was habitually resident in Australia immediately before the removal on 1 February 2017.

  • The trip to the UK in November 2016 was a temporary visit/holiday to introduce the child to family, evidenced by return tickets for April 2017 and school exemptions obtained in Australia.

  • The Father's own evidence in previous UK proceedings (City L) supported a 'clear and settled intention' to move to Australia.

  • The child's presence in the UK was on a visitor visa, and no joint steps were taken to secure her permanent status there.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Mother's purchase of return tickets for April 2017 and the Australian school exemption for the older children were critical indicators of a lack of settled purpose in the UK.

  • The Father's failure to produce his own statement from the 20 December 2016 UK proceedings led to an adverse inference that its contents would not support his case.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 (Cth) Reg 16(1A) - requirements for wrongful removal.

  • Reg 16(1)(c) - failure to establish wrongful removal/retention.

  • The 'habitual residence' test as defined in LK v DG [2009] HCA 9.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Father failed to provide the statement accompanying his UK Application despite multiple court warnings.

  • Lack of documentary evidence supporting the Father's claim that the Mother declared UK residency to claim benefits.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother admitted to slapping the Father on 1 December 2016 while holding the child, resulting in a police caution.

  • Father made multiple reports to Social Services alleging Mother was unkempt and assaulting the child.

### Protection Orders

  • No formal protection orders mentioned, though Mother changed locks on advice of UK social workers.

### Child Exposure

  • Child was present during the physical altercation on 1 December 2016.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the violence but primarily used it to explain the breakdown of the relationship and the Mother's subsequent actions, rather than as a bar to return under Reg 16(3)(b) (grave risk), which was not reached because habitual residence was not established.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Social Services records (Exhibit 5) indicated the Father's reports were likely motivated by his own mental health or relationship distress rather than actual risk posed by the Mother.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the nature of the conflict.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of the severity and context of the domestic incidents.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case reinforces the 'broad factual inquiry' required to determine habitual residence under the Hague Convention, emphasizing that physical presence and parental intention are not the sole determinants."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court applied the rule in Jones v Dunkel, drawing an adverse inference from the Father's failure to produce his UK court statement, which was deemed critical to understanding the parties' original intentions.

  • The 'underlying reality' of the child's connection was found to be with Australia, where she was born and where the Mother (primary carer) held permanent status and a clear intent to reside.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 and the High Court's interpretation of 'habitual residence' in LK v DG.

  • Section 111B of the Family Law Act 1975 as the enabling provision for the Convention.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Father's credibility was severely undermined by his refusal to provide the UK statement and his attempts to 'explain away' contemporaneous Facebook messages that contradicted his court testimony.

  • The Father's evidence regarding the 'naming ceremony' was found to be misleading, as he omitted the fact that it was also a 'farewell' party for the Mother's return to Australia.

  • The Mother was found generally more reliable, with her testimony corroborated by travel documents and school records.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous Orders are discharged.

  • The Application in Form 2, filed by the Department of Communities, Child Safety and Disability Services on 26 May 2017 and later prosecuted by Mr Hibbert, is dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge preferred the Mother's evidence, finding the Father's testimony 'highly selective' and 'unconvincing'.

  • The court determined that the parties had a shared, settled intention for the child to live in Australia permanently, which was formed prior to the child's birth and partially implemented by the Mother's relocation in mid-2016.

  • The breakdown of the relationship in the UK did not unilaterally shift the child's habitual residence to the UK, as the Mother's intention to return to Australia remained constant and was supported by objective evidence (return tickets).

  • The UK Court's prior finding on 20 December 2016 that it lacked jurisdiction due to the child's Australian habitual residence was given significant weight.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are citizens of Country E who arrived in Australia in July 2015 with their two eldest children.

  • The mother came to Australia on a student visa, which she claims was to escape danger in Country E; she later applied for a protection visa in October 2016.

  • The third child, D, was born in Australia on 30 April 2016.

  • The father returned to Country E in June 2016 after failing to find work in Australia, then returned to Australia unannounced on 7 February 2017.

  • The mother alleged the father sexually assaulted her during his 2015 visit and engaged in long-term abuse.

  • Following the father's return in 2017, the mother obtained a Domestic Violence Order in the ACT Magistrates Court on 21 February 2017.

  • The mother and children are currently on bridging visas pending the outcome of protection visa applications.

  • The father's visa status is precarious, with expert evidence suggesting his visa may be cancelled due to family violence undertakings.

  • The father sought the summary return of the children to Country E for parenting matters to be determined there (forum non conveniens).
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B (daughter), born 2004 (approx. 13 years old at trial)

  • C (son), born 2008 (approx. 9 years old at trial)

  • D (son), born 2016 (approx. 20 months old at trial)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations that the mother neglected the children by leaving B (aged 12) to care for C and D overnight while she was out.

  • Allegations that the father hit B with a mobile phone in June 2017 (not proven).

  • Risk of children not being returned to Australia if they visit Country E, as it is a non-Hague Convention country.

### Expert Evidence

  • Ms J (Family Report Writer): Noted B's resistance to the father and the benefits of the children's settlement in Australia. Initially concerned about potential fabrication of violence by the mother.

  • Mr Q (Migration Consultant): Testified that the mother's protection visa claim was 'quite good' but the father's visa was likely to be cancelled due to family violence undertakings.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined it was in the children's best interests to remain with the mother, their long-term primary carer, in Australia where they are settled.

### Orders

  • Mother to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with the mother.

  • If the father is in Australia: Time each Tuesday (4pm-6pm); B and C alternate weekends (Friday after school to Monday morning); D each Saturday (10am-6pm).

  • If the father is overseas: 14 days of time in Australia per year on 28 days' notice.

  • Electronic communication: Mondays, Thursdays, and Sundays at 5pm via audio-visual means.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted by findings of family violence (coercive control).

  • The mother's long-standing role as primary carer outweighed concerns regarding her credibility and the 'neglect' incident of leaving B in charge overnight.

  • The children's settlement in Australia and B's strong views were significant factors.

  • Geographical distance and visa uncertainty made joint decision-making unworkable.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found the mother's evidence regarding leaving B (12) in charge of siblings overnight was supported by school psychologist records.

  • The court found the father's involvement in the children's lives was historically secondary to the mother's.

  • The court found that Country E is a non-Hague Convention country, creating a risk regarding the children's return if they were to visit.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(a) benefit of meaningful relationship.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from harm.

  • s 61DA(2) rebuttal of presumption of equal shared parental responsibility due to family violence.

  • s 65DAC regarding the impracticality of joint decision-making.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Uncertainty regarding the mother's actual risk of harm in Country E from the neighbor or authorities.

  • Conflicting evidence regarding the 'trashing' of the matrimonial home in February 2017.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • The mother requested financial support, noting the father stopped payments in January 2017.

### Capacity to Pay

  • The father's capacity is unclear as he is currently away from his employment in Country E.

### Income & Expenses

  • Father previously paid $650 on 16 Jan 2017 and $1,100 on 31 Jan 2017.

### Orders

  • No specific maintenance orders made; focus was on parenting.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the cessation of support but did not accord it significant weight given the father's own displacement from work during the litigation.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of clear evidence regarding the father's current income or assets in Country E.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged sexual assault in 2015 upon arrival in Australia.

  • Alleged threats to kill the mother in December 2016 and in the court foyer in 2017.

  • Alleged coercive control regarding sexual acts and religious dress (hijab).

### Protection Orders

  • ACT Magistrates Court Domestic Violence Order granted 21 February 2017.

  • Mutual undertakings given without admissions in the current proceedings.

### Child Exposure

  • No direct evidence of children witnessing physical violence, but B was aware of the conflict and allegations.

### Orders

  • Parties restrained from speaking ill of each other in front of the children.

  • Father restrained from physically disciplining the children.

### Reasoning

  • The court accepted that the father engaged in psychologically coercive conduct to secure sex, which constitutes family violence under the Act.

  • The court found other allegations (group sex, internet filming) lacked particularity and were disclosed too late to be cogent.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's response in a recorded conversation appeared to accept that he used psychological pressure for sex in Country E.

  • The mother's admission in a recording ('Yes, I lie... to protect myself') undermined her general credibility but did not negate the finding of coercive control.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence (coercive and controlling behavior).

  • s 60CC(2)(b) primary consideration of protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses to the alleged physical assaults.

  • Conflicting accounts of the threats made in the court foyer.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case highlights the court's approach to parenting disputes involving international relocation, non-Hague Convention countries, and precarious migration status."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the children's stability and the mother's long-term caregiving role over the father's desire for a return to Country E.

  • The finding of 'coercive control' in a sexual context was sufficient to rebut the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility, even where physical violence was not proven.

  • The court refused the 'summary return' (forum non conveniens) because the children were well-settled in Australia and a full welfare hearing had already been conducted.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 61DA (parental responsibility) and s 4AB (family violence definition).

  • Application of the 'best interests' principle under s 60CC in a cross-border context where one parent faces likely deportation.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was significantly damaged by a recording where she admitted to lying to 'protect herself' and by the late introduction of extreme allegations (group sex).

  • The father's credibility was undermined by his initial support of the mother's 'untrue' protection visa claims and his subsequent denial of those same facts.

  • The court found both parties had involved the children in the litigation (e.g., father telling C to choose him to force the mother to return).

  • The mother's failure to disclose her employment (Dec 2016 - Feb 2017) in early affidavits was noted as a capacity/honesty concern.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The mother has sole parental responsibility for B, C, and D.

  • The children shall live with the mother.

  • Specific visitation and electronic communication schedules as detailed in the parenting section.

  • The Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) is discharged effective twelve months after the making of these orders.

  • Each party to keep the other informed of mobile telephone numbers and medical emergencies.

  • Parties are at liberty to alter arrangements by agreement in writing.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the mother's history as primary carer against her admitted 'ruse' and potential fabrications regarding her protection visa.

  • The court found that even if the mother's claims about the neighbor were uncertain, the father's coercive sexual behavior was a established fact that impacted the parenting dynamic.

  • The court determined that the children's best interests were served by remaining together with the mother in Australia, as D had never lived elsewhere and B was strongly opposed to returning to Country E.

  • The decision to grant sole parental responsibility was driven by the high level of conflict and the geographical separation that would make joint decision-making impossible.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced a relationship in 2006 and began living together in late 2007.

  • The child, B, was born in early 2008 and has a rare genetic disorder (10P duplication, 10Q deletion) causing developmental delays.

  • The parties separated in April 2012.

  • Final consent orders were made in February 2013 providing for the child to live with the mother and spend time with the father.

  • In May 2015, the mother relocated the child to live with her new partner, Mr. M, in Suburb K, approximately 235 kilometres from the father.

  • In April 2016, the mother placed the child in the father's care in Suburb F, where the child has remained and flourished.

  • The mother alleged the father sexually assaulted her in November 2015 during a medical emergency, an allegation the court found unsupported by evidence.

  • The mother misrepresented her relationship status with Mr. M to the court and experts, claiming they had separated when they had not.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, a girl aged 10 years at the time of judgment.

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleged father had guns in the home (unsubstantiated).

  • Father alleged Mr. M engaged in inappropriate 'tickling' with the child (unsubstantiated).

  • Mother's emotional dysregulation and deceptive behavior identified as a risk to the child's stability.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. E (Single Expert Psychiatrist) assessed the mother as having DSM Cluster B traits (dramatic, erratic, deceptive) and recommended the child live with the father.

  • Mr. X (Family Consultant) recommended the child live with the father and noted the mother's tendency to denigrate the father.

### Best Interests

  • Stability in the father's care was found to be paramount given the child's genetic disorder and developmental needs.

  • The risk of the mother undermining the father's relationship with the child was a significant factor.

### Orders

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility for the child.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Mother to spend time with the child two weekends per school term (3rd and 6th weekends) from 4:30 pm Friday to 4:30 pm Sunday.

  • Mother to have one week of time during each school holiday period.

  • Specific changeover locations at Suburb C and Suburb D Train Stations.

  • Mother permitted telephone communication each Tuesday and Thursday between 6:00 pm and 6:30 pm.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the child had flourished in the father's care since 2016 and a change in residence would pose an unacceptable risk of destabilization.

  • The mother's history of deception and lack of insight into her behavior made shared parental responsibility unworkable.

  • The distance of 235km (3+ hours travel) necessitated a structured weekend and holiday schedule rather than frequent short visits.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's allegation of sexual assault was found to be a fabrication or significant exaggeration.

  • The mother's claim of separating from Mr. M was a 'ruse' to influence the court and experts.

  • The child's rare genetic condition requires a high degree of stability which the father's environment provided.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations: balancing meaningful relationship with protection from psychological harm.

  • s 61DA: presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted due to the mother's conduct and lack of communication.

  • s 60CC(3)(a): the child's views were considered but weighted against her global developmental delay.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The mother failed to provide an affidavit from her partner, Mr. M, until the second day of trial.

  • Conflicting accounts of the father's alcohol and drug use, though CDT testing eventually supported the father.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged sexual assault in November 2015 (found not to have occurred).

  • Alleged threats by Mr. M to 'slit the father's throat'.

  • Interim AVOs were previously in place but did not proceed to final orders.

### Protection Orders

  • Interim Apprehended Violence Order (AVO) made on 22 October 2015 protecting the father from Mr. M.

### Child Exposure

  • The child was present during the mother's medical emergency in November 2015 but the court found no evidence of the alleged assault in her presence.

### Orders

  • Restraint from denigrating the other parent, their family, or partners in the child's presence.

  • Restraint from inflicting corporal punishment.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the mother's allegations of violence were largely tactical or lacked a factual basis, but maintained standard non-denigration orders to protect the child's emotional well-being.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court rejected the mother's claim that the father sexually assaulted her while she was suffering a miscarriage.

  • The court found the mother likely coached the child to tell the Family Consultant that 'Dad has guns'.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: definition of family violence considered in the context of the mother's allegations.

  • s 60CC(2)(b): the need to protect the child from psychological harm caused by exposure to conflict and false allegations.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses to the alleged altercations between the father and Mr. M.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the court's prioritization of stability and the 'status quo' when a child with special needs is flourishing, even if the initial change in residence was intended to be temporary."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's lack of credibility and history of deceptive litigation tactics (including false allegations of sexual assault and concealment of her relationship) were fatal to her application for residence.

  • The father's successful transition to primary carer, supported by a robust extended family network, outweighed the mother's historical role as primary carer.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC (best interests) and s 61DA (parental responsibility) where high conflict and deception make shared responsibility impossible.

  • The application of s 60CC(3)(m) regarding the 'any other relevant factor'—specifically the mother's personality traits as identified by the Single Expert.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother was found to be an unreliable witness who was 'evasive' and 'deceptive without remorse'.

  • The mother's partner, Mr. M, was found to be 'not particularly reliable' with inconsistent evidence regarding his drug use history.

  • The father was found to be 'composed and thoughtful', making appropriate concessions about his past marijuana use and the mother's early care of the child.

  • The mother's pattern of making serious, unsubstantiated allegations (sexual assault, firearms) significantly undermined her credibility across all issues.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Father granted sole parental responsibility.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Mother to spend time with the child on a graduated schedule (weekends and holidays).

  • Registrar to provide a copy of the orders and judgment to the NSW Department of Family and Community Services.

  • Mother granted liberty to provide orders to a Dialectical Behaviour Therapy (DBT) practitioner if she elects to undergo treatment.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the child's current stability in a supportive environment over returning her to a mother whose household was in flux and who demonstrated a pattern of deceptive behavior.

  • The court found that the mother's 'Cluster B' traits posed a risk of emotional dysregulation that could harm the child's development.

  • The father was viewed as the parent more likely to facilitate a relationship with the other parent, whereas the mother was found to be dismissive and denigrating of the father's role.

  • The geographical distance (235km) made a 50/50 or week-about arrangement impractical and not in the child's best interests.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties met in 1986 and cohabited from 26 August 1986 until final separation on 12 July 2012.

  • The parties were married in 1986 and divorced in 1990 but continued their relationship as a de facto couple.

  • The parties operated a construction and property development business through various entities, including QQ2 Pty Ltd (partnership) and Kellner Pty Ltd.

  • The matter was a rehearing following a successful appeal by the First Respondent against orders made on 8 April 2016.

  • The Second Respondent (the First Respondent's daughter) held a legal interest in one property (I Street) but did not participate in the rehearing.

  • The First Respondent resided at the former matrimonial home (U Street) with his new wife and child at the time of the hearing.

  • The Applicant resided at the F Street property since May 2014.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Property E (F Street, Suburb D) - $870,000

  • AGREED: Property B (C Street, Suburb D) - $770,000

  • AGREED: Property H (I Street, Suburb K) - $600,000

  • AGREED: Property T (U Street, Suburb V) - $1,000,000

  • AGREED: Partial property settlements received by Wife - $100,000

  • AGREED: Partial property settlements received by Husband - $100,000

  • AGREED: Proceeds from JJ Pty Ltd/II Pty Ltd (Wife) - $400,000

  • AGREED: Proceeds from JJ Pty Ltd/II Pty Ltd (Husband) - $400,000

### Contributions

  • The court found contributions during the 26-year relationship were equal.

  • The Husband was the primary builder/operator of the business; the Wife managed all administration and worked outside the home for 13 years.

  • The Wife was the primary homemaker and caregiver for the Husband's three children from a previous marriage.

  • Post-separation contributions were heavily weighted by the Husband's negative conduct, including failure to account for partnership income and failure to maintain loan repayments.

### Future Needs

  • Wife (63) is currently unemployed due to a shoulder and arm injury; her future earning capacity is limited.

  • Husband (72) is retired and receives the age pension.

  • A 10% adjustment was made in favor of the Wife under s 90SF(3)(r) due to the Husband's post-separation financial conduct.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court determined a 60/40 split in favor of the Wife was just and equitable given the Husband's significant non-disclosure and waste of assets post-separation.

### Orders

  • Wife to retain F Street and C Street properties.

  • Husband to retain U Street and I Street properties.

  • Husband to pay the Wife a lump sum of $361,234 to effect the 60/40 split.

  • Husband to pay the Wife $31,838 in outstanding costs from previous orders.

  • Wife to resign as director and transfer shares in Kellner Pty Ltd to the Husband.

  • Husband to indemnify the Wife against liabilities of Kellner Pty Ltd and QQ2 Pty Ltd (except the F Street mortgage).

  • In default of payment, U Street to be sold with a reserve of $950,000.

### Reasoning

  • The judge applied a 10% adjustment for the Wife under s 90SF(3)(r) because the Husband failed to account for partnership income, dealt in significant amounts of undisclosed cash, and allowed interest/penalties to accrue on joint debts despite having funds to pay them.

  • The court treated the Second Respondent's legal interest in I Street as being held on trust for the parties, as they had paid for the property and paid her $70,000 for her interest in 2009.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Husband's failure to provide full and frank disclosure regarding cash receipts (estimated at $260,000) and safety deposit box contents ($150,000) led the court to prefer the Wife's evidence.

  • The Husband's unilateral operation of the partnership post-separation resulted in significant 'waste' of the asset pool through accrued interest and charges.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 90SM(4) contributions assessment (found equal during relationship).

  • s 90SF(3)(r) 'any other fact or circumstance' used to adjust for financial misconduct and non-disclosure.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No partnership tax returns or financial statements were prepared after 2011.

  • No independent valuations of the businesses (QQ2 Pty Ltd or Kellner Pty Ltd) were provided.

  • Lack of admissible evidence regarding the exact quantum of Capital Gains Tax (CGT) liabilities.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the severe consequences of a party's failure to provide full and frank financial disclosure and the 'waste' of assets through intentional non-payment of joint liabilities."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court's willingness to make a significant percentage adjustment (10%) based on s 90SF(3)(r) conduct factors rather than just contributions.

  • The determination that a third party's legal title (Ms King) can be overridden by equitable findings in property settlement proceedings when the evidence shows the parties paid for the asset.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 90SM (alteration of property interests) and s 90SF(3)(r) (justice of the case).

  • Application of s 106A for the execution of instruments by the court in the face of a non-compliant party.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The First Respondent (Husband) was found to be a highly unreliable witness who used an interpreter to obfuscate and delay.

  • The Husband attempted to disown his own affidavit during cross-examination, claiming his wife had prepared it without his input.

  • The Husband's pattern of non-disclosure regarding cash income and bank accounts undermined his entire case.

  • The Applicant (Wife) was found to be generally reliable, and her evidence was largely unchallenged due to the Husband's failure to effectively cross-examine her.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The First Respondent pay the Applicant $361,234 within 28 days.

  • The First Respondent pay the Applicant $31,838 in outstanding costs within 28 days.

  • Registrar of the Family Court at Darwin appointed under s 106A to execute documents if the First Respondent fails to do so.

  • Injunction granted restraining the First Respondent from dealing with U Street until payment is made in full.

  • Each party to pay their own costs of the rehearing.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge found the Husband's evidence to be evasive, inconsistent, and disingenuous, particularly regarding cash dealings and his safety deposit box.

  • The court balanced the 26-year equal contribution history against the Husband's post-separation conduct which depleted the available pool.

  • The 60/40 split was necessary to compensate the Wife for the Husband's failure to account for partnership income and his role in increasing the parties' debt through neglect of mortgage interest.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced a relationship in 2008 and separated in October 2014.

  • The parties have two children: X (born 2009, age 8) and Y (born 2011, age 7).

  • The mother has a third child, Z (born 2017, age 19 months), with another partner, Mr. E.

  • The father suffered a workplace injury in September 2015 resulting in a partial permanent disability to his arm.

  • In March 2016, the father unilaterally removed the children from school and changed their enrollment without the mother's consent.

  • Interim orders in June 2016 placed the children in the father's primary care with weekend time for the mother.

  • The father applied to relocate with the children to C Town, New Zealand, to live within a Maori community supported by the paternal grandparents.

  • The children were assessed by paediatrician Dr. B in November 2016, identifying significant health and learning issues for X.

  • The mother was unrepresented at trial and had previously lost Legal Aid due to non-attendance at appointments.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2009, age 8)

  • Y (born 2011, age 7)

  • Z (half-brother, born 2017, age 19 months)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mutual allegations of historical family violence.

  • Concerns regarding the mother's ability to regulate anger, particularly toward X.

  • Concerns regarding the father's failure to address X's medical and educational needs.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Consultant Ms. F (Report dated 25 October 2017) recommended against relocation due to the impact on the relationship with the mother and half-sibling Z.

  • Paediatrician Dr. B (Report dated 14 November 2016) identified X's obesity, borderline hypertension, poor dentition, and significant learning difficulties.

  • Teacher Ms. G reported X's academic work was at kindergarten level and noted violent outbursts/self-harm ideation.

### Best Interests

  • Maintaining the relationship with the mother and half-sibling Z in Australia.

  • Stability in the current primary care arrangement with the father while ensuring substantial time with the mother.

  • Refusal of relocation to New Zealand as it would effectively sever the bond with the maternal family.

### Orders

  • All existing parenting orders discharged.

  • Equal shared parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with the father in Australia.

  • Children to spend time with the mother: alternate weeks from Thursday after school to Tuesday morning during term; half of all school holidays.

  • Father to obtain a paediatric review for both children; mother to participate.

  • Parties to ensure children hold valid Australian passports; overseas travel permitted during school holidays with 28 days' notice.

### Reasoning

  • Relocation would cause 'inevitable' change to the children's relationship with the mother and half-sibling Z.

  • The father failed to provide persuasive reasons for not implementing Dr. B's medical recommendations despite having primary care for two years.

  • While the mother had past failings (dental/school attendance), she made frank concessions and took responsibility.

  • An equal time (week-about) arrangement was rejected as X might struggle emotionally with the disruption to his primary attachment to the father.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that the father's failure to address X's health and learning needs (ADHD, obesity, dental) undermined his argument that relocation would improve the children's welfare.

  • The bond between the children and their 19-month-old half-brother Z was a 'constant' that would be lost if relocation was granted.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(3)(d) - The likely effect of separation from the mother and half-sibling.

  • s 60B(3) - Right of Aboriginal/Maori children to enjoy their culture (considered regarding the New Zealand proposal but outweighed by other factors).

  • s 65DAA - Consideration of 'substantial and significant time' as relocation made equal time impossible.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent evidence to corroborate mutual family violence allegations.

  • Lack of updated medical or psychometric assessments for X despite court orders from 2016.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mutual allegations of past physical and verbal violence.

### Child Exposure

  • X described the mother as 'angry'; Family Consultant noted mother's high level of anger during interviews.

### Orders

  • Parties restrained from exposing children to violence, denigration, or illicit drugs.

  • Changeovers to occur at school where possible to minimize parental contact.

### Reasoning

  • Allegations were deemed 'largely historical' and lacked independent corroboration.

  • The court focused on minimizing future conflict through structured changeovers rather than making specific findings on past violence.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's admitted anger issues were a factor in maintaining the father as the primary carer, but not sufficient to restrict her time to holidays only.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB - Definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) - The need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses or police reports to verify the severity of the alleged historical violence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case centers on the tension between a father's desire to relocate for cultural and family support versus the children's right to maintain a meaningful relationship with their mother and half-sibling."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's failure to act on critical medical advice for X (obesity/hypertension) while in primary care weakened his position as the 'superior' parent for relocation purposes.

  • The mother's honesty regarding her past failings (dental neglect and school absences) and her current emotional state ('broken and angry') made her a more credible witness regarding her commitment to improve.

  • The sibling bond with Z was a critical factor in preventing the geographical separation of the children from the mother's household.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(3)(d) (separation impact) and s 60B(3) (cultural rights). The court prioritized the immediate family unit and sibling bonds over the broader cultural benefits of the Maori community in New Zealand.

  • s 65DAA - The court found equal time not in the best interests due to X's emotional vulnerability and the mother's current responsibilities with an infant.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother was found to be 'frank' and 'honest' about her failings, which mitigated the risk of her past neglect being used to justify relocation.

  • The father's credibility was hampered by his unilateral removal of the children from school in 2016 and his subsequent failure to follow medical recommendations for X.

  • The mother's high level of anger was identified as a risk factor, but the court attributed this partly to the stress of the litigation and the threat of relocation.

  • The paternal grandparents, while supportive, had limited recent involvement with the children, reducing the weight of their evidence regarding the children's best interests in New Zealand.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Relocation to New Zealand refused.

  • Children to live with the father in Sydney.

  • Equal shared parental responsibility granted.

  • Mother to have substantial and significant time (alternate weekends Thursday to Tuesday).

  • Specific orders for paediatric reviews and passport maintenance.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the father's stable housing and family support in New Zealand against the 'inevitable' damage to the maternal and sibling relationships.

  • The court adopted the ICL's middle-ground proposal: maintaining the father as primary carer (due to his established stability over the last two years) but increasing the mother's time to a 'substantial and significant' level (Thursday to Tuesday) to foster the bond with the half-sibling Z.

  • The father's explanations for neglecting X's health (lack of funds despite paternal grandparents' wealth) were found unpersuasive, suggesting that relocation might not solve the children's underlying developmental issues.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties married in 1999 and separated on 30 October 2015.

  • The parties have three children aged 14, 12, and 10.

  • The primary asset is a jointly owned investment property at B Street, C Town.

  • The property is encumbered by a mortgage of approximately $161,400.

  • The mortgage repayments are $709 per fortnight, which exceeds the rental income.

  • The husband has been paying the mortgage shortfall and council rates.

  • The wife filed an interim application for the sale of the property and a $20,000 payment.

  • The husband's father, Mr. Hills Snr, offered to provide $20,000 to pay the wife to avoid a sale.

  • The wife abandoned her request for the $20,000 payment at the hearing, citing unproven Centrelink complications.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Investment property at B Street, C Town - Value not specified (Mortgage ~$161,400)

  • AGREED: Husband's Superannuation - ~$90,000

  • AGREED: Wife's Superannuation (3 funds) - ~$6,000

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly totaled in judgment

### Contributions

  • Husband has paid the mortgage shortfall (Rental income $15,704 vs Repayments $18,340 in FY2018).

  • Husband has paid council rates for the C Town property.

### Future Needs

  • Wife claimed potential jeopardy to Centrelink payments due to notional rental income, but provided no evidence.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it was not just or equitable to order an interim sale that would defeat the husband's final claim to retain the property.

### Orders

  • The wife's application for the sale of the B Street, C Town property is dismissed.

  • The husband is to provide copies of all credit card statements from September 2018 to the date of the order within 14 days.

### Reasoning

  • Ordering a sale at the interim stage would completely defeat the husband's final claim to acquire the wife's interest in the property.

  • The husband demonstrated a serious desire and capacity (via his father) to retain the property.

  • The wife failed to provide evidence regarding her alleged Centrelink complications.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's father (Mr. Hills Snr) was willing and able to provide funds to facilitate the husband's retention of the property.

  • The wife's late abandonment of the $20,000 lump sum request undermined the urgency of the sale.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the court's power to make property orders.

  • The court's discretion to refuse interim sales that prejudice final outcomes.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the B Street property was provided at the interim stage.

  • No evidence was adduced regarding the wife's specific situation with Centrelink.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2004, age 14)

  • Y (born 2006, age 12)

  • Z (born 2008, age 10)
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case reinforces the principle that the court will generally not order the interim sale of a significant asset if doing so would preclude a party from seeking to retain that asset at a final hearing, especially where financial capacity to pay out the other party is demonstrated.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's father's financial support was a critical factor in demonstrating the husband's capacity to eventually settle the matter without a sale.

  • The lack of evidentiary support for the wife's claim regarding Centrelink meant the court could not weigh that factor in favor of a sale.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The court's procedural powers to manage interim applications to preserve the subject matter of the dispute for final determination.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility regarding her financial need was weakened by her last-minute abandonment of the $20,000 payment request and the lack of evidence regarding her Centrelink status.

  • The husband's evidence regarding his financial contributions to the property (mortgage and rates) was uncontradicted.

  • The husband's father was viewed as a reliable source of financial backing for the husband's proposals.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Within 14 days, the husband will provide to the wife copies of all of his credit card statements for the period September 2018 to date.

  • The wife's Application in a Case filed on 16 July 2018 is dismissed.

  • The husband's Response to an Application in a Case filed on 30 August 2018 is dismissed.

  • The wife's Minute of proposed orders filed on 26 November 2018 is dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the preservation of the asset pool to allow for a range of final outcomes, including the husband's proposal to retain the property.

  • The court noted the small size of the overall asset pool (limited superannuation and one property), suggesting that a premature sale might not be the most efficient way to resolve the financial issues.

  • The disclosure orders were limited to credit card statements because the husband had already complied with other disclosure requests or the wife had independent access to the records (e.g., joint mortgage statements).

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final parenting orders were made by Justice Stevenson on 18 July 2018 following a three-day trial in May 2018.

  • The children involved are X (born 2011, age 7) and Y (born 2012, age 6).

  • On 22 November 2018, the mother filed an Application in a Case seeking leave to disclose the Final Orders to schools and health providers and to vary changeover supervision.

  • On 23 November 2018, the father filed a Response seeking to ban specific medical practitioners from seeing the children and to discharge existing orders.

  • The court identified that no extant proceedings existed at the time of filing, making the 'Application in a Case' procedurally incorrect.

  • The father consented to disclosure to education providers but opposed disclosure to health providers due to a dispute regarding specific practitioners.

  • The matter was heard in the Duty List on 26 November 2018.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2011, age 7)

  • Y (born 2012, age 6)

### Safety Concerns

  • The existence of Order 12 requiring professional supervision of changeovers by BB Group or an agreed person suggests historical safety or conflict concerns.

### Expert Evidence

  • Mr S (Psychologist who has seen X since November 2015)

  • Dr U (Paediatrician)

  • Dr T (Paediatrician)

  • V Paediatrics Clinic

### Best Interests

  • It is in the children's best interests for their treating health and education professionals to be aware of the legal allocation of parental responsibility.

### Orders

  • The parties are granted leave to provide a sealed copy of the Orders made on 18 July 2018 to any education providers, including before and after school care facilities.

  • The parties are granted leave to provide a sealed copy of the Orders made on 18 July 2018 to educational specialists including occupational therapists and speech pathologists.

  • The parties are granted leave to provide a sealed copy of the Orders made on 18 July 2018 to the children's medical/allied health care providers including therapists, counsellors, psychologists, and psychiatrists.

  • The mother's Amended Application in a Case (22 November 2018) and the father's Response (23 November 2018) are otherwise dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court held that health professionals (including GPs and dentists) and educators must be informed of the legal position regarding parental responsibility to effectively care for the children.

  • The court found that the father's specific objection to Mr S and V Paediatrics did not justify withholding the orders from all health practitioners.

  • The court determined that substantive variations to parenting orders (such as changeover supervision or discharging orders) could not be dealt with via an Application in a Case when no proceedings were active; an Initiating Application was required.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found it 'abundantly obvious' that third-party providers need to know the legal status of parental responsibility.

  • The court identified a procedural bar to hearing the substantive variation claims due to the lack of an Initiating Application.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 61DA (Parental responsibility framework)

  • Family Law Rules 2004 regarding the distinction between Initiating Applications and Applications in a Case.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court noted that each party should have the opportunity to test evidence before making further parenting orders, which was not possible in the current procedural posture.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Pivotal Findings

  • The court maintained the existing safety framework (supervised changeovers) by dismissing the mother's application to vary the supervision agency and the father's application to discharge the order without a full hearing.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) (Protection from harm)
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The judgment reinforces the procedural requirement that once final orders are made and proceedings are closed, any substantive variation requires a new Initiating Application rather than an Application in a Case. It also establishes the court's view on the necessity of disclosing parenting orders to third-party professionals to facilitate the practical exercise of parental responsibility."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the administrative necessity of third-party disclosure over the father's specific grievances with medical practitioners.

  • The court strictly enforced procedural rules to ensure that substantive changes to parenting arrangements are only made after a full evidentiary test.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 (Procedural regularity)

  • s 60CA (Best interests of the child as the paramount consideration in disclosure decisions)
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's position was noted as contradictory: he consented to disclosure for schools but opposed it for health providers based on a personal dispute with specific doctors, which the court found impractical.

  • Both parties faced litigation risk due to procedural errors (filing the wrong type of application), leading to the dismissal of their primary requests for variation.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Leave granted to provide sealed copies of the 18 July 2018 orders to education and health providers.

  • Purported Amended Application in a Case filed by the mother on 22 November 2018 dismissed.

  • Purported Response to an Application in a Case filed by the father on 23 November 2018 dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge reasoned that schools and doctors cannot comply with the law or respect parental rights if they are kept in the dark about court-ordered responsibility allocations.

  • The court balanced the father's right to challenge specific practitioners against the children's general need for coordinated care, concluding that disclosure to all health providers was necessary.

  • The overarching logic for dismissing the bulk of the applications was the 'functus officio' nature of the court after final orders, requiring a fresh start for substantive variations.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The child, Chloe, was born in 2002 and was 16 years old at the time of the hearing.

  • Chloe was born genetically male but identifies as female.

  • Chloe was assumed into the care of the Minister in March 2010.

  • The Minister has sole parental responsibility for Chloe pursuant to orders made under the Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998 (NSW).

  • In December 2015, the Court authorized Stage 1 therapy for Chloe.

  • The current application seeks a declaration regarding Stage 2 treatment (gender-affirming hormone treatment).

  • Treating practitioners agree that Chloe is Gillick competent and the treatment is therapeutic.

  • The Mother appeared by telephone and agreed with the proposed treatment.

  • The Father was not served as his whereabouts were unknown, and service was dispensed with.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • 16 years old (born 2002)

### Expert Evidence

  • Treating practitioners diagnosed Chloe with Gender Dysphoria.

  • Treating practitioners agreed Chloe is Gillick competent.

  • Treating practitioners agreed the proposed Stage 2 treatment is therapeutic.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that Stage 2 treatment is therapeutic and there is no controversy regarding its administration.

### Orders

  • Declaration that Family Court authorization is not required for Stage 2 treatment for Chloe, provided she is diagnosed with Gender Dysphoria, practitioners agree she is Gillick competent, the treatment is therapeutic, and there is no controversy.

  • Declaration that the Minister, as the person with parental responsibility, is not required to seek court authority for Phase 2 treatment.

  • The Minister is not required to seek court authorization to change the child's name.

### Reasoning

  • The court followed the reasoning in Re: Matthew [2018] FamCA 161, applying it to Stage 2 treatment.

  • The court found no logical reason to distinguish between the rights of biological parents and a Minister who has been legally allocated parental responsibility.

  • Under s 79(2) of the NSW Care and Protection Act, the Minister's responsibility includes medical and dental treatment.

  • Since parents do not require court authorization for Stage 2 treatment under Re: Kelvin (2017), the Minister (holding the same responsibilities) also does not require it.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Minister is a 'child welfare officer' under s 4 of the Family Law Act 1975.

  • The child is Gillick competent and the treatment is non-controversial.

  • The Minister's parental responsibility under State law confers rights equivalent to or greater than those under the Family Law Act.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 69ZK Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (Jurisdiction regarding children under child welfare laws).

  • s 79 Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998 (NSW) (Allocation of parental responsibility).

  • s 4 Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (Definition of child welfare officer).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Father's views were unknown as he could not be located, though service was dispensed with.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case clarifies that the principles established in Re: Kelvin and Re: Matthew regarding Stage 2 and Stage 3 gender dysphoria treatment apply equally to children in state care where the Minister has parental responsibility.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The legal status of a Minister with parental responsibility is functionally identical to that of a biological parent for the purposes of consenting to therapeutic medical treatment for gender dysphoria.

  • The Family Court's jurisdiction under s 69ZK is invoked when the Minister (as a child welfare officer) institutes the proceedings, but the court found that substantive authorization is unnecessary if the Kelvin criteria are met.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 69ZK of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) and s 79 of the Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998 (NSW).

  • Application of s 28(2) of the Births Deaths and Marriages Act 1995 (NSW) regarding the Minister's power to change a child's name.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • There were no significant credibility risks as the matter was largely non-adversarial.

  • The Mother supported the application, and the Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) provided submissions supporting the Minister's jurisdiction and authority.

  • The court was satisfied with the efforts to locate the Father and proceeded in his absence.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Application filed 3 October 2018 is otherwise dismissed.

  • Strict suppression orders regarding the identity of Chloe, her family, medical practitioners, and the school.

  • Anonymised Reasons for Judgment and Orders to be released to non-parties.

  • No person permitted to search the Court file without leave of a Judge.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge reasoned that because the Full Court in Re: Kelvin removed the requirement for court authorization for Stage 2 treatment for children with parental support, and because the Minister holds the same legal parental responsibility as a parent, the Minister should not be burdened with a requirement that does not apply to biological parents.

  • The court emphasized that the criteria for bypassing court authorization are: diagnosis of Gender Dysphoria, Gillick competence of the child, agreement that treatment is therapeutic, and an absence of controversy.

  • Regarding the name change, the court found that since the Minister has sole parental responsibility and the parents cannot exercise theirs, the Minister already has the statutory power under NSW law to apply for a name change without a Family Court order.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are engaged in parenting litigation concerning their daughter, X, born in 2007 (11 years old at the time of judgment).

  • The child has spent no time with the father for over four years following allegations of child sexual abuse.

  • In 2014, the father was charged with three counts of sexual intercourse with a child under the age of ten.

  • A provisional Apprehended Violence Order (AVO) was issued against the father in 2014, and he was charged with a breach involving the maternal grandmother, Ms B.

  • The father was acquitted of all criminal charges in the District Court of New South Wales in 2016.

  • The father successfully defended the AVO application in the Local Court, where the Magistrate awarded costs against the police and described the application as 'doomed to failure'.

  • On 23 October 2018, the father issued a subpoena to the Commissioner of Police for unredacted Cellebrite communications and JIRT interview records.

  • The Commissioner, the mother, and the maternal grandparents filed objections to the subpoena on grounds of privacy and lack of legitimate forensic purpose.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2007 (11 years old at the time of the 2018 proceedings)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of child sexual abuse were the primary basis for the father's exclusion.

  • The father was acquitted of these charges in 2016.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Consultant Ms C (Report dated 24 September 2018) suggested the initial child protection investigation might have been 'somewhat flawed'.

  • Ms C warned of the 'tragedy' of the child growing up with a false belief of being a victim if the abuse did not occur.

### Best Interests

  • The court considered the paramountcy of the child's best interests under s 60CA of the Family Law Act 1975.

  • The court weighed the potential psychological harm of a 'false narrative' against the privacy concerns of the mother's family.

### Reasoning

  • The judge found that the father's 'legitimate forensic purpose' was to establish whether the child had been coached or manipulated.

  • The court relied on the Family Consultant's opinion that the original investigation may have been flawed to justify the production of unredacted records.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's 2016 acquittal in the District Court of NSW.

  • The Family Consultant's report identifying the possibility of a flawed initial investigation.

  • The Magistrate's prior finding that the police AVO application was 'doomed to failure'.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CA Family Law Act 1975 (Best interests of the child)

  • s 121(9)(g) Family Law Act 1975 (Publication of judgment)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Commissioner of Police had previously produced only redacted documents.

  • Detective F had explicitly stated in correspondence that certain captured material would not be produced.

  • The father had been excluded from inspecting material previously produced to the Independent Children's Lawyer.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged child sexual abuse (2014).

  • Alleged breach of AVO against maternal grandmother Ms B (2014).

### Protection Orders

  • Provisional AVO issued in 2014.

  • Subsequent AVO application by police successfully defended by the father.

### Child Exposure

  • The child was the subject of JIRT interviews and criminal court cross-examination.

### Reasoning

  • The court acknowledged the father's acquittal and the Magistrate's findings regarding the lack of merit in the police's AVO application.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's successful defense of criminal and quasi-criminal (AVO) proceedings.

  • The Magistrate's award of costs against the police in the AVO matter.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB Family Law Act 1975 (Definition of family violence)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Unredacted call logs and SMS messages between the mother, Mr Paxton, Ms B, and Mr D between 1 March 2014 and 30 November 2014.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This judgment establishes that a party's right to test allegations of child abuse through unredacted police records constitutes a 'legitimate forensic purpose,' particularly where a criminal acquittal and expert reports suggest the original investigation may have been flawed."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The intersection of a criminal acquittal and the Family Court's duty to determine 'unacceptable risk' necessitates full disclosure of investigative materials.

  • Privacy considerations of third parties (such as the mother's husband and parents) are secondary to the court's ability to determine the truth of abuse allegations in the child's best interests.

  • The Family Consultant's identification of a potential 'false narrative' was a critical threshold finding that justified overriding the Commissioner's redactions.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CA (Paramountcy of child's best interests)

  • s 60CC (Determining best interests, specifically protection from harm vs. meaningful relationship)

  • Family Law Rules 2004 regarding subpoena compliance and objections.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court noted the Local Court Magistrate's assessment that the police informant's conduct was 'unusual' and 'inappropriate', casting doubt on the investigative process.

  • The father's credibility was bolstered by his acquittal and the successful defense of the AVO, while the mother's husband (Mr Paxton) and the maternal grandparents were identified as potentially having a 'hidden agenda'.

  • The Family Consultant's report raised significant concerns about the reliability of the initial child protection investigation, suggesting a risk that the child's statements were not adequately contextualized.

  • The Commissioner's failure to fully comply with previous subpoenas without formal objections was noted as a procedural concern.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The objections to the subpoena issued to the Commissioner of Police by the father, Mr Wilford, are dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge, Justice Stevenson, determined that the father's acquittal and the Magistrate's comments on the police's conduct provided a sufficient basis to question the reliability of the evidence used to exclude the father from the child's life.

  • The court accepted the father's argument that the unredacted Cellebrite reports (including SMS messages and call logs involving Ms Paxton, Mr Paxton, Ms B, and Mr D) were necessary to investigate whether the mother had a 'hidden agenda' to relocate and exclude him.

  • The judge prioritized the 'legitimate forensic purpose' of ensuring the court has all relevant evidence to assess the child's welfare over the privacy objections raised by the mother and the Commissioner.

  • The court found it 'unusual' and 'inappropriate' that police officers had previously elected not to produce captured material without formal objection, reinforcing the need for the current subpoena.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The child, X, was born in 2011 to the mother and father who separated in September 2014.

  • The mother has a long-standing history of alcohol addiction, including inpatient treatments in 2014 and 2018, and multiple drink-driving convictions.

  • On 5 February 2015, the mother presented to E Hospital with a blood alcohol level of 0.46.

  • In April 2018, the mother was arrested for assaulting her partner, Mr C (a quadriplegic), while recorded with a blood alcohol level of 0.26; she was subsequently convicted.

  • The mother made multiple allegations of sexual abuse against the child's half-brothers and the maternal grandfather, which were investigated by the Joint Investigation Review Team (JIRT) and found to be unsubstantiated.

  • Evidence from JIRT and the child's therapist, Ms B, indicated the mother was coaching the child to make disclosures of abuse and filming the child's genitals.

  • On 1 June 2018, police attended the mother's home where she was found manic and intoxicated (BAC 0.25), screaming that the child had been raped; the mother was scheduled under s 22 of the Mental Health Act.

  • On 21 November 2018, DFCS assumed care of the child and placed her with the father.

  • The maternal grandparents initially sought for the child to live with them but later supported the father's care after allegations were made against the maternal grandfather.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2011 (7 years old at the time of judgment)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother's severe alcohol abuse (BAC readings of 0.46, 0.25, and 0.26).

  • Psychological harm from the mother's 'relentless and persistent' coaching of the child regarding sexual abuse.

  • Mother's erratic and delusional behavior, including threats of self-harm and harm to the child.

  • Allegations of sexual abuse against half-brothers (unsubstantiated) and maternal grandfather (untested).

### Expert Evidence

  • JIRT reports found no substantiation of sexual harm but substantiated 'Risk of Psychological Harm' due to mother's coaching.

  • Ms B (Therapist) reported concerns that the mother was coaching the child to obtain medication.

  • Mr L (Family Therapist) noted the mother's 'all-consuming desire' to have the child's alleged experiences known was potentially harmful.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined the child's best interests were served by living with the father to ensure safety from the mother's dysregulated behavior and psychological pressure.

### Orders

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility for the child.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Father to ensure child is not brought into contact with any person alleged to have perpetrated abuse (specifically the half-brothers).

  • Mother to have supervised time for two hours every Saturday or Sunday, nominated by DFCS.

  • Maternal grandparents to have supervised time for two hours every alternate week.

  • Injunctions restraining parents from illicit drug use, denigration, and physical discipline.

  • Mother restrained from consuming alcohol within 24 hours of spending time with the child.

  • Mother to attend random urinalysis or CDT testing at the request of DFCS.

### Reasoning

  • The mother's alcohol abuse and mental instability posed a significant risk of neglect and psychological harm.

  • The mother's fixation on sexual abuse allegations and coaching of the child constituted a form of emotional abuse.

  • The maternal grandparents were not suitable for unsupervised care until allegations against the grandfather were tested and because they failed to report the mother's erratic behavior to DFCS.

  • The father was the only party not subject to credible allegations of causing harm and demonstrated a loving, appropriate relationship with the child during supervised visits.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's blood alcohol levels (0.46 and 0.25) and her conviction for assaulting a quadriplegic partner established a pattern of dangerous instability.

  • The JIRT finding that the mother was 'relentless' in questioning the child despite denials was critical to the finding of psychological risk.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (protection from harm vs. meaningful relationship).

  • s 60CC(3) additional considerations (parental capacity and family violence).

  • s 68B injunctions for the protection of the child.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No Single Expert psychiatrist report was available at the interim stage.

  • Allegations against the maternal grandfather remained untested.

  • Conflicting accounts of the 'cut-throat' gesture allegedly made by the paternal grandfather.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother's physical assault on partner Mr C in April 2018, resulting in facial lacerations.

  • Mother's erratic behavior on 1 June 2018, involving screaming and dragging the child in front of police.

  • Mother's verbal abuse of maternal grandparents and physical altercation on 9 November 2018.

### Protection Orders

  • An Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (ADVO) was issued for the protection of Mr C against the mother.

### Child Exposure

  • The child was present during the 1 June 2018 incident and was described by police as 'extremely distressed' and attempting to hide under her bed.

### Orders

  • Injunctions under s 68B restraining the mother from removing the child from school or the father's care.

  • Restraints on alcohol consumption and denigration.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the mother's behavior 'dysregulated' and 'unrestrained,' posing a high risk of emotional and psychological abuse to the child.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The police observation of the child's fear (hiding under the bed) during the mother's intoxicated episode was a key indicator of the need for removal from the mother's care.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence (including emotional and psychological abuse).

  • s 60CC(2)(b) the need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The mother denied the accuracy of police and hospital records, though the court gave them significant weight as objective third-party records.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the court's prioritization of a child's psychological safety in the face of 'Munchausen-by-proxy' type behaviors where a parent fixates on and coaches a child regarding unsubstantiated sexual abuse."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's severe alcohol addiction and mental health instability (BAC 0.46, 0.25) rendered her care of the child untenable.

  • The 'cumulative harm' of the mother's relentless coaching of the child regarding sexual abuse was a primary driver for the change in residence.

  • The maternal grandparents' failure to report the mother's erratic behavior undermined their suitability as interim carers.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The court balanced the s 60CC(2)(a) benefit of a meaningful relationship against the s 60CC(2)(b) need to protect the child from harm, giving overriding weight to protection from psychological harm.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was severely compromised by her extreme intoxication levels, her conviction for assault, and recordings showing her coaching the child.

  • The maternal grandparents' credibility was affected by their admission that they did not report the mother's erratic behavior to DFCS, suggesting a lack of protective capacity.

  • The court noted that police and hospital records are generally given greater weight at the interim stage than the subjective reports or denials of family members.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous orders discharged.

  • Father granted sole parental responsibility and the child to live with him.

  • Supervised contact for the mother (weekly) and maternal grandparents (fortnightly).

  • Injunctions regarding drugs, alcohol, denigration, and physical discipline.

  • Mother to undergo random drug and alcohol testing.

  • DFCS permitted to conduct random home visits and meet with the child.

  • Parties to participate in risk assessments and case plans developed by DFCS.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was that the child required immediate stability and protection from the mother's 'dysregulated' and 'obsessive' behavior.

  • The court preferred the evidence of objective third parties (Police, DFCS, Hospital, School) over the mother's denials.

  • The decision to place the child with the father was based on his history of appropriate care and the absence of substantiated risks in his household, contrasted with the high risk in the mother's and grandparents' households.

  • The court found that unsupervised contact with the mother or grandparents was inappropriate until the serious allegations of abuse and family violence could be properly tested at a final hearing.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were involved in an interim dispute regarding financial affairs and property preservation.

  • Initial interim orders were made on 12 November 2018.

  • Both parties jointly requested the court to amend the 12 November 2018 orders using the 'slip-rule'.

  • The Respondent holds interests in a complex structure of 13 entities including B Trust, C Trust, D Trust, E Trust, F Pty Ltd, G Pty Ltd, H Pty Ltd, J Pty Ltd, K Pty Ltd, L Pty Ltd, M Trust, N Pty Ltd, and O Pty Ltd.

  • The Applicant was in possession of $17,000 cash and a VW motor car belonging to one of the corporate entities.
