  • The Respondent has been residing in the R Street Property since May 2015.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: 1, 2 and 3 T Street, Suburb S, QLD - Value not stated

  • AGREED: 1-10 P Street, Suburb Q, QLD - Value not stated

  • AGREED: R Street Property, Suburb S, QLD - Value not stated

  • AGREED: Cash held by Applicant - $17,000

  • AGREED: VW motor car - Value not stated

  • DISPUTED: Interests in 13 Entities (B Trust, C Trust, D Trust, E Trust, F Pty Ltd, G Pty Ltd, H Pty Ltd, J Pty Ltd, K Pty Ltd, L Pty Ltd, M Trust, N Pty Ltd, O Pty Ltd) - Value to be determined by experts

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated; valuations pending

### Just & Equitable

  • The court determined it was necessary to preserve the asset pool via injunctions pending a final hearing.

### Orders

  • Respondent restrained from dealing with T Street and P Street properties other than in the ordinary course of business.

  • Respondent to provide Applicant with copies of purchase offers within 48 hours and signed contracts within 24 hours for the Properties.

  • Respondent to provide 7 days' notice of any transaction increasing indebtedness on existing loan facilities.

  • Respondent restrained from investing funds or establishing new businesses with funds subject to proceedings without consent or court order.

  • Applicant to withdraw registered caveats against real property owned by the companies and trusts by 2 November 2018.

  • Applicant to return $17,000 cash and the VW motor car to the relevant company within 14 days.

  • Parties to jointly instruct court experts for land and business/trust valuations and share costs equally.

### Reasoning

  • The judge accepted the parties' joint draft for amended orders despite not being fully convinced a 'mistake' occurred in the original 12 November 2018 orders.

  • The orders aim to maintain the status quo and ensure transparency regarding the Respondent's dealings with complex corporate and trust structures.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found it appropriate to discharge the 12 November 2018 orders in favor of the parties' agreed-upon amended framework.

  • The inclusion of specific notice periods (24 hours to 14 days) for financial transactions was deemed necessary for transparency.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 property preservation principles

  • Family Court Rules regarding 'court experts' for valuations

  • The 'slip-rule' (implied procedural mechanism for correcting orders)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No current valuations for the real property or the 13 business/trust entities.

  • The specific company owning the $17,000 and the VW car was not identified in the text.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • The Applicant’s application for interim spouse maintenance and interim legal cost funding be dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The dismissal was part of the broader interim financial dispute resolution, though specific reasons for the lack of 'need' or 'capacity' were not detailed in this specific judgment text.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court determined that interim maintenance and cost funding were not warranted at this stage of the proceedings.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 duty to maintain

  • s 117 regarding legal cost funding

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of detailed financial statements regarding the Applicant's immediate 'need' versus the Respondent's 'capacity' in the provided text.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case demonstrates the court's willingness to utilize the 'slip-rule' or consent-based amendments to refine complex interim financial injunctions, ensuring the preservation of a multi-entity asset pool while balancing the operational needs of the businesses."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the parties' consensus on the form of the orders over its own initial assessment of whether a clerical error had occurred.

  • The requirement for the Respondent to provide an updated Financial Statement including all 13 entities is a critical step for future property division.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (property) and the procedural 'slip-rule' (Rule 17.02 Family Law Rules 2004).

  • Use of Court Experts under the Family Court Rules to resolve valuation disputes early.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The judge expressed slight skepticism regarding the parties' claim of a 'mistake' in the previous orders, noting he was 'not convinced' a mistake was actually made, which suggests a cautious approach to the parties' representations.

  • The Respondent's failure to provide an updated Financial Statement necessitated a specific order (Order 18), indicating a risk of non-disclosure.

  • The requirement for the Applicant to return company assets ($17,000 and a car) suggests the court is strictly enforcing the separation of personal and corporate assets during the interim phase.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Order made on 12 November 2018 is discharged.

  • Interim spouse maintenance and legal cost funding applications dismissed.

  • Injunctions granted restraining the Respondent from dealing with specific real properties and trust appointorships without notice.

  • Applicant ordered to return $17,000 and a VW motor car to the corporate entities.

  • Respondent to cause current tenants to vacate R Street Property within seven days.

  • Parties to jointly appoint land and business valuers as court experts.

  • Costs of the application reserved to the Trial Judge.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The overarching logic was to facilitate a workable interim framework that protects the asset pool (via injunctions and notice requirements) while allowing the Respondent to continue 'ordinary course of business' activities.

  • The judge deferred to the parties' joint legal representatives regarding the specific wording of the orders to ensure they were practical for the complex trust and company structures involved.

  • The dismissal of maintenance and cost funding suggests a preliminary finding that the Applicant did not meet the threshold for interim relief in those areas at this time.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties met in approximately 1990 while the Applicant was providing professional services to the Respondent.

  • The Applicant moved into the Respondent's unencumbered property at Suburb B in late 1992 after losing his own home to a mortgagee.

  • The parties shared a bed and a sexual relationship commenced in late 1992.

  • The Respondent worked without remuneration as an office manager for the Applicant's business from 1992 to 1995.

  • The Applicant paid approximately $22,000 for improvements/extensions to the Suburb B property and covered some rates and school fees early in the relationship.

  • The Applicant was declared bankrupt on 6 February 1998.

  • The Applicant slept on a lounge for a period of approximately two years between 1999 and 2001 following relationship strain.

  • The Respondent provided $28,000 in 2005 to assist the Applicant in purchasing a property at L Town and a further $25,000 for a crop failure.

  • The Respondent moved into a separate bedroom in 2009 after her daughter, Ms. H, vacated the home.

  • The parties continued to take occasional trips together, including a trip to the Riverina in November 2012.

  • The Respondent engaged solicitors in December 2014 to demand the Applicant vacate the property; she moved out herself in February 2015.

  • The Respondent obtained summary judgment for possession of the Suburb B property in the Supreme Court of New South Wales on 1 December 2016.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Former residence at Suburb B - Unencumbered (Owned by Respondent)

  • DISPUTED: Property at L Town - Acquired by Applicant in 2005 (Respondent claimed $28,000 and $25,000 payments were loans)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not determined due to lack of jurisdiction

### Contributions

  • Respondent: Provided primary residence (Suburb B); worked unpaid in Applicant's business (1992-1995); provided $53,000 total for L Town property and crop failures.

  • Applicant: Paid $22,000 for house extensions; paid some rates and school fees early in the relationship; claimed to contribute all income to household, though this was found to be 'partially untrue'.

### Future Needs

  • Respondent: Aged pensioner since 2003.

  • Applicant: Working in an allied professional field; previously bankrupt (1998).

### Just & Equitable

  • The court did not reach a determination on what was just and equitable as the threshold for a de facto relationship post-1 March 2009 was not met.

### Orders

  • The initiating application filed by Mr. Brownless on 18 August 2015 is dismissed for lack of jurisdiction.

  • The order of Watts J made 27 March 2017 in relation to the Suburb B property is discharged.

  • The application in a case filed by Mr. Brownless on 12 July 2017 is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that while a de facto relationship existed in the early 1990s, it had eroded significantly by 2000.

  • The cessation of the sexual relationship (circa 1993), the period of the Applicant sleeping on the lounge (1999-2001), and the lack of financial interdependence in later years indicated the 'merger of lives' had ended.

  • The Applicant failed to discharge the onus of proving the relationship continued as a de facto relationship after the referral of powers on 1 March 2009.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Applicant's move to the lounge for two years marked a significant break in 'coupledom'.

  • The Respondent's move to a separate bedroom in 2009 was a definitive end to any remaining intimacy.

  • The financial contributions made by the Applicant were largely confined to the early years of the 22-year cohabitation.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AA: Definition of de facto relationship.

  • s 90RD: Declarations of de facto relationship.

  • Family Law Amendment (De Facto Financial Matters and Other Measures) Act 2008 (Cth): Transitional provisions regarding relationships ending before 1 March 2009.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Applicant failed to produce tax returns or bank statements (O Bank) to support his claim that his income was paid into household expenses.

  • The Applicant produced only peripheral evidence, such as a 2012 coffee shop receipt and an NRMA invoice.

  • Conflicting testimony regarding the nature of the $28,000 and $25,000 payments (loan vs contribution).
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Reasoning

  • Maintenance was not considered as the court found no jurisdiction under Part VIIIAB.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • The Respondent alleged the Applicant called her a 'useless fat woman', 'uneducated', and 'stupid' around 2000.

  • The Respondent claimed the Applicant made her life 'hell' if she did not comply with his financial demands.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the verbal abuse and the Respondent's fear of the Applicant's reaction as context for why she did not evict him sooner, rather than as evidence of a continuing de facto relationship.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Respondent's daughters (Ms. C and Ms. D) corroborated the verbal abuse and the Respondent's unhappiness.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: Definition of family violence (contextualized within the quality of the relationship).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No formal police reports or medical evidence of violence were provided, though verbal abuse was accepted by the court.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case clarifies the high evidentiary burden on an applicant to prove the continuation of a de facto relationship past the 1 March 2009 jurisdictional threshold, particularly in 'rooming house' style arrangements."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court distinguished between 'common residence' and 'living together on a genuine domestic basis'.

  • The cessation of sexual intimacy and financial interdependence, combined with the Respondent's repeated requests for the Applicant to leave, outweighed the fact of continued cohabitation and occasional social outings.

  • The Applicant's lack of financial transparency (failure to produce bank records/tax returns) was fatal to his claim of ongoing financial interdependence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 4AA (indicia of de facto relationship) and the transitional provisions of the 2008 Amendment Act regarding the 1 March 2009 cutoff for NSW de facto property matters.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Applicant was found to be highly unreliable regarding dates, admitting he reconstructed his timeline based on the Respondent's assertions and external records.

  • The Applicant's claim that he contributed his entire income to the household was found to be 'untrue' and 'partially untrue' under cross-examination.

  • The Respondent's account of the relationship's commencement was described as 'odd' and 'inconsistent with common sense', but her evidence regarding the later years was largely corroborated by her daughters.

  • The Applicant's excuse for missing evidence (alleging the Respondent's daughter stole documents) was rejected, given his sole occupation of the property for a significant period during litigation.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The initiating application filed by Mr. Brownless on 18 August 2015 is dismissed for lack of jurisdiction.

  • The order of Watts J made 27 March 2017 in relation to the Suburb B property is discharged.

  • The application in a case filed by Mr. Brownless on 12 July 2017 is dismissed.

  • Parties seeking costs must file a minute of order and financial statement within 14 days.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the 'coupledom' test from Jonah v White, focusing on whether the parties had 'merged their lives'.

  • The court found that the relationship had transitioned from a de facto relationship to one of mere cohabitation or a 'landlord/unwelcome guest' dynamic long before 2009.

  • The judge prioritized the evidence of the Respondent's daughters (Ms. C and Ms. D) over the parties' own testimony, finding them to be objective and reliable observers of the relationship's decline.

  • The court held that the onus of proof rests on the party asserting the relationship (the Applicant), and he failed to prove the relationship existed at the relevant statutory time.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property orders were made on 14 November 2017, resulting in a 65/35 split in favor of the wife regarding two primary assets.

  • The husband filed an application for costs on 8 December 2017, seeking $81,641.33.

  • The costs sought related to proceedings from 14 June 2017, an interim application from 20 July 2016, and a contravention application from 31 August 2016.

  • The wife demonstrated a pattern of non-compliance with court-ordered filing deadlines for the costs application.

  • The underlying property dispute involved a former matrimonial home in Suburb E and a hotel business operated through a corporate entity.

  • The wife had a significant disability which was acknowledged by the court but found not to be the primary cause of the trial's prolongation.

  • The wife made various unsubstantiated allegations during the trial, including forgery, theft, and non-disclosure by the husband.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Former matrimonial home at Suburb E - Value not specified but subject to sale.

  • DISPUTED: Hotel business (corporate entity) - Wife claimed $2,000,000 vs Husband's assessment (value not specified but proposed sale).

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly totaled, but centered on the net equity of the Suburb E property and the Hotel.

### Contributions

  • The court previously found the wife made a greater contribution than the husband, leading to an initial assessment before future needs adjustments.

### Future Needs

  • The court factored in a modest adjustment for the wife's future needs, resulting in a final 65% share of the net equity in the two primary assets.

### Just & Equitable

  • A split of 65% to the wife and 35% to the husband of the net equity in the two assets was determined to be just and equitable in the 14 November 2017 judgment.

### Orders

  • The wife pay the husband’s costs of the Supreme Court proceedings in an amount to be agreed, and failing agreement, as assessed by a registrar (reiterated from the 14 November 2017 order).

  • The wife contribute towards, and pay, 2/7 of the husband’s costs calculated for the period from 14 June 2017 until the last day of trial.

### Reasoning

  • The court rejected the husband's claim for costs related to the 2016 interim and contravention applications because those had been previously dismissed by consent with each party to bear their own costs.

  • The court found the wife's conduct as a litigant justified a departure from the s 117(1) principle that each party bears their own costs.

  • The wife's pursuit of a $2 million valuation for the hotel without any expert evidence was deemed irresponsible.

  • The court determined that 2 out of the 7 trial days were wasted due to the wife's unsubstantiated allegations of forgery, theft, and non-disclosure.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's allegations regarding the 'V Bank account' and forgery were found to be completely unsubstantiated and could have been clarified with basic inquiry.

  • The husband's offer in his June 2017 case outline (65% to the wife) was nearly identical to the final outcome, making the wife's continued litigation largely unsuccessful.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117(1) - General rule that each party bears their own costs.

  • s 117(2) and s 117(2A) - Factors justifying a costs order, specifically the conduct of the parties and the relative success of the parties.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife failed to obtain an independent valuation of the hotel, despite asserting a $2 million value.

  • The wife failed to provide evidence for allegations that her solicitor's instructions to discontinue previous proceedings were fraudulent.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • The wife made allegations of domestic violence during the trial.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the allegations of domestic violence were among the issues that were inadequately prepared or unjustifiably put forward, contributing to the prolongation of the trial.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court noted that the allegations of domestic violence had not been properly investigated by the wife's legal team before being pleaded.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117(2A)(g) - 'Such other matters as the court considers relevant' (litigation conduct regarding allegations).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of corroborating evidence for the domestic violence allegations which were used as a basis for the wife's litigation position.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case clarifies the application of s 117(2A) costs orders in instances where a party's disability is present but does not excuse unreasonable litigation conduct, particularly the pursuit of unsubstantiated allegations and unrealistic property valuations."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court distinguished between conduct arising from a disability and conduct as a 'litigant' who is properly advised.

  • The court rejected the 'indemnity costs' (solicitor/client) standard, affirming that such orders remain 'exceedingly rare' and were not justified here despite the wife's 'stubbornness'.

  • A proportional approach (2/7) was used to quantify costs based on the specific number of trial days wasted by the unreasonable conduct.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 117(1) (general rule) and s 117(2A) (discretionary factors for costs).

  • Application of Family Law Rules 2004, Chapter 19 regarding costs assessment and Schedule 3 for the family law scale.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility was undermined by her failure to substantiate serious allegations of forgery and theft, which the husband successfully refuted with banking evidence.

  • The wife's pattern of non-compliance with filing deadlines (e.g., filing responses three weeks late) was noted as 'troubling' by the court.

  • The husband's disclosure was found to be adequate, contrary to the wife's broad and unsubstantiated claims of non-disclosure.

  • The court noted that the wife's legal advisors must have flagged the potential for a prolonged trial given the nature of her evidence-in-chief, yet failed to investigate the claims properly.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The wife contribute towards, and pay, 2/7 of the husband’s costs calculated for the period from 14 June 2017 until the last day of trial by agreement as to the amount and failing agreement, as assessed.

  • The application in a case filed 8 December 2017 and the response thereto filed 1 November 2018 are otherwise dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the wife's right to litigate with the husband's right not to be burdened by costs associated with 'groundless contentions'.

  • The court found that while the wife was not 'ethically or morally delinquent', her position was 'unreasonable' and 'ambitious' given the known assets.

  • The court emphasized that costs are compensatory, not punitive, intended to cover expenses that could have been avoided if the litigation was conducted differently.

  • The 2/7 fraction was derived from the judge's assessment that 2 out of 7 trial days were specifically wasted on issues that were inadequately prepared or unjustifiably pursued.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced co-habitation in May 2005, married in 2014, and finally separated in June 2014.

  • There are two children of the marriage: X (born 2005, aged 13 at trial) and Y (born 2008, aged 10 at trial).

  • At the start of the relationship, the wife had net assets of approximately $500,000, including properties in Suburb C and Suburb E.

  • The husband brought significantly higher initial assets, held via the Mr Daly Discretionary Trust (DD Trust), valued at approximately $4,974,512 as of June 2005.

  • The parties claimed to have kept finances separate, but the court found extensive intermingling through various corporate entities and property investments.

  • The husband was diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease in October 2017, though medical evidence suggested he remained capable of parenting.

  • The mother sought to relocate with the children to Brisbane to care for her mother who has dementia.

  • The father sought sole parental responsibility and for the children to live with him in Region LL.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real properties and business interests held via M Group and other entities - $42,488,564 (Gross)

  • DISPUTED: Loan from Wife's mother for N Street property - $521,500 (Accepted by Court)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: $12,524,921

### Contributions

  • Initial contributions: Husband $4,974,512 vs Wife $500,000.

  • During relationship: Parties intermingled finances despite claims of independence; contributions to the family enterprise were found to be equal.

  • Post-separation: Both parties used retained funds to increase asset values; no distinction made between their post-separation efforts.

  • Overall contribution assessment: 80% to the husband and 20% to the wife, primarily due to the significant disparity in initial capital.

### Future Needs

  • Husband retains significantly more income-earning assets and business entities.

  • Husband's superannuation ($342,351) is substantially higher than the wife's ($83,618).

  • Wife has a reduced earning capacity compared to the husband's investment income.

  • Husband will have primary care of the children, but the wife requires a suitable home for their time with her.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court determined it was just and equitable to make an adjustment given the length of the relationship and the intermingling of assets.

### Orders

  • Husband to pay the wife the sum of $2,565,113 within one year.

  • Husband to pay monthly interest on the outstanding balance at the rate prescribed by Rule 17.03 of the Family Law Rules 2004.

  • Each party to retain property currently in their possession.

  • No superannuation splitting order made; disparity treated as a s 75(2) factor.

### Reasoning

  • The court rejected the parties' assertion of 'independent' finances, finding that they strategically intermingled funds for tax and investment benefits.

  • The 80/20 contribution split reflects the husband's massive initial capital injection.

  • A 10% adjustment under s 75(2) was granted to the wife to address the disparity in future earning capacity and superannuation.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's initial contribution of nearly $5M via the DD Trust was the dominant factor in the percentage split.

  • The finding of financial intermingling negated the 'separate finances' argument, leading to an equal assessment of mid-relationship contributions.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79(4) contributions (initial disparity)

  • s 75(2) future needs (income-earning asset disparity and superannuation)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Dispute over the exact source of funds for the wife's post-separation property purchases, though the court accepted the mother's loan evidence.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2005, age 13)

  • Y (born 2008, age 10)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother's history of 'meltdowns' and emotional dysregulation.

  • Mother's admitted physical discipline of children (hitting Y with a wooden spoon, throwing water at X).

  • Incident where Mother called for a knife to open a door Y had locked herself behind in fear.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr L (Child Psychiatrist) recommended children live with the father due to the mother's volatility and negative views of the father.

  • Ms EE (Therapist) noted the mother's lack of self-regulation and 'meltdown' hits.

  • Ms FF (Psychologist) reported Y's fear of the mother's outbursts.

### Best Interests

  • Stability in the father's care was found to be paramount.

  • Protection from the mother's emotional volatility outweighed the benefit of equal time.

### Orders

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with the father.

  • X to spend alternate weekends with the mother (Friday to Monday).

  • Y to spend five nights per fortnight with the mother (Thursday to Monday in week 1, Thursday to Friday in week 2).

  • Mother's relocation application dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court gave significant weight to X's views given his age (13.5 years).

  • The mother's inability to acknowledge the children's positive relationship with the father was viewed as a risk to their emotional development.

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted by findings of family violence (specifically the mother's conduct toward the children).

### Pivotal Findings

  • The 'knife incident' and 'wooden spoon incident' established a pattern of unsafe emotional dysregulation by the mother.

  • The Father's Day 2018 incident (calling police on the child) was the catalyst for X's total refusal to see the mother.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (protection from harm vs meaningful relationship)

  • s 60CC(3)(a) child's views (weight given to 13-year-old X)

  • s 61DA rebuttal of equal shared parental responsibility due to violence

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of the severity of the father's physical discipline (e.g., the 'horse grip' and the HH Town incident).
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • Spousal maintenance was not specifically ordered; financial disparity was addressed via property adjustment.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • November 2007: Alleged throat grabbing (Not proven).

  • December 2011: Physical altercation resulting in Mother's forehead injury (Father admitted restraining her; Mother admitted head-banging history).

  • June 2014: Mutual assault in front of children.

  • July 2014: Father admitted grabbing Mother's throat to protect children from her 'meltdown'.

  • May 2017: Mother smacked Y repeatedly and called for a knife to open a locked door.

### Child Exposure

  • Children witnessed multiple physical altercations between parents.

  • X described the parents' arguments as 'horrible' and 'scary'.

### Orders

  • Each parent restrained from using physical punishment.

  • Parents restrained from derogatory comments about the other in the children's presence.

  • Father to hold children's passports.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that while both parents had been involved in violence, the mother's ongoing emotional instability posed a more direct risk to the children's daily well-being.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's admission of hitting the children 'almost daily' in 2013-2014 was a critical finding.

  • The court accepted that the father's violence was often reactive to the mother's 'meltdowns'.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from harm

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of medical records for several alleged early incidents of violence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the court's prioritization of emotional stability and the child's stated views (for adolescents) over the traditional goal of shared care in high-conflict families with a history of violence."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's 'relentlessly negative' view of the father was a decisive factor in awarding him sole parental responsibility.

  • The significant initial financial contribution of the husband ($4.9M) heavily skewed the property division despite a long relationship and intermingled finances.

  • The court's refusal to force a 13-year-old (X) to spend time with a parent he was actively resisting.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (property) and s 60CC (parenting) where the mother's conduct and the father's superior financial position both led to significant adjustments from a 50/50 starting point.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was undermined by her 'relentlessly negative' perception of the father, which the court found did not match the children's reality.

  • The mother's admissions of 'almost daily' violence toward the children in the past made her denials of recent volatility less persuasive.

  • The father was found to be the more 'emotionally and physically stable parent' despite his Parkinson's diagnosis and admitted reactive violence.

  • The court noted the mother's tendency to blame the father entirely for her own lack of self-regulation.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Property: Husband to pay Wife $2,565,113 within 12 months plus interest.

  • Parenting: Father granted sole parental responsibility; children to live with Father.

  • Parenting: Specific fortnightly schedules for X and Y to spend time with Mother.

  • Relocation: Mother's application to relocate to Brisbane dismissed.

  • Passports: Children's passports to be held by the Father.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the husband's massive initial financial contributions against the wife's future needs, resulting in a 70/30 split.

  • In parenting, the court followed the expert recommendations of Dr L, concluding that the mother's emotional dysregulation and 'alienating' attitude toward the father made her unsuitable as a primary carer.

  • The court determined that forcing the children to relocate to Brisbane would destroy their stability and relationship with their father, which was found to be the more stable of the two parental bonds.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties lived together for 15 years and were married for 6 years.

  • The parties have two children: X (born 2012, age 6) and Y (born 2016, age 2).

  • Separation occurred on 18 November 2016 following allegations by the Mother that the Father sexually abused X.

  • The Mother alleged X made disclosures in mid-2015, June 2016, and November 2016 regarding the Father touching her 'privates'.

  • On 16 November 2016, X allegedly demonstrated digital penetration by the Father using her finger.

  • The Father denied all allegations, claiming the Mother manufactured them or was paranoid due to her work in child protection.

  • The Father broke into the former matrimonial home on 21 November 2016 after the Mother changed the locks.

  • Interim orders in August 2017 provided for supervised time between the Father and children.

  • Property matters were settled by consent during the trial.

  • The trial focused on whether the Father posed an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse to the children.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Former matrimonial home at O Street, Suburb P Victoria - Retained by Mother

  • AGREED: Investment property at G Street, Suburb H Victoria - Retained by Father

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated as parties settled by consent during trial.

### Contributions

  • The parties were in a 15-year relationship.

  • Mother was on maternity leave from February 2016; previously worked in child protection roles.

  • Father employed as a manager.

### Future Needs

  • Mother has sole care of two young children (ages 6 and 2).

  • Mother suffers from inflammatory bowel disease (diagnosed 1999) requiring ongoing medical treatment.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court accepted the consent orders reached by the parties during the trial as just and equitable.

### Orders

  • Mother to retain and have sole use and occupation of the former matrimonial home at O Street, Suburb P Victoria.

  • Father to retain and have sole use and occupation of the Suburb H property.

### Reasoning

  • The parties reached a settlement during the trial, which the court formalised via consent orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The settlement of property by consent removed the financial dispute from the court's determination, allowing focus on parenting risks.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the alteration of property interests.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2012, age 6)

  • Y (born 2016, age 2)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of digital penetration of X by the Father.

  • Allegations of family violence, including the Father choking the Mother in August 2015 while she was pregnant.

  • Father's impulsive behavior, including breaking into the home in November 2016.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr L (Forensic Psychologist): Rated Father's risk of sexual offending as 'Low-Moderate'; found Mother had 'Low parental risk'.

  • Ms M (Family Consultant): Reported X's conflicting statements; initially suggested Mother might be 'hyper-vigilant' but conceded findings of fact were for the court.

  • Ms K (Social Worker): Conducted therapeutic play therapy; found no direct evidence of abuse in play but noted X's stable relationship with both parents.

  • Dr T (Paediatrician): Confirmed X had recurring vaginitis/thrush but noted these are common and non-specific findings.

  • Associate Professor V: Provided general literature on child sexual abuse disclosures.

### Best Interests

  • Protection from harm (unacceptable risk of sexual abuse) outweighed the benefit of an unsupervised meaningful relationship with the Father.

### Orders

  • Mother to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with the Mother.

  • Father to spend supervised time: Thursdays 4:00 pm to 6:00 pm (Family Contact Service); alternate weekends Saturday 10:00 am to 6:00 pm and Sunday 10:00 am to 4:00 pm (supervised by Ms B or Ms C Fitzwater).

  • Father to spend time on Christmas Day (9:30 am to 12:00 noon) and Father's Day (10:00 am to 4:00 pm), supervised.

  • Supervisors must sign enforceable undertakings to the Court.

  • Airport Watch List order maintained until 23 November 2018.

### Reasoning

  • The court found an 'unacceptable risk' of sexual abuse based on X's disclosures to the Mother and maternal grandmother, which were found to be genuine and reasonably based.

  • The Mother's account of X's demonstration of digital penetration was found to be 'compelling'.

  • The Father's denials were undermined by his inconsistent evidence and minimization of past family violence.

  • The court preferred the evidence of Ms K over the Family Consultant regarding a disputed conversation about 'coaching'.

### Pivotal Findings

  • X's demonstration of digital penetration on 16 November 2016 was the critical evidentiary turning point.

  • The Father's admission of lying about the August 2015 violence incident damaged his overall credibility.

  • The court found the Mother's belief in the abuse was genuine and not driven by litigation strategy.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) Primary considerations (protection from harm vs meaningful relationship).

  • s 60CC(2A) Weighting protection from harm more heavily.

  • s 61DA Presumption of equal shared parental responsibility (rebutted due to unacceptable risk).

  • The 'unacceptable risk' test from M v M (1988).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No forensic medical evidence confirmed sexual trauma (Dr T noted findings were non-specific).

  • X did not disclose abuse to police or child protection workers, only to family members.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • Spousal maintenance was in dispute but final orders were settled by consent alongside property division.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • August 2015: Father choked Mother (8 weeks pregnant) and pushed her onto a couch.

  • 2008: Father punched a fence and did 'burnouts' in the street.

  • 2012: Father punched and broke X's cot.

  • February 2016: Father threw Mother's iPhone into a bush.

  • November 2016: Father broke into the former matrimonial home.

### Protection Orders

  • Interim intervention order granted 21 November 2016 at Suburb J Magistrates’ Court.

### Child Exposure

  • X was present during the August 2015 physical altercation.

  • X was present during the February 2016 phone-throwing incident.

### Orders

  • Father restrained from unsupervised contact.

  • Supervisors required to sign undertakings to cease contact if Father exposes children to harm.

### Reasoning

  • The Father's history of impulsive and aggressive behavior (breaking the cot, throwing the phone, breaking into the house) supported a finding of family violence and a lack of self-control.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Father's admission during trial that he had previously lied about the August 2015 incident (claiming no violence occurred) was a major credibility blow.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB Definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) Protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses for the 2007/2008 incidents; Mother did not seek medical treatment or take photos of bruising in 2015.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the court's application of the 'unacceptable risk' test in sexual abuse allegations where forensic evidence is absent but child disclosures are deemed credible."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the Mother's evidence of the child's spontaneous disclosures over the Family Consultant's concerns about coaching.

  • The Father's lack of insight into the children's emotional needs and his history of impulsive violence made unsupervised time untenable.

  • The conflict between the Family Consultant and the therapeutic social worker (Ms K) regarding 'coaching' admissions was resolved in favor of the social worker, significantly shifting the weight of the expert evidence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) and (b), where the 'unacceptable risk' of harm (b) necessitated the restriction of the meaningful relationship (a) to a supervised format.

  • Rebuttal of s 61DA presumption of equal shared parental responsibility due to findings of family violence and unacceptable risk.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother was found to be a generally credible witness despite her 'hyper-vigilance' regarding sexual boundaries.

  • The Father's credibility was severely compromised by his inconsistent affidavits, his admission of lying about family violence, and his evasive testimony regarding his new partner and pregnancy planning.

  • The Paternal Grandmother was found to be 'embedded' in the Father's case but still capable of acting as a responsible supervisor if properly instructed by the ICL.

  • A significant procedural concern was the Family Consultant's misreporting of a conversation with Ms K, which the court corrected by preferring Ms K's direct testimony.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous parenting orders discharged (except Airport Watch List).

  • Mother granted sole parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with Mother.

  • Father to spend supervised time: Thursdays (Contact Service), alternate weekends (Family Supervisors).

  • Specific holiday and special day supervised time (Christmas, Father's Day, Fitzwater Family Christmas).

  • Paternal grandmother and cousin to provide enforceable undertakings to the Court.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer to confirm supervisor obligations and then be discharged.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the framework from N & S (1996) to assess the risk, focusing on the nature, detail, and consistency of X's disclosures.

  • The court found the Mother's belief was 'reasonably based and genuinely held', rejecting the Father's theory of a 'sexual prism' or 'paranoia' as the sole driver of the allegations.

  • The Father's behavior in breaking into the home and his disingenuous claims about the Mother's pregnancy planning undermined his reliability.

  • The court determined that splitting the siblings (allowing unsupervised time for Y but not X) would be 'highly problematic' and not in their best interests, leading to supervised time for both.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Proceedings commenced by the husband in September 2015 seeking parenting and property orders.

  • The parties have one child, B, born in 2008.

  • Interim parenting orders were made on 23 November 2015 and 27 July 2016.

  • Interim property orders were made on 15 August 2016, including restraints on asset disposal and exclusive use of certain vehicles.

  • The wife filed an Application in a Case on 25 September 2018 seeking $75,000 for termite repairs and mortgage payment orders.

  • A consent order was reached on 22 October 2018 for mortgage payments of $1,656 per fortnight from an Investment Loan Account.

  • The final hearing was scheduled for 3-5 December 2018.

  • Valuations for plant, equipment, and real property were noted as outstanding in April 2018.

  • The wife sought rectification of a wall at D Street, Suburb E, and repairs to business premises including gyprock and stairways.

  • The husband sought to adjourn or dismiss the wife's interim application.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Mortgage payments for K Street, Suburb L - $1,656 per fortnight

  • DISPUTED: Termite damage and garage repairs - $75,000

  • DISPUTED: Rectification of wall and business premises at D Street, Suburb E - $100,000

  • AGREED: Vehicle fleet including 4WD, German motor vehicle, Motor home, Bobcat, Tipper truck, Forklift, and Vintage car

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the wife's application for $75,000 for repairs and other rectification works involved disputed factual matters that required the scrutiny of a final hearing.

  • The judge characterized the filing of the interim application as 'precipitate' and an 'inappropriate use of process' given the proximity of the trial.

  • Consolidation was deemed the most efficient way to resolve the remaining controversies while preserving the trial dates.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's application for $75,000 in repairs was deemed a matter for the final hearing due to the imminent trial date.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 property adjustment (contextual)

  • s 106A (execution of documents)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of plant, equipment, or updated real property valuations.

  • Disputed extent of termite damage and necessary rectification costs.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B (born 2008)

### Expert Evidence

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) granted access to subpoenaed documents.

### Reasoning

  • Parenting issues were consolidated with property issues for a holistic determination at the final hearing under Division 12A.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Trial directions issued 3 April 2018 required parenting and property to be heard together.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Division 12A (Principles for conducting child-related proceedings)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Outstanding affidavit from the wife's treating medical practitioner.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Wife sought husband to pay mortgage of $1,628/month for the K Street property.

### Capacity to Pay

  • Husband had access to an Investment Loan Account to fund mortgage payments.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted that the parties had already reached a consent agreement on 22 October 2018 for the husband to pay up to $1,656 per fortnight toward the mortgage, effectively addressing the wife's immediate need.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Consent orders for mortgage payments mitigated the immediate financial urgency.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 duty to maintain (contextual)
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the court's procedural priority to consolidate late-stage interim applications with imminent final hearings to avoid fragmented litigation and ensure all financial claims are assessed against the total asset pool."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's interim application was filed only two months before the final hearing.

  • Consent orders for mortgage payments mitigated the immediate financial urgency.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Division 12A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's decision to file a complex interim application shortly before trial was viewed unfavorably as a procedural delay or inefficiency.

  • The parties' failure to obtain single expert valuations for plant and equipment by the directed deadlines created a risk of further delay.

  • The husband's response was preferred as it aligned with the court's goal of reaching a final determination.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • That the wife’s Application in a Case filed 25 September 2018 and the husband’s Response thereto be consolidated with the issues for determination at final hearing commencing 3 December 2018.

  • That costs be reserved to final hearing.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The overarching judicial logic was to prevent the fragmentation of the property dispute by dealing with repair costs and mortgage obligations as part of the final asset division rather than in an interim vacuum.

  • The court prioritized the trial schedule, noting that the final hearing was starting the same day the judgment was delivered.

  • The judge emphasized that contested evidence regarding the state of the properties (termite damage and council compliance) was necessary for a just determination, which could only be achieved at trial.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parents separated finally in 2011.

  • The children, X (born 2003, aged 15) and Y (born 2004, aged 14), initially lived with the mother following interim orders in October 2014.

  • In May 2016, the Department of Child Safety intervened due to protection concerns while the children were in the mother's care.

  • The children were placed in the father's care on 9 September 2016 and moved to D Town, New South Wales.

  • On 11 December 2017, the Children’s Court of Queensland granted the Chief Executive custody for 12 months, expiring on 10 December 2018.

  • The mother has vigorously contested all child welfare orders, including lodging appeals and a pending application in the High Court.

  • The mother appeared by telephone at the interim hearing on 27 November 2018, claiming she was unaware of the date change despite court records showing notification on 17 September 2018.

  • The mother conceded during oral submissions that she was not seeking the children live with her on an interim basis to avoid further trauma.

  • The children have attended local schools in D Town since September 2016 and consistently expressed a wish to remain with the father.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X: 15 years old (born 2003)

  • Y: 14 years old (born 2004)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother's 'unpredictable' and 'ballistic' behavior reported by children.

  • Allegations by mother of significant family violence by the father (denied by father).

  • Departmental assessment that children were in need of protection while with the mother.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. E (Psychiatrist, March 2015): Conducted early assessment.

  • Mr. F (Family Report, June 2015): Noted children were then aligned with the mother and refused to see the father.

  • Dr. G (Psychologist, August 2016): Opined that mother's alienating behavior was intrusive and supported change of residence to father.

  • Ms. B (Family Report, July 2018): Reported children have suppressed memories of life with mother; seek peace and stability with father; recommended no contact with mother.

### Best Interests

  • Stability and routine provided by the father are paramount.

  • Protection from the 'toxic' parental conflict and the mother's uninvited intrusions.

  • Respecting the views of the children given their ages (14 and 15).

### Orders

  • Children to live with the father in D Town area.

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility for major long-term issues.

  • Children to spend no physical time with the mother.

  • Telephone communication permitted only if initiated by the children, with caller ID disabled.

  • Injunction under s 68B restraining mother from approaching children at home, school, or in public.

  • Proceedings transferred to the Sydney Registry of the Family Court.

### Reasoning

  • The court relied heavily on the recent report of Ms. B (July 2018) and the children's clear wishes under s 60CC(3)(a).

  • The parents' relationship is 'toxic,' making shared parental responsibility impossible.

  • The children have been 'gravely damaged emotionally' by entrenched conflict and need peace.

  • The court found that forcing contact against the children's wishes would cause further trauma.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The children's views shifted from total alignment with the mother in 2015 to a total refusal to see her in 2018.

  • Ms. B's finding that the mother was playing 'mind-games' with the children.

  • The mother's concession that she did not seek interim residence was a significant procedural pivot.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(3)(a): Weight given to the views of the 14 and 15-year-old children.

  • s 60CC(3)(l): Making orders least likely to lead to further proceedings.

  • s 68B: Necessity of an injunction for the welfare of the children due to mother's 'uninvited intrusions'.

  • s 69ZK: Coordination with the expiry of state child welfare orders.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The mother's claims of alienation by the father remain 'trial issues' to be tested via cross-examination.

  • The mother's current housing and employment status were not formally evidenced but were questioned by the children's observations.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother alleged significant family violence by father in 2014.

  • Children reported mother 'rocking up out-of-the-blue', yelling, and blocking school buses with her car.

### Protection Orders

  • A Temporary Apprehended Violence Order (AVO) exists against the mother naming the father and children as protected persons.

### Child Exposure

  • Children have been 'directly exposed' to parental conflict for 'virtually as long as they can remember'.

  • Exposure to mother's 'unpredictable' emotional outbursts.

### Orders

  • Injunction under s 68B restraining the mother from approaching the children.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the injunction necessary to protect the children from 'uninvited intrusions' that caused them stress and anxiety.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The children's reports of feeling 'on edge' and 'on the lookout' for the mother's car drove the need for protective orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 68B: Personal protection of the children and restraint from entering places of education/residence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of the severity of past violence vs. current behavioral issues.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case demonstrates the court's reliance on the views of mature children (ages 14-15) to override traditional presumptions of shared time when a relationship is deemed 'toxic' and the children's psychological well-being is at risk."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The transition from state-based child welfare (Children's Court) to federal family law jurisdiction necessitated immediate interim orders to prevent a legal vacuum.

  • The children's consistent and emphatic rejection of contact with the mother, documented by Ms. B, was the primary driver of the 'no contact' order.

  • The mother's history of litigation and non-compliance with procedural directions suggested a high risk of ongoing conflict.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC (best interests/views) and s 69ZK (child welfare orders).

  • Application of s 68B to reinforce state-based AVOs within the Family Law framework.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was undermined by her failure to comply with court directions regarding filing material and her claim of being unaware of the hearing date despite email evidence.

  • The court preferred the evidence of the children and the family report writer (Ms. B) over the mother's assertions of alienation.

  • The mother's focus on 'justice' and 'endless challenges' was viewed by the court as a factor achieving 'ongoing trauma' for the children.

  • The father was viewed as a stable and reliable caregiver, supported by the children's testimony and school performance.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Commencing 11 December 2018, children to live with father.

  • Father granted sole parental responsibility.

  • No physical time with mother; telephone contact only if initiated by children with caller ID disabled.

  • Section 68B injunction restraining mother from approaching children.

  • Proceedings transferred to the Sydney Registry of the Family Court of Australia.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the children's need for 'peace and respite' over the mother's desire for a relationship, given the children's ages and emotional damage.

  • The court determined that the mother's behavior (described as 'mind-games' and 'unpredictable') posed a risk to the children's stability in their new environment in NSW.

  • Sole parental responsibility was granted to the father because the parents' communication was 'toxic' and non-existent, preventing any collaborative decision-making.

  • The transfer to the Sydney Registry was ordered for the convenience of the parties now that the children are settled in NSW.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties have a daughter, X, born in 2008 (aged 10 at the time of the hearing).

  • The litigation history spans five years, including 20 court hearings and two previous sets of final parenting orders (July 2013 and August 2015).

  • The 2013 orders provided for equal shared parental responsibility and a 'week about' school arrangement despite parents living 160km apart.

  • The father failed to file any affidavit or formal response despite orders made on 7 June 2018 and 15 October 2018.

  • The father failed to cooperate with the Family Report process, attending a day late and refusing to answer questions, citing a 'cultural clash'.

  • On the morning of the final hearing (27 November 2018), the father attempted to file a 42-page affidavit, which the court rejected.

  • The mother alleged the father subjected the child to 'trust exercises' involving her wandering streets alone at night and walking home alone as punishment for failing to recite psalms.

  • A witness, Mr D, found the child distressed and lost on his doorstep on 1 April 2017 after the father made her ride a bike without lights at night.

  • The father's time with the child was suspended by Johns J on 15 October 2018 due to safety concerns.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2008 (10 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Child wandering streets alone at night for 'confidence building'.

  • Punitive discipline (making child walk alone in unfamiliar areas).

  • Lack of supervision at father's workplace.

  • Father's lack of insight into developmental needs and physical risks.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Consultant Ms C found the father's parenting style 'rigid' and 'punitive'.

  • Ms C opined that the father's actions posed a risk to the child's physical safety and emotional well-being.

  • Ms C noted the father demonstrated limited capacity to prioritize the child's needs over his own ideological goals.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that the child's need for protection from physical and psychological harm outweighed the benefit of an unconstrained relationship with the father.

### Orders

  • All existing parenting orders discharged.

  • Mother to have sole parental responsibility for all major long-term decisions.

  • Child to live with the mother.

  • Father to have supervised contact at a children’s contact centre.

  • Supervision to continue as long as the service provides or until parties agree otherwise.

  • Mother permitted to travel internationally with the child; Mother is the only person with parental responsibility for passport purposes.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted under s 61DA(4) due to the total breakdown in communication and the father's lack of insight.

  • The court applied s 60CC(2A), giving greater weight to the protection of the child from harm over the benefit of a meaningful relationship.

  • The father's 'trust exercises' were found to be irresponsible and a source of significant distress for the child.

  • The court found no prospect of cooperative parenting given the father's hostile attitude toward the mother and the court process.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's refusal to participate in the Family Report and his hostile presentation to Ms C.

  • The unchallenged evidence of Mr D regarding the child being lost and distressed at night while in the father's care.

  • The father's admission (via cross-examination) that he prioritized his 'cultural education' over the child's safety and interests (ballet).

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2A) - Priority of protection from harm.

  • s 61DA(4) - Rebuttal of the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

  • s 60CC(3) - Additional considerations regarding the child's maturity and the parents' capacity.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Father failed to file any admissible evidence (affidavit rejected due to extreme delay).

  • No evidence provided by paternal grandparents despite the father's suggestion they could supervise.

  • Father provided no medical evidence for his claimed anxiety/panic attacks as a reason for non-compliance.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Prior 'skirmishes' in the local magistrates' court resulting in undertakings (now expired).

  • Incident in May 2017 at the child's school where the child feared the mother was injured.

### Protection Orders

  • Previous undertakings in the magistrates' court.

### Child Exposure

  • Child was exposed to entrenched conflict and witnessed at least one incident requiring police attendance.

### Orders

  • Supervised contact at a children's contact centre.

### Reasoning

  • The father's parenting methods (trust exercises and punitive walking) constituted emotional abuse and physical risk.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's lack of insight into the child's fear and the dangers of her wandering alone.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB - Definition of family violence (including emotional abuse).

  • s 60CC(2)(b) - The need to protect the child from being subjected to or exposed to abuse or family violence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Neither party explicitly relied on family violence as a primary ground for the current application, though the history of conflict was noted.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case underscores the court's refusal to tolerate procedural non-compliance and the prioritization of child safety over parental rights to 'ideological' parenting styles."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's persistent failure to comply with court directions and cooperate with experts led to the case being heard effectively as an undefended matter under Rule 11.02(2)(c).

  • The court found that five years of 'tinkering' with orders and shared responsibility had failed because of the father's rigid and dominant personality.

  • The father's 'trust exercises' were legally characterized as posing an unacceptable risk of physical and psychological harm.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2) and s 60CC(2A) was decisive, with the court determining that the risk of harm from the father's parenting style necessitated the cessation of unsupervised time.

  • Rule 11.02(2)(c) of the Family Law Rules 2004 allowed the court to proceed without the father's evidence due to his 'recalcitrance'.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother was found to be a 'responsive, truthful' witness who showed genuine distress regarding the father's behavior.

  • The father's credibility was severely undermined by his failure to file evidence, his hostile presentation to the Family Consultant, and his 'blunt' and 'confident' demeanor in court which ignored the child's reported distress.

  • The father's attempt to file a 42-page affidavit at 5:00 AM on the day of the trial was viewed as a 'manipulation of the Court’s processes'.

  • The father's failure to call the paternal grandparents as witnesses, despite claiming they could supervise, led to an inference that they might not support his position.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All existing parenting orders discharged.

  • Mother granted sole parental responsibility.

  • Child to live with the mother.

  • Father to have supervised contact at a contact centre.

  • Mother permitted to apply for and hold the child's passport without father's consent.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer discharged.

  • All other extant proceedings dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge emphasized that the court's resources are a public asset and the father's 18-month delay and last-minute filing were an 'enormous waste'.

  • The court rejected the father's 'cultural clash' argument as a justification for non-cooperation with the Family Consultant.

  • The decision to grant sole parental responsibility was based on the 'non-existent' communication between parents, which made joint decision-making impossible and dangerous for the child's future (education/health).

  • The shift to supervised contact was justified by the father's total lack of insight into the child's anxiety and the physical dangers of his 'confidence building' exercises.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • On 11 September 2018, the Court ordered the return of two children, X and Y, to New Zealand under child abduction regulations.

  • On 21 September 2018, the return order was amended by consent to extend the time for the children to leave Australia.

  • On 21 November 2018, the Department filed a Form 2D application seeking to discharge the return orders.

  • The children's father provided written confirmation on 19 October 2018 supporting the children remaining in Australia with the mother.

  • The matter was heard as an application to discharge a return order under the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (male) born 2005 (aged 13 at time of judgment)

  • Y (male) born 2010 (aged 8 at time of judgment)

### Expert Evidence

  • Affidavit of Ms. D (Department of Child Safety, Youth and Women) filed 21 November 2018.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that discharging the return order was appropriate given the mutual agreement of the parents for the children to remain in Australia.

### Orders

  • The Order made on 11 September 2018 and varied on 21 September 2018 for the return of the children to New Zealand is discharged.

  • The Commissioner of the Australian Federal Police (AFP) to remove the Mother and children from the Family Law Watchlist.

  • Ms. D or her nominee to release all current passports for the Mother and children to the Mother.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the parents had reached an agreement for the children to remain in Australia, satisfying the requirements for discharging a return order.

  • The judge relied on Regulation 19A(2)(a) of the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986, which allows discharge when all parties consent.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's written confirmation on 19 October 2018 expressing support for the children to stay in Australia was the decisive factual element.

  • The Department (the Applicant) actively sought the discharge of the order they had previously obtained.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Regulation 19A of the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 (Cth) regarding the discharge of return orders.

  • Section 111CE of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the court's discretion in international child abduction matters.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court required the formal filing/emailing of the father's written communication mentioned in Ms. D's affidavit to finalize the record.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case demonstrates the procedural mechanism for reversing a Hague Convention return order when the initiating party and the left-behind parent subsequently consent to the children remaining in the requested state.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The transition from a contested abduction scenario to a consent-based residency arrangement.

  • The court's insistence on verifying the 'left-behind' parent's (the father's) consent through direct written evidence despite the Department's affidavit.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 and the general discretionary powers under s 111CE of the Family Law Act 1975.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • No significant credibility risks were identified as the parties were acting by consent.

  • The court maintained a procedural safeguard by directing the production of the father's actual written communication to ensure the Department's summary of his position was accurate.

  • The use of a pseudonym and s 121(9)(g) approval indicates the sensitive nature of international child abduction proceedings.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Discharge of return orders for children X and Y to New Zealand.

  • Removal of Mother and children from the Family Law Watchlist.

  • Release of passports to the Mother.

  • Direction for the Applicant to provide the Court with the father's written consent by 4:00 pm on 7 December 2018.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The overarching logic is that the primary purpose of the Hague Convention (prompt return of children) is superseded by the mutual agreement of the parents under Regulation 19A.

  • The judge balanced the mandatory nature of return orders with the reality of parental reconciliation or agreement regarding the children's habitual residence.

  • The court exercised its power to discharge the order only after being satisfied that the prerequisites of Regulation 19A(2)(a) were met, ensuring the father's consent was genuine and documented.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties married in 2008 and separated on 1 February 2014.

  • The child, B, was born in 2007 and is the only child of the marriage.

  • Post-separation, the child initially lived with the mother; however, following interim orders in October 2016, the child moved to the father's care.

  • The mother has a history of major depressive disorder diagnosed in August 2008 and has taken Lexapro and Zoloft at various intervals.

  • The mother made multiple allegations of sexual and physical abuse against the father to the police and the Department of Human Services, which were not substantiated.

  • On 13 October 2016, the mother failed to comply with an interim order to deliver the child to the father, instead taking the child to a police station for further interviews.

  • The mother video-recorded the child in December 2015 while the child was in significant distress to obtain 'disclosures' against the father.

  • The father is a teacher aged 40; the mother is aged 36 and works 24 hours per week for a local council.

  • The trial was delayed significantly due to the mother's health issues and a seven-month delay in filing her written submissions.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, born 2007 (aged 11 at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Unacceptable risk of psychological harm to the child if in the mother's unsupervised care due to her 'fixed and false' beliefs regarding the father.

  • Risk of the mother withholding the child or subjecting the child to unnecessary police/medical interventions.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. E (Psychiatrist): Concerned about the mother's 'fragility' and potential for 'altruistic filicide' if she lost the child; recommended robust psychiatric treatment for 1-2 years.

  • Dr. K (Forensic Psychiatrist): Disagreed with Dr. E; found no evidence of psychosis or risk of filicide, but acknowledged the mother was 'traumatised'.

  • Dr. F (Family Consultant): Supported the father's proposal; found the mother's retraction of abuse allegations was 'strategic' rather than genuine.

### Best Interests

  • Determined that the child's need for protection from psychological harm (alienation and unnecessary trauma) outweighed the benefit of unsupervised time with the mother.

### Orders

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Child to spend supervised time with the mother at a contact centre (e.g., G Group) for at least two hours fortnightly at the mother's expense.

  • Child to continue enrollment at H School, Suburb J.

  • Mother restrained by injunction from going within 1km of the child's residence or school.

  • Mother to deliver child's passports to the Court; Father permitted to obtain a passport without mother's consent.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted due to the mother's inability to communicate and her history of thwarting orders.

  • The court preferred the evidence of Dr. E and Dr. F over Dr. K, noting Dr. K's report lacked testing of the mother's treating professionals' data.

  • Indefinite supervision was necessary because the mother had not demonstrated a genuine shift in her belief that the father was a risk.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's act of video-recording the child in distress was a key indicator of her lack of insight and psychological risk.

  • The mother's non-compliance with the October 2016 handover order and subsequent police interview of the child was viewed as a major breach of trust and detrimental to the child.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (protection from harm vs meaningful relationship).

  • s 61DA rebuttal of the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

  • s 60CC(3) additional considerations regarding the mother's mental health and capacity.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of direct evidence or reports from the mother's treating psychiatrist (Dr. CC) and psychologist (Ms. BB) to corroborate her claims of wellness.

  • No MMPI-2 personality testing was conducted despite recommendations from the family consultant in 2016.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • Maintenance not sought or addressed.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • 12 May 2012: Alleged incident where mother grabbed a knife; father pushed her to the floor in response.

  • 1 February 2014: Mother attacked father's female acquaintance and later smashed father's guitar and glasses.

  • December 2015: Mother alleged father threatened to kill her family during a visit.

### Protection Orders

  • Interim Intervention Order against father (7 August 2015) - later withdrawn.

  • Safety Notice and Interim Intervention Order against mother (October 2016) following non-compliance with court orders.

### Child Exposure

  • Child was present during the 2014 separation conflict and was subjected to multiple police interviews (VARE) at the mother's instigation.

### Orders

  • Injunction under s 68B restraining the mother from approaching the child or removing him from the father's care.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the mother's repeated, unsubstantiated allegations and her use of the child to 'gather evidence' constituted a form of psychological abuse.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's conduct in taking the child to the police station on the night of a court-ordered handover was a critical factor in determining risk.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence (including emotional and psychological abuse).

  • s 60CC(2)(b) priority given to protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of the 2012 knife incident; however, the court made no formal finding as it was historical.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case underscores the court's willingness to order indefinite supervised contact and sole parental responsibility in instances where a parent's mental health and 'fixed' negative beliefs about the other parent pose an unacceptable risk of psychological harm and alienation."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's 'strategic' withdrawal of abuse allegations at trial did not mitigate the court's concern regarding her underlying beliefs.

  • The maternal grandmother's influence was identified as a reinforcing factor in the mother's harmful behaviors.

  • The child's physical and emotional improvement while in the father's care (e.g., ceasing to soil himself, becoming 'relaxed') validated the change of residence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2) (meaningful relationship vs protection from harm) where protection from psychological harm was given paramount weight.

  • Rebuttal of s 61DA presumption based on the mother's history of litigation-driven behavior and non-compliance.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother was found to be an unreliable witness with a 'fraught' demeanour and inconsistent memory regarding her disclosures to medical professionals.

  • The maternal grandmother was viewed as non-responsive and biased, volunteering negative opinions of the father that undermined her credibility.

  • The father was found to be generally credible and was given credit for making concessions about his own past denigrating language toward the mother.

  • A significant risk was identified in the mother's 'chronic level of anxiety' which led to repeated, unnecessary reports to child protection authorities.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous parenting orders discharged.

  • Father granted sole parental responsibility.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Mother to have supervised time fortnightly for 2 hours at a contact centre.

  • Mother restrained from going within 1km of the child's residence or school.

  • Mother to deliver child's passports to the Court by 13 December 2018.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer discharged.

  • All other parenting applications dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge found the mother's evidence to be 'confusing', 'evasive', and 'non-responsive', particularly regarding her own history of trauma and her allegations against the father.

  • The court relied heavily on the Family Consultant's assessment that the mother's retraction of allegations was a legal strategy rather than a genuine change in insight.

  • The court balanced the child's view (wanting to live with both) against the expert evidence of psychological risk, concluding that the child's stability and protection from conflict were paramount.

  • The decision to change the child's school was based on the child's own reports of exhaustion and the practical necessity of living near the father's workplace.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The child, B, was born in the Ukraine in 2009 to a Ukrainian mother and an Australian/Country N/Country O citizen father.

  • The parents' relationship ended in late 2015 or early 2016.

  • The father removed the child from the Ukraine in the first week of September 2016 without the mother's consent.

  • The father and child arrived in Australia on 2 November 2016 after traveling through Europe, Country N, and Country O.

  • The mother applied to the Ukraine Central Authority for the child's return on 20 September 2017.

  • The New South Wales Central Authority (FACS) filed the application for the child's return in the Family Court of Australia on 8 December 2017.

  • The application was filed more than 12 months after the child's removal from the Ukraine (approximately 15 months later).
