  • The child has resided in a unit in Suburb Q, Sydney, owned by the paternal grandmother, since arrival.

  • The child has a younger brother, F (born 2012), who remains in the Ukraine with the mother.

  • The father claimed to have Irlen Syndrome/dyslexia, affecting his written expression, though he holds undergraduate and Masters degrees.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, born 2009 (8 years old at the time of the hearing).

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleged the father assaulted the child in January 2016 and frequently assaulted her.

  • Father alleged the mother severely assaulted one of the boys in January 2016 and has poor mental health.

  • Father alleged the mother might hide the child in Belorussia or Country J if returned.

  • Father alleged Ukrainian authorities threatened to place the child in an orphanage/psychosocial rehabilitation center.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Consultant Ms. U prepared a report regarding the child's views.

  • Ms. U found the child was aligned with the father and lacked the developmental maturity to anticipate the consequences of his objection.

  • Ms. U recommended that minimal weight be placed on the child's expressed wish due to his age and lack of maturity.

### Best Interests

  • Under the Abduction Regulations, the 'best interests' of the child is not the paramount consideration; the focus is on the 'settled' exception and wrongful removal.

### Orders

  • The Application of the Secretary of the New South Wales Department of Family and Community Services filed 8 December 2017 is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The Court found the child was 'settled' in Australia within the meaning of Regulation 16(2).

  • The child has excelled academically (NAPLAN results), is receiving necessary medical treatment (T Children’s Eye Hospital for 'lazy eye'), and has a stable social and family network.

  • The Court adopted the view that once a child is found to be settled and the 12-month period has expired, the Court has no further discretion to order a return under the Regulations.

  • The judge followed the reasoning of Kay J in 'State Central Authority v Ayob' and 'State Central Authority & CR', concluding that the Court's powers under the Regulations are exhausted upon a finding of settlement.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The child is highly integrated into the Australian community through school (R School), health care (T Dental Hospital, T Children’s Eye Hospital), and extended family.

  • The application was filed approximately 15 months after the child's removal, triggering the 'settled' exception analysis.

  • The father's evidence regarding the child's daily life and stability was preferred over the mother's distant observations.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Regulation 16(2) of the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 (the 'settled' exception).

  • Regulation 16(3)(c) regarding the child's objection (found not to be a valid defense due to lack of maturity).

  • Regulation 16(3)(b) regarding 'grave risk' (found not to be established by the father).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of family violence with no independent corroboration or police reports from the Ukraine.

  • Lack of clarity regarding the mother's alleged Country J citizenship.

  • Inconsistent evidence regarding the father's Irlen Syndrome and his high-level academic achievements.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged assault by the father on the child in January 2016.

  • Alleged frequent physical assaults by the father on the mother.

  • Alleged assault by the mother on one of the children in January 2016.

  • Father's attempted abduction of the younger child, F, from a kindergarten in City H on 17 January 2017.

### Protection Orders

  • No formal protection orders were in evidence from the Ukraine or Australia.

### Child Exposure

  • The children were allegedly present during multiple incidents of parental conflict and physical altercations in the Ukraine.

### Reasoning

  • The Court noted that both parents alleged the Ukrainian authorities were ineffective in intervening to protect the children.

  • The judge found that despite the allegations, the father was able to move freely in the Ukraine in early 2017, suggesting a lack of immediate legal threat to him there.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Court found that the 'grave risk' defense under Reg 16(3)(b) was not established because the father's allegations were uncorroborated and he had demonstrated an ability to return to the Ukraine safely.

  • The mother's failure to successfully engage Ukrainian law enforcement undermined the weight of her safety allegations in the context of the Hague proceedings.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Regulation 16(3)(b) 'grave risk' of physical or psychological harm.

  • Section 4AB of the Family Law Act 1975 (definition of family violence) as a contextual reference.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Total lack of independent evidence (medical records, police statements, or witness accounts) for the alleged physical assaults in the Ukraine.

  • No evidence explaining the nature of the 'psychosocial rehabilitation center' mentioned in the Ukrainian welfare letter.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case reinforces the strict application of the 'settled' exception in Hague Convention matters where an application is filed more than 12 months after removal. It clarifies the judicial preference in the Family Court of Australia for the view that once settlement is established, the Court's discretion to order a return is extinguished."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The 12-month delay between removal (September 2016) and the filing of the application (December 2017) was the primary procedural hurdle for the Central Authority.

  • The child's exceptional academic and social adjustment in Australia (NAPLAN, language skills, extended family) provided a high threshold of evidence for 'settlement'.

  • The Court rejected the 'grave risk' and 'child's objection' defenses, meaning the case turned entirely on the timing of the application and the degree of the child's integration.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between Regulation 16(1) (mandatory return within 12 months) and Regulation 16(2) (discretionary/settled exception after 12 months).

  • The interpretation of Regulation 15(1) as a potential but ultimately rejected source of residual power to order return after a finding of settlement.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • Both parents were found to have significant credibility issues; their versions of events were 'diametrically opposed'.

  • The father's credibility was damaged by his misleading initial account of the attempted abduction of the younger child, F, in January 2017.

  • The mother's credibility was questioned due to her estrangement from her parents (who supported the father) and her nonsensical explanation for a letter regarding adult children in the US.

  • The father's claim of a reading disability (Irlen Syndrome) was difficult to reconcile with his high-level academic degrees, though the Court accepted his oral presentation was 'disorganized'.

  • The Court noted that it could not simply prefer one party's evidence over the other and had to make findings on an issue-by-issue basis.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Application of the Secretary of the New South Wales Department of Family and Community Services filed 8 December 2017 is dismissed.

  • Publication of the judgment under the pseudonym 'Department of Family and Community Services & Magoulas' is approved.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge determined that the child was 'settled' based on his stable residence, school performance, medical care, and social ties, which outweighed the mother's claims of wrongful removal.

  • The Court addressed a significant conflict in Australian authority regarding whether a residual discretion to return exists after a finding of settlement, ultimately siding with the line of authority that denies such discretion.

  • The judge found that the mother's consent to the child obtaining Australian citizenship in 2015 did not constitute consent to the child's permanent removal from the Ukraine in 2016.

  • The 'grave risk' defense was rejected because the father's fears of the mother hiding the child or the child being placed in an orphanage were not supported by compelling evidence.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties participated in a long marriage and have one child, X, born in 2014.

  • Separation occurred under one roof in 2016 (Mother claims November; Father claims April).

  • The parties lived together until January 2018, sharing care of the child.

  • On 8 January 2018, an incident occurred where the Mother alleged the Father hit the child; police attended.

  • On 10 January 2018, a provisional Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (ADVO) was served on the Father, and both parties vacated the matrimonial home at Suburb C.

  • The Mother and child returned to the Suburb C property in February 2018.

  • The Father had no contact with the child from 10 January to 28 February 2018.

  • From 28 February to 20 May 2018, the Father had supervised time via D Group.

  • On 8 March 2018, the police withdrew the ADVO complaint.

  • On 10 May 2018, the Mother agreed to unsupervised daytime contact.

  • Both parents are high-earning senior executives; the Mother travels internationally for work.

  • The Mother has a history of psychiatric treatment (2010-2013) which the Father alleges involves personality and bipolar disorders.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Mother's available funds (NAB) - $116,000

  • AGREED: Father's available funds - $63,000

  • DISPUTED: Former matrimonial home at Suburb C - Value not stated

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated for interim hearing

### Contributions

  • Both parties are senior executives in corporations.

  • Father claims active parenting role since birth, matching Mother's care during separation under one roof.

  • Mother claims to be the primary caregiver.

### Future Needs

  • Both parties have high earning capacities.

  • Adjustment for expert fees to be addressed in final property proceedings.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it just to have parties contribute equally to expert fees from their respective disclosed cash holdings ($116k and $63k).

### Orders

  • Each party to pay one half of the fees for the single expert, Dr G.

  • Interim property orders sought by parties are listed for future determination before a judge.

### Reasoning

  • The judge determined that since both parties had significant individual funds ($116,000 and $63,000), they should contribute equally to the expert report cost rather than using joint funds, with final adjustments to be made at the property trial.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The disclosure of significant liquid assets ($116k for Mother, $63k for Father) allowed the court to order immediate payment of expert fees without depleting joint assets.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 property proceedings (referred for future listing).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Full asset pool values and specific contribution weights were not determined at this interim stage.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2014 (approx. 4 years old at time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegation of Father hitting child on 8 January 2018 (denied by Father).

  • Mother's mental health history (intensive psychiatric treatment 2010-2013).

  • Father's alleged depression and suicidal ideation (denied by Father).

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr G (Child and Family Psychiatrist) appointed as single expert to report on risk, mental health, and parenting capacity.

### Best Interests

  • Reinstating overnight time with the Father is in the child's best interests to maintain a meaningful relationship and allow the expert to observe the child's reaction to overnight stays.

### Orders

  • Child to live with Mother.

  • Graduated time with Father: Phase 1 (4 weeks) - Wednesdays 4pm to Thursday morning and Sundays 10am-6pm.

  • Phase 2 (from 18 July 2018) - Wednesdays overnight and alternate weekends (Saturday 10am to Sunday 6pm).

  • Phase 3 (from 15 August 2018) - Wednesdays overnight and alternate weekends (Friday 5pm to Sunday 6pm).

  • Father to have 'first right of refusal' if Mother is unable to care for child overnight.

  • Child's name placed on the Family Law Watchlist for 24 months.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the child had previously spent significant overnight time with the Father during 2017 and that the Mother's agreement to unsupervised time indicated she did not truly perceive a physical safety risk.

  • The graduated approach balances the child's need for stability with the reinstatement of the prior status quo of shared care.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Mother's agreement to unsupervised time was a pivotal finding that undermined her claims of physical risk to the child.

  • The history of the child sleeping in the Father's room during 2017 supported the immediate reinstatement of overnight time.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (meaningful relationship vs protection from harm).

  • s 60CC(3) additional considerations (nature of relationship, capacity of parents).

  • s 65D power to make parenting orders.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of independent expert evidence on the parties' respective psychiatric conditions (pending Dr G's report).

  • Conflicting accounts of the 8 January 2018 incident.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • Maintenance not addressed in this interim judgment.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged hitting of child by Father on 8 January 2018.

  • Allegations of Father's controlling behavior and yelling.

### Protection Orders

  • Provisional ADVO served 10 January 2018; withdrawn by police 8 March 2018.

### Child Exposure

  • Child allegedly present during yelling and the 8 January incident.

### Orders

  • Parties restrained from exposing child to violence, threats, or intimidation.

  • Parties restrained from denigrating each other in the child's presence.

  • Parties restrained from being under the influence of alcohol or illicit drugs while caring for the child.

### Reasoning

  • The court implemented 'without admission' restraints to manage the high level of enmity and protect the child from the impact of parental conflict.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The withdrawal of the ADVO and the Mother's subsequent consent to unsupervised time suggested that the risk of physical harm was not at a threshold requiring supervised contact.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No conclusive evidence regarding the 8 January incident as the Mother did not witness it and the Father denied it.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case illustrates the court's willingness to reinstate a status quo of shared care (including overnights) at an interim stage when allegations of risk are undermined by a party's own conduct (e.g., consenting to unsupervised time)."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Mother's agreement to unsupervised time was the critical turning point that allowed the court to move past the 8 January 2018 violence allegations for interim purposes.

  • The high-income status of both parties influenced the procedural order for expert fee payments from personal rather than joint funds.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) (meaningful relationship) and s 60CC(2)(b) (protection from harm) was resolved in favor of the relationship, given the lack of sustained evidence of ongoing risk.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court noted a 'high level of enmity' between the parties that was 'palpable' during the hearing.

  • The Mother's credibility regarding the necessity of supervision was weakened by her own agreement to unsupervised time shortly before the hearing.

  • Both parties face litigation risk regarding their mental health allegations, which will be tested by the single expert report.

  • The Father's denial of violence is yet to be tested, but the withdrawal of the ADVO reduced his immediate evidentiary burden.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Child X to live with Mother and spend graduated time with Father, including overnights.

  • Dr G appointed as single expert psychiatrist.

  • Child's name placed on Family Law Watchlist for 24 months.

  • Father to provide 28 days' notice if he intends to reoccupy the Suburb C property.

  • Matter listed for chamber mention on 30 November 2018.

  • Parties to share expert costs equally from personal funds ($116k and $63k).
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to return the child to a care arrangement similar to the one that existed throughout 2017, which had been disrupted by the January 2018 incident and subsequent ADVO.

  • The court balanced the Mother's concerns about the child's age and the 'gap' in overnight care against the Father's history of significant involvement and the child's prior comfort sleeping in his care.

  • The appointment of Dr G was deemed essential to resolve the competing allegations of psychiatric instability made by both parents.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties married in 1989 and separated in late 2012 or early 2013.

  • The parties were divorced in December 2015.

  • There are three adult children of the marriage.

  • The wife resides in the former matrimonial home at B Street, Suburb C, which is mortgage-free.

  • The husband resides in a property at Suburb G, acquired post-separation, with his de facto partner.

  • The husband's employment with K Ltd was terminated on 24 November 2017.

  • The husband previously earned $564,779.51 between March 2017 and November 2017.

  • The wife is currently unemployed and claims her savings have reduced from $286,692 to approximately $35,000.

  • The husband has been paying mortgages for properties in Suburb C, Suburb G, and Suburb Q.

  • The husband is supporting his de facto partner, Ms M, including her legal fees for parenting proceedings in Country V.

  • The parties have a significant asset pool involving the D Trust.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Former Matrimonial Home at B Street, Suburb C - Value not specified (Wife seeks to retain)

  • AGREED: Property at Suburb G - Value not specified (Husband's residence)

  • AGREED: Property at Suburb Q - Value not specified

  • AGREED: D Trust - Liquid funds available for distribution

  • DISPUTED: Alleged dissipation of $1.4M by the wife - $1,400,000

  • DISPUTED: Alleged offshore accounts held by the wife - Value unknown

  • DISPUTED: Husband's post-separation income and share schemes - $564,779.51

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly totaled, but described as 'significant' enough to cover interim distributions of $400,000+.

### Contributions

  • The parties had a long marriage of approximately 23-24 years.

  • The husband contributed significant post-separation income ($564,779.51 over 8 months) to the D Trust and mortgage payments.

  • The wife asserts a claim for 70% of the matrimonial pool based on her contributions and future needs.

  • The husband claims the wife dissipated $1.4 million during the proceedings without explanation.

### Future Needs

  • The wife is unemployed and has exhausted the majority of her personal savings.

  • The husband's employment was recently terminated, impacting his ability to meet ongoing mortgage payments of $2,808 per week for Suburb G.

  • The youngest child, Mr L, requires ongoing support of $250 per week until eligible for Centrelink.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it just and equitable to provide interim distributions to ensure both parties can meet living expenses and legal/tax obligations pending final trial.

  • The court determined that an even-handed distribution of $200,000 each was appropriate to avoid prejudice to the wife's final claim.

### Orders

  • Husband to cause payment of $200,000 to each party from the D Trust as an interim property distribution.

  • Husband to pay $15,000 from D Trust for legal costs related to his separation deed with K Ltd.

  • Husband to pay accounting fees for 2017 and 2018 tax returns for both parties and children from the D Trust.

  • Husband to pay tax liabilities of the wife and children arising from unpaid present entitlements in the D Trust.

### Reasoning

  • The court held that $200,000 each was sufficient to meet immediate needs without pre-judging the final percentage split.

  • The judge noted that while the wife seeks 70%, such a high award after a long marriage is 'outside of the expected range' but remains a possibility for trial.

  • The husband's request for a disproportionate interim payment ($250k vs $200k) was rejected to maintain equity.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's termination of employment created an immediate need for capital to manage debts and living costs.

  • The wife's depletion of savings from $286,692 to $35,000 established a clear need for interim funds.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act (Property settlement powers)

  • s 80(1)(h) (Power to make interim orders)

  • Principles from Gollings & Scott [2007] regarding post-separation income.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the Suburb G or Suburb Q properties provided in the interim reasons.

  • Conflicting allegations regarding the wife's alleged $1.4M dissipation and offshore accounts.

  • Uncertainty regarding the husband's future earning capacity following his termination from K Ltd.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Three adult children; youngest is a university student (Mr L).

### Best Interests

  • The court noted the agreement for the husband to support the adult son, Mr L, until he qualifies for government benefits.

### Orders

  • The parties agree that their son Mr L is to be paid $250 per week from the D Trust until he becomes eligible for a Centrelink benefit.

### Reasoning

  • The order reflects the parties' agreement to support the youngest child's education and living expenses post-separation.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The son's status as a university student and the husband's prior commitment to support him.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 66L (Maintenance of children over 18 in specific circumstances, though here by agreement).
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • The wife demonstrated a need for $9,139 per month to cover living expenses as she is unemployed.

### Capacity to Pay

  • The D Trust holds sufficient capital to fund periodic payments despite the husband's loss of employment.

### Income & Expenses

  • Wife's requested monthly payment: $9,139.

  • Husband's requested monthly payment: $15,700 (based on claimed expenses of $5,860 per week).

### Orders

  • Husband to cause payment from D Trust to the wife in the sum of $9,139 per month.

  • Husband to cause payment from D Trust to himself in the sum of $15,700 per month.

### Reasoning

  • The court accepted the husband's financial statement showing higher expenses, noting that any exaggeration can be adjusted at the final hearing.

  • The asset pool is large enough that a higher interim payment to the husband does not risk the wife's ultimate entitlement.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's expenses were found to be significantly higher than the wife's, partly due to his new domestic arrangements and debts.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 (Duty to maintain)

  • s 75(2) (Factors for maintenance/adjustment)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife challenged the husband's expenses as 'exaggerated' and 'exponentially' grown, but the court could not determine this without a full hearing.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case illustrates the court's approach to interim financial relief in high-net-worth matters where there are mutual allegations of non-disclosure and dissipation."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized maintaining the parties' financial stability through the D Trust while preserving the bulk of the assets for final determination.

  • The decision to allow the husband a higher monthly stipend ($15,700 vs $9,139) highlights that 'need' is assessed individually rather than by strict parity, provided the pool can accommodate adjustments later.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 79 and s 80 allows the court to make substantial 'interim property' orders that function as both maintenance and early distribution of capital.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • Both parties face significant credibility risks regarding financial disclosure.

  • The husband is accused of failing to comply with disclosure orders and 'machining' his way around court orders.

  • The wife is accused of dissipating $1.4 million and hiding funds in offshore accounts.

  • The court noted it could not resolve these 'he-said-she-said' financial allegations at an interim stage, leaving them as primary battlegrounds for the final trial.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Interim property distribution of $200,000 to each party from the D Trust.

  • Monthly payments of $9,139 to the wife and $15,700 to the husband from the D Trust.

  • Payment of $250 per week to son Mr L from the D Trust.

  • Provision for payment of tax liabilities and accounting fees from the D Trust.

  • Husband's legal costs for separation deed (up to $15,000) to be paid from the D Trust.

  • Matter listed for further hearing on 9 March 2018 if mediation fails.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the wife's concern about the husband's 'exponential' spending against the husband's immediate need to service debts and living costs following his job loss.

  • The court adopted a 'wait and see' approach regarding the wife's 70% claim, providing enough funds to satisfy immediate needs without locking in a final percentage.

  • The use of the D Trust as a 'clearing house' for tax, accounting, and legal costs was seen as a pragmatic way to manage the litigation's financial burden on both parties.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties married in 2010 and separated on 27 August 2013.

  • The husband's assets significantly exceeded the wife's at the commencement of the relationship.

  • The wife was not employed during the marriage and remains unemployed at age 50.

  • The husband, aged 56, is a senior manager at E Pty Ltd and managing director of F Pty Ltd.

  • A 2010 financial agreement was set aside by consent in 2015.

  • The 'Mr Mullens Trust' was established in 2008 to manage inheritances for the husband's daughter, G (now 14), from her grandparents.

  • The trust received $325,515 in 2010 and $154,925 in 2012 for G's benefit.

  • The husband is the principal and his company F Pty Ltd is the sole trustee; the wife argues his control makes the trust a financial resource or property.

  • The wife has expended over $200,000 in legal costs; the husband has expended approximately $100,000.

  • The wife applied for the appointment of single experts to value the trust and its real property holdings.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property owned by the husband in Suburb I, A Town, and N Town - $8,700,000

  • DISPUTED: Mr Mullens Trust Net Assets (Wife claims as resource/property) - $587,000

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Approximately $9,287,000 (if trust included; otherwise $8.7M plus cash/other assets)

### Contributions

  • Husband brought significantly greater assets into the marriage.

  • Wife acted as homemaker and was not in paid employment during the marriage.

### Future Needs

  • Wife is 50, unemployed, and owns no real property.

  • Husband is 56, employed in senior management, and has significant existing property holdings.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted the wife's claim for 40% of the pool was described by her own counsel as 'ambitious'.

### Orders

  • The wife’s Application in a Case filed 2 November 2017 (seeking valuation of the Mr Mullens Trust) be dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the trust was established for the specific purpose of protecting the daughter's inheritance.

  • The husband has no legal or beneficial interest in the trust; the possibility of him varying the trust to benefit himself was deemed 'remote'.

  • Expert valuation was not 'necessary' under Rule 15.45 as balance sheets already disclosed the trust's assets and liabilities.

  • The court applied Shaw & Shaw (1989), noting that if a trust is a financial resource rather than property, a precise valuation may not be required to appreciate the extent of the resource.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The trust assets were derived from inheritances gifted specifically to the husband's daughter, not matrimonial efforts.

  • The husband's powers under the trust deed (Clauses 6.24 and 10) did not equate to a proprietary interest in the context of this interim application.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 15.42 (significant issue in dispute)

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 15.45 (necessity of expert evidence)

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 15.46 (instructions to single expert)

  • s 79(4)(e) and s 75(2) regarding financial resources.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife provided no evidence to support the 'conjecture' that the husband moved matrimonial assets into the trust (Suburb O and Suburb P properties).

  • The wife's financial information was outdated (last formal update in August 2015).
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • G (husband's daughter): 14

  • Wife's children: 18, 17, 16
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Wife claims parlous financial circumstances and lack of employment.

### Capacity to Pay

  • Husband is a person of 'some wealth' with high earning capacity.
summary_sections.prenup_postnup:
### Agreement Details

  • 2010

### Orders

  • Noted that the 2010 agreement was set aside by consent in 2015.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case reinforces the court's reluctance to order expensive expert valuations of third-party trusts where the connection to a party's proprietary interest is remote and sufficient financial disclosure (balance sheets) already exists."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court distinguished between 'control' of a trust and 'beneficial interest', particularly when the trust's corpus consists of inheritances for a child.

  • The wife's failure to provide evidence of asset dissipation into the trust made the valuation request unnecessary and disproportionate.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117(2) and 117(2A) of the Family Law Act regarding costs orders for unsuccessful interim applications.

  • Interplay between Rule 15.45 (necessity of experts) and the overarching purpose of minimizing litigation costs.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility was weakened by making 'mere conjectures' regarding the husband's movement of assets into the trust without evidentiary support.

  • The wife's legal spending ($200,000) was noted as high, suggesting a lack of proportionality in her litigation strategy.

  • The husband's evidence regarding the trust's purpose and his lack of interest was accepted, corroborated by the trust deed and the source of the funds (grandparents' inheritances).
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The wife’s Application in a Case filed 2 November 2017 be dismissed.

  • The wife pay the husband’s costs of and incidental to the Application fixed in the sum of $6,329.00.

  • Payment of costs to be made within 14 days of the finalisation of the property proceedings between the parties.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge dismissed the application because the trust was clearly for the daughter's benefit and the husband's potential to benefit was too remote to justify the cost of experts.

  • The court balanced the wife's 'parlous' financial state against her 'wholly unsuccessful' pursuit of an 'ambitious' and unsupported claim.

  • Costs were awarded to the husband because the wife had been on notice since December 2017 that the application had no prospects of success and the husband had already provided significant disclosure.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in 1995 and married in 2005.

  • The husband is aged 57 and the wife is aged 56.

  • The parties have three children: Mr K (19), and twins J and L (17).

  • Mr K has a medical condition and lives in supported accommodation at the Suburb F property.

  • The parties separated in October 2015.

  • Following separation, the parties sold a jointly owned property in Suburb M for $1,490,000 in May 2016.

  • In December 2015, the parties purchased a property in Suburb C for $1,260,000, which the wife currently occupies.

  • The husband previously resided in a granny flat at the Suburb F property but vacated it following concerns regarding superannuation compliance.

  • The wife filed an Application in a Case on 7 April 2017 seeking urgent spousal maintenance and injunctive relief.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Suburb C Property (Wife's residence) - $1,260,000

  • AGREED: Suburb M Property (Sold post-separation) - $1,490,000

  • AGREED: Suburb F Property (Owned by Fanshaw Superannuation Fund) - Value not specified but part of $4.3M gross pool

  • AGREED: Investment property in Suburb N - Value not specified

  • AGREED: Investment property in Suburb O (Owned by Fanshaw Pty Ltd) - Value not specified

  • AGREED: Two industrial units in Suburb P (One joint with partner Mr Q, one owned by Fanshaw Pty Ltd) - Value not specified

  • DISPUTED: Corporate entities (S Pty Ltd, T Pty Ltd, Fanshaw Pty Ltd, Fanshaw Family Trust) - Values contested

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Approximately $3,000,000 (Gross assets ~$4.3M less mortgages ~$1.3M)

### Contributions

  • The parties had a long-term relationship (20 years cohabitation/marriage).

  • The husband has been solely responsible for servicing all mortgages ($1.3M total debt) post-separation.

  • The husband paid $6,205 per month to service Suburb C and Suburb M mortgages during the bridging period.

  • The wife worked in administrative roles during the marriage and currently earns $517-$577 per week.

### Future Needs

  • The wife (56) claims a need for $1,563 per week to cover expenses.

  • The wife requires computer skill refreshment to increase earning capacity.

  • The husband (57) claims he cannot maintain long working hours as he previously did.

  • The wife has $35,158 in personal savings and $5,000 in a daughter's account.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it necessary to preserve the Suburb F property as a special needs residence for the parties' son.

  • The court determined that the wife should not be required to exhaust all capital, but her current savings precluded a finding of 'urgent' need.

### Orders

  • The husband to cause all necessary payments to be made to the NAB FlexiPlus line of credit (account #39) to service the Suburb C property mortgage.

  • Both parties restrained from extending the line of credit beyond the limit existing on 7 April 2017.

  • Both parties restrained from further encumbering or selling the Suburb C property.

  • The husband to cause D Pty Ltd (as trustee for the Super Fund) to enter an 18-month lease for the Suburb F property with G Ltd at no less than $800 per week.

  • Rental income from Suburb F to be applied to its mortgage and expenses.

  • Parties restrained from selling or alienating the Suburb F property without written consent.

  • Matter referred to a conciliation conference.

### Reasoning

  • The court sought to preserve the asset pool (specifically the Suburb F property) pending final trial.

  • The husband's agreement to continue paying the Suburb C mortgage rendered a specific maintenance order for that expense unnecessary.

  • The lease of Suburb F was deemed necessary to ensure the property's viability and compliance with superannuation rules.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Suburb F property was modified for special needs and its preservation was critical for the son's care.

  • The husband's threat to evict G Ltd created an objective risk to the asset's value and the son's stability.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 114(3) power to grant interlocutory injunctions where just and convenient.

  • s 79 preservation of property principles.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the husband's corporate entities (S Pty Ltd, T Pty Ltd).

  • Unclear terms regarding the husband's alleged $15,000 loan for rent.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Mr K (19)

  • J (17)

  • L (17)

### Best Interests

  • The court noted the importance of maintaining the Suburb F property as a residence for Mr K due to his medical condition.

### Reasoning

  • The court declined to include the expenses of J and L (17) in the wife's maintenance calculation because they earn $650 per week each and are not 'children' for the purposes of s 75(2) in the context of their independent income.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The adult/near-adult children (J and L) have substantial independent incomes ($650/week each), negating the wife's claim for their support via spousal maintenance.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 75(2) factors regarding the support of other persons.

  • s 66L (limited application to adult children).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting evidence on whether J and L contribute to the wife's household expenses.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Wife claimed $1,563 weekly expenses vs $577 income (shortfall of $986).

  • Court adjusted need by removing $450 mortgage (paid by husband) and $457.50 for adult children.

  • Adjusted shortfall: $78 per week.

### Capacity to Pay

  • Husband claimed income of $3,765 per week and expenses of $3,761 per week.

  • Wife alleged husband has greater capacity through control of S Pty Ltd and T Pty Ltd (total assets ~$5M).

### Income & Expenses

  • Wife income: $517-$577/week.

  • Husband income: $1,817 (S Pty Ltd) + $288 (T Pty Ltd) + $1,660 (Investment) = $3,765/week.

### Orders

  • The wife’s application for spouse maintenance is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The wife failed to establish she was unable to support herself adequately under s 72.

  • The wife's adjusted need of $78/week did not justify an urgent order given her $35,158 cash reserve.

  • The court refused to order maintenance to cover the expenses of adult children who earn $650/week each.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's inclusion of the mortgage in her expenses was 'incorrect and misleading' as the husband was already paying it.

  • The wife's $35,158 savings precluded a finding of urgent need.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 threshold (inability to support oneself).

  • s 77 (urgent maintenance).

  • s 75(2) factors.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife failed to prove the husband's corporate entities were his 'alter ego' to the extent of hiding significant surplus income at the interim stage.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Wife alleged the husband removed her access to the FlexiPlus line of credit on 9 March 2017.

  • Wife alleged the husband threatened: 'If you don’t agree and we go to court, I’ll ensure we both end up with nothing.'

### Orders

  • Husband restrained from causing any extension of the FlexiPlus line of credit beyond the current limit.

### Reasoning

  • The wife submitted that removing financial access constituted family violence under s 4AB.

  • The court addressed the financial control issue via property preservation orders rather than a specific violence finding.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's unilateral control over the line of credit necessitated an order to ensure mortgage payments were maintained for the wife's residence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence (economic abuse).
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case clarifies the high threshold for 'urgent' spousal maintenance when a party retains significant cash reserves and attempts to claim expenses for adult children with independent incomes."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court will not award spousal maintenance to subsidize the lifestyle of adult children who have the capacity to support themselves.

  • Interim property orders will focus on the preservation of unique assets (like special needs housing) and maintaining the status quo of mortgage repayments.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 72 (maintenance threshold) and s 114 (injunctive powers for property preservation).

  • Application of s 75(2) in the context of adult children's financial independence.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility was slightly undermined by claiming $450/week in mortgage expenses that she was not actually paying.

  • The husband's transparency regarding his corporate interests (S Pty Ltd and T Pty Ltd) was a point of contention, with the wife alleging they were his 'alter egos'.

  • The husband's claim of having zero surplus income ($3,765 income vs $3,761 expenses) was viewed with skepticism but not formally rejected at the interim stage.

  • The wife's transfer of $5,000 to her daughter's account for 'living expenses' was noted but not found to be a significant risk factor.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Wife's application for urgent spousal maintenance dismissed.

  • Husband to maintain mortgage payments on Suburb C property via NAB FlexiPlus account.

  • Injunctions granted to restrain sale or further encumbrance of Suburb C and Suburb F properties.

  • Mandatory injunction for the husband to execute an 18-month lease of Suburb F to G Ltd at $800/week.

  • Matter referred to a conciliation conference.

  • Liberty to apply if the matter does not settle within 21 days of the conference.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge dismissed the maintenance claim because the wife's 'need' was artificially inflated by including a mortgage she didn't pay and expenses for high-earning adult children.

  • The court applied the principle from Mitchell & Mitchell that while a party shouldn't have to exhaust all capital, a $35,000 reserve is sufficient to cover a $78/week shortfall until a final hearing.

  • The injunction regarding the Suburb F property was granted because the husband's previous threats to evict the care provider (G Ltd) posed a risk to the asset's stability and the son's welfare.

  • The court prioritized a 'clean break' approach by pushing the parties toward a conciliation conference rather than making complex interim financial adjustments.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were in a cohabitation relationship that lasted between 5.5 and 6.5 years.

  • Mr. Mercier possesses significant wealth, including approximately $170,000,000 in cash resources.

  • Ms. Calhoun had an annual income of $656,947 for the 2018 financial year, derived from an asset valued at up to $3,000,000.

  • Ms. Calhoun filed an Application in a Case on 12 June 2018, which was dismissed by the court on 22 August 2018.

  • Mr. Mercier applied for costs on 11 September 2018, seeking $44,608.79 for legal expenses incurred in opposing the dismissed application.

  • Neither party served an offer of compromise or settlement prior to the hearing on 30 July 2018.

  • Ms. Calhoun seeks a final property adjustment of $40,000,000, while Mr. Mercier seeks an order for no property adjustment ($0).
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Mr. Mercier's cash resources - $170,000,000

  • DISPUTED: Ms. Calhoun's income-producing asset - Up to $3,000,000

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not finalized, but exceeding $173,000,000 based on disclosed figures

### Contributions

  • Cohabitation period of 5.5 to 6.5 years.

  • Ms. Calhoun's income of $656,947 in FY 2018.

### Future Needs

  • Significant disparity in wealth between Mr. Mercier ($170M cash) and Ms. Calhoun ($3M asset).

  • Ms. Calhoun's claim for $40,000,000 vs Mr. Mercier's claim for $0.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted the 'high-stakes' nature of the $40 million claim versus a nil claim.

### Reasoning

  • The property division remains undecided; the current judgment focuses on the costs associated with an interim application.

  • The judge observed that the probability suggests neither party will be fully successful in their extreme positions ($40M vs $0).

### Pivotal Findings

  • The 'extraordinary' wealth of the respondent ($170M) and the high income of the applicant ($656k) create a unique financial context for property adjustment.

  • The short-to-medium length of cohabitation (5.5-6.5 years) is a critical factor in evaluating a $40 million claim.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 property adjustment principles

  • s 75(2) future needs factors in the context of extreme affluence

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The exact duration of cohabitation remains a disputed fact (5.5 vs 6.5 years).

  • The final valuation of Ms. Calhoun's $3,000,000 asset is not yet determined.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This judgment clarifies the court's discretion under s 117(2A) when dealing with ultra-high-net-worth individuals where one party is 'wholly unsuccessful' in an interim application but the other party possesses vastly superior financial resources."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that while being 'wholly unsuccessful' is a strong basis for costs, it can be neutralized by a 'vastly superior financial position' of the other party.

  • The failure to make offers of compromise (e.g., offering to pay for a single expert or mediator) reflects poorly on a party seeking a costs order.

  • The costs of interim applications in 'high-stakes' litigation ($40M dispute) are treated similarly to 'costs in the cause' and reserved for the final trial judge.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117(2A) Family Law Act 1975: The criteria for departing from the general rule that each party bears their own costs.

  • s 117(2A)(a) financial circumstances and s 117(2A)(e) success/failure in proceedings.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court noted an 'extraordinary' set of facts regarding the parties' wealth, which influenced the perception of their litigation conduct.

  • The lack of settlement offers suggested a tactical 'high-stakes' approach rather than a genuine attempt to minimize legal costs.

  • Mr. Mercier's failure to provide a copy of any offer of compromise (as required by the previous order) weighed against his application for costs.

  • The judge signaled that the extreme positions taken by both parties ($40M vs $0) were likely to be moderated by the court at final hearing, suggesting a risk of over-litigation by both sides.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The application for costs made by Mr Mercier on 11 September 2018 is dismissed.

  • Each party’s costs of the hearing giving rise to orders made 22 August 2018, together with any necessary legal work in preparation for same, are reserved to the final hearing.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the fact that Ms. Calhoun was 'wholly unsuccessful' against Mr. Mercier's 'overwhelmingly better' financial position ($170M cash).

  • The court criticized the conduct of both parties for failing to make any offers to settle, interpreting this as a sign that neither party was motivated by the cost of litigation due to their extreme affluence.

  • The judge noted that Mr. Mercier could have taken steps to avoid the hearing, such as offering to meet the costs of a single expert or mediator.

  • The court determined that because the parties are 'playing for high-stakes' ($40M vs $0), the interim costs should be reserved so the trial judge can view them in the context of the final property outcome.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property and parenting orders were made on 13 April 2017 on an undefended basis due to the Wife's non-participation.

  • The Husband sought the 2017 orders, asserting they were just and equitable despite the Court's concerns regarding evidentiary gaps.

  • The parties have a child, E, born in 2002, who is currently living with the Wife with no contact with the Husband.

  • In December 2017, the Wife sought to stay an auction of a trust property, claiming she intended to set aside the April 2017 orders.

  • The Wife alleges her father contributed over $200,000 to the purchase of a property now held within the Family Trust.

  • The Husband's son, Mr HH Narkis, is the current trustee of the Family Trust.

  • There is a dispute as to whether the Court's power under s 79 of the Family Law Act was exhausted by the April 2017 orders, specifically regarding an SMSF and the Family Trust.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property at M Street, Suburb N - Value not stated (subject to repairs and land tax)

  • AGREED: Real property at Q Street, Suburb R - Value not stated

  • AGREED: Real property at S Street, Suburb T - 45% of sale proceeds to be held in trust

  • DISPUTED: Ms JK & Mr Narkis Family Trust assets - Wife claims equitable interest based on $200,000 contribution from her father

  • DISPUTED: Self-Managed Superannuation Fund (SMSF) - Member accounts potentially not dealt with in 2017 orders

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated; involves multiple real properties and trust interests

### Contributions

  • Wife claims a financial contribution of over $200,000 from her father towards the purchase of the trust property.

  • Husband maintained in the 2017 undefended hearing that the proposed orders were just and equitable based on the then-available evidence.

### Future Needs

  • Wife has assumed responsibility for the child's private school fees.

  • Wife is responsible for 'holding costs' of $4,167 per month for the M Street property.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court previously expressed concern in April 2017 whether the orders were just and equitable due to truncated evidence.

  • The current proceedings seek to determine if the previous orders effectively exhausted the court's jurisdiction under s 79.

### Orders

  • Wife to pay land tax owing of $8,094 and an instalment of $5,772 for M Street.

  • Wife to pay building insurance of $2,438 and repair accounts of $2,500 for M Street.

  • Wife to provide names of 3 tradespersons to inspect M Street and Q Street for repairs.

  • Wife restrained from attending property inspections.

  • Wife to file an Amended Initiating Application and Statement of Claim regarding the Family Trust and s 79A claim by 4 May 2018.

  • Husband and Trustee to file Defences and submissions by June 2018.

### Reasoning

  • The judge found that the Wife's obligation to cover 'holding costs' included land tax and repairs to preserve the assets.

  • The court determined that a formal Statement of Claim was necessary to clarify the complex trust and equitable claims (resulting trust vs. life tenancy).

  • The court accepted the need to ventilate whether the s 79 power was exhausted regarding the SMSF, as the 'catch-all' orders may not have reached member accounts.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The 2017 orders were made on an 'undefended basis' which does not equate to a default judgment; the court must still find them just and equitable.

  • The Wife's claim of a $200,000 contribution from her father creates a triable issue regarding the trust assets.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act (alteration of property interests and exhaustion of power)

  • s 79A Family Law Act (setting aside orders)

  • s 79(2) (requirement for just and equitable orders)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Unclear status of the SMSF member accounts.

  • Conflicting accounts of the trust property's ownership (Family Trust vs. resulting trust for the Wife's father vs. life tenancy).

  • Lack of clarity on the 'remaindermen' of the trust property following the death of the Wife's father.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • E, born in 2002 (approximately 16 years old at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • History of an intervention order against the Husband.

  • Past concerns regarding the Wife's living conditions (home described as 'uninhabitable' and living 'above a shop').

  • Concerns regarding E's school attendance.

### Expert Evidence

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) previously involved; new ICL appointment ordered.

### Best Interests

  • The court found it in the child's best interests to have independent representation to provide a voice independent of the mother.

### Orders

  • Wife to file an affidavit by 13 April 2018 explaining why the Husband should not have the child's address.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) appointed for E under s 68L(2).

  • Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) invited to intervene under s 91B.

### Reasoning

  • The court is 'bereft of information' about E's current welfare and school attendance.

  • The history of DHHS involvement and the 'mystique' around E's current situation justifies s 91B intervention.

  • The court applied the 'Re K' guidelines, finding the case involves intractable conflict and potential alienation, justifying an ICL.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The child's age (16) makes her views significant, but her absence from school and the mother's 'silence' are red flags.

  • The failure of the 2017 parenting orders to provide certainty necessitated new intervention.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 68L (Independent Children's Lawyer)

  • s 91B (Intervention by state welfare authorities)

  • s 60CC (Best interests of the child)

  • s 69ZW (Reports from welfare authorities)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No current evidence explaining why the child is missing school.

  • Lack of information regarding the child's current residential conditions.

  • Uncertainty regarding the basis for current intervention orders.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Income & Expenses

  • Wife is paying $4,167 per month in holding costs for a property.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mention of an existing intervention order against the Husband.

### Protection Orders

  • Intervention order is in place, which the Wife cites as a reason for non-disclosure of the address.

### Child Exposure

  • Past concerns about the child's living environment being 'uninhabitable'.

### Orders

  • Wife restrained from attending property inspections to prevent conflict.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the uncertainty regarding the reasons for the intervention orders and the need for DHHS to provide clarity.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The existence of an intervention order is a factor in the Wife's refusal to disclose the child's address, but the court requires evidence to sustain this refusal.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) (Protection from harm)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The specific grounds for the current intervention orders were not detailed in the judgment.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case highlights the procedural and jurisdictional complexities that arise when final orders are made in undefended hearings with incomplete financial disclosure, particularly involving third-party trust structures.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court's power under s 79 may not be 'exhausted' if specific assets (like SMSF accounts) were not clearly dealt with in the final orders.

  • Joinder of a third-party trustee is appropriate where a colorable claim of an equitable interest in trust property is raised, even if the claim is not yet fully particularised.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (finality) and s 79A (setting aside for miscarriage of justice or new evidence).

  • The use of s 91B and s 68L to fill evidentiary voids in high-conflict parenting matters where one parent is overseas and the other is non-communicative.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Wife's credibility is under scrutiny due to her previous non-participation in the 2017 hearing and her current 'unusual' silence regarding the child's welfare and address.

  • The Husband's credibility is tempered by his previous insistence on proceeding with an undefended hearing despite known evidentiary gaps.

  • The court noted the Wife's failure to provide evidence for her assertions (e.g., regarding school absences) as a significant procedural risk.

  • The Trustee's role is currently procedural, but the court noted the potential for costs if the Wife's anticipated claims against the trust prove unfounded.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All outstanding applications adjourned to a date to be fixed.

  • Wife to file an Amended Initiating Application and Statement of Claim by 4 May 2018.

  • Husband to file Amended Response and Defence by 5 June 2018.

  • Trustee to file Defence and submissions by 15 June 2018.

  • Costs of the parties and the Trustee are reserved.

  • Liberty to apply granted.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to move the case from a state of 'mystique' and 'confusion' toward a structured, pleaded dispute.

  • The court balanced the need for finality (April 2017 orders) against the reality that those orders were based on 'truncated' evidence and may have missed significant assets.

  • In parenting, the judge prioritized the child's right to independent representation (ICL) and the court's need for objective welfare information (DHHS) over the Wife's preference for privacy.

  • The requirement for the Wife to pay property expenses was based on her prior undertakings and the need to maintain the value of the disputed assets pending a final determination.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are the parents of one child, X, born in 2012.

  • Consent orders were originally made on 25 October 2012 providing for the child to spend significant time with the father.

  • In 2014, the father was arrested and charged with a violent crime, leading to a sentence of six years imprisonment with a non-parole period of three years and ten months.

  • The father has a history of drug and alcohol abuse starting at age 15, including cannabis and methamphetamine use, resulting in drug-induced psychosis and delusions.

  • The father was diagnosed with Antisocial Personality Disorder (ASPD) and has a history of violent offending, including convictions for assault causing harm in 2009 and 2013.

  • The father was released from prison in late 2017 and resides with his sister.

  • The father filed an Amended Application on 20 September 2018 seeking 12 supervised visits at a contact centre.

  • The father returned positive drug tests for amphetamines on 12 June 2018 and 11 July 2018, which he attributed to cold and flu medication.

  • The Parole Board issued a warrant for the father on 30 October 2018 due to multiple failures to report for supervision.

  • The mother opposes all time between the father and the child due to concerns regarding family violence and the father's instability.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born in 2012 (approximately 6 years old at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Father's history of drug-induced psychosis and methamphetamine use.

  • Father's diagnosis of Antisocial Personality Disorder and history of violent criminal offending.

  • Mother's fear of the father due to alleged serious domestic violence.

  • Father's non-compliance with parole and recent positive drug tests for amphetamines.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr K (Psychiatrist) report dated 13 March 2018: Diagnosed father with ASPD; noted prognosis is 'guarded' due to father's tendency to blame others and lack of engagement with therapy.

  • Family Consultant report dated 19 June 2018: Recommended 12 months of supervised time but noted the 'culmination of risk factors is concerning'.

  • Mr G (Psychologist): Provided a report for the father's criminal sentencing regarding his history of substance abuse and impulsivity.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined it was not in the child's best interests to resume time given the father's failure to address his violent behavior and substance abuse issues.

### Orders

  • The Amended Application filed 20 September 2018 is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court adopted a conservative approach appropriate for interim proceedings where risk of harm is present.

  • The father failed to demonstrate 'any energy' in accessing therapy for anger management or substance abuse since his release.

  • The benefits of a relationship were outweighed by the risks of psychological and emotional harm if contact was resumed and then terminated due to the father's instability.

  • The father's explanations for missing court and failing drug tests were found to be unconvincing when compared to Parole Board records.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's positive drug tests for amphetamines in June and July 2018 undermined his claims of rehabilitation.

  • The Parole Board's evidence of the father's non-compliance with reporting (missing 7 weeks of supervision) outweighed the father's claim of a 'mechanical breakdown' preventing court attendance.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b): The need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm was given greater weight than the benefit of a meaningful relationship under s 60CC(2)(a).

  • s 60CC(2A): Requirement to give greater weight to protection from harm.

  • s 61DA: The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was not applied due to reasonable grounds to believe there was family violence and the interim nature of the proceedings.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of evidence that the father has engaged in any psychological therapy for anger management or family violence since his release from prison.

  • Uncertainty regarding the father's current parole status following the October 2018 warrant.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Father convicted of assault causing harm in 2009 and 2013.

  • Father sentenced to 6 years imprisonment in 2014 for a violent crime.

  • Mother alleges a history of serious domestic violence during the relationship.

### Protection Orders

  • The mother remains fearful of the father and references previous injunctions.

### Child Exposure

  • The child has been largely protected from exposure due to the father's incarceration since 2013.

### Orders

  • Application for supervised time dismissed to ensure child's safety.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the father had not addressed his propensity for violent conduct or his difficulty in controlling anger.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's ASPD diagnosis and history of drug-induced psychosis created a high-risk profile that necessitated a protective approach.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: Definition of family violence applied to the father's criminal history and the mother's allegations.

  • s 60CG: Court's obligation to ensure parenting orders do not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts of the father's behavior since release, though Parole Board records provided independent verification of non-compliance.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case reinforces the 'conservative approach' in interim parenting proceedings where a parent has a significant history of violence and untreated substance abuse."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the child's emotional and psychological stability over the father's desire to re-establish a relationship, particularly given the father's lack of transparency regarding his parole breaches and drug use.

  • The father's failure to engage in recommended therapy (ASPD treatment) was a fatal flaw in his application for even supervised time.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) and (b), where the 'protection from harm' mandate (2A) decisively overrode the 'meaningful relationship' principle due to the father's active parole violations and positive drug tests.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's credibility was significantly compromised by the discrepancy between his affidavits and the Parole Board records.

  • The father's claim that cold and flu medication caused positive amphetamine readings was viewed with skepticism by the court and the Parole Board.

  • The father's excuse for missing a court date (mechanical breakdown) was contradicted by evidence of a pattern of avoiding supervision to evade drug testing.

  • The paternal grandmother's credibility as a potential supervisor was undermined by her 'unqualified support' for the father and alleged past failures to enforce court-ordered boundaries.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Amended Application filed 20 September 2018 is dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the principles from Marvel v Marvel and SS v AH, emphasizing that interim orders should be cautious and aimed at avoiding harm.

  • The court found that the father had not met the evidentiary burden of showing he had addressed the risks identified by Dr. K and the Family Consultant.

  • The father's lack of credibility regarding his drug use (claiming cold medicine caused positive amphetamine tests) and his court absence (claiming car trouble while parole records showed systemic non-reporting) led the court to doubt his commitment to rehabilitation.

  • The court determined that starting and potentially stopping contact (due to the father's instability) posed an unacceptable risk of psychological harm to the child.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The mother and father have a child, X, born in 2012.

  • Parenting orders were made on 8 December 2014 and 15 September 2017 providing for the child to spend time with the paternal grandmother.

  • An injunction restrained the paternal grandmother from taking the child to visit the father while he was incarcerated.

  • The mother ceased the child's time with the grandmother in March 2018 due to suspicions that the child was being brought into contact with the father.

  • The paternal grandmother filed contravention applications on 21 March 2018 and 2 August 2018 alleging 20 instances of non-compliance.

  • The contravention applications were dismissed on 29 August 2018 because the orders had been informally varied by correspondence, making them technically unenforceable via contravention.

  • The mother applied for costs fixed at $2,880.90, representing her Legal Aid expenditure.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Paternal Grandmother's home - $260,000

  • AGREED: Paternal Grandmother's motor vehicle and boat - $21,000

  • AGREED: Paternal Grandmother's superannuation - 'Limited'

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not specified as a matrimonial pool; Grandmother's personal assets total approximately $281,000

### Future Needs

  • The mother is in receipt of Legal Aid with a funding cap and modest financial circumstances.

  • The paternal grandmother is 68 years old, single, and works part-time in a bakery earning $400 per fortnight plus an aged pension of $876 per fortnight.

  • The grandmother has already paid $30,000 in legal fees since 2017 and has no remaining resources for future fees.

### Orders

  • The paternal grandmother pay the mother’s costs fixed in the sum of $1,440.

  • Payment to be made to the Trust Account of Carmen Wood and Associates within four calendar months.

### Reasoning

  • The court balanced the grandmother's total lack of success in the contravention proceedings against the fact that the mother's unilateral cessation of time was the catalyst for the litigation.

  • A 50% reduction of the sought costs ($1,440 of $2,880.90) was deemed appropriate given the grandmother's 'ill-conceived' choice of application type but acknowledging the utility of the proceedings in eventually reinstating time.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The grandmother was 'wholly unsuccessful' in the contravention proceedings, which is a significant factor under s 117(2A)(e).

  • The grandmother's financial position is 'parlous' despite owning a home, leading to an extension of time to pay.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117(1) general rule that parties bear their own costs.

  • Section 117(2A) factors for justifying a costs order.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2012 (6 years old at time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleged the grandmother was facilitating contact between the child and the father in breach of an injunction.

### Best Interests

  • The court noted the utility of the proceedings under s 70NBA to reconsider best interests, leading to the reinstatement of time in September 2018.

### Orders

  • Contravention applications filed 21 March 2018 and 2 August 2018 dismissed (ordered 29 August 2018).

  • Interim orders made 26 September 2018 for alternate weekend time (Saturday 10am to Sunday 4pm).

### Reasoning

  • The contravention applications failed because the underlying orders had been modified by correspondence, creating a technical defense for the mother as the 'orders' being breached were not the formal court orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The variations to parenting orders via correspondence resulted in an 'entirely different order' that was not enforceable through contravention proceedings.

  • The mother's defense was characterized as 'technical'.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 70NBA power to change parenting orders even if contravention is not established.

  • Section 121(9)(g) approval for publication under pseudonym.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No finding was made as to whether the mother had a 'reasonable excuse' for non-compliance based on her fear of the father, as the case was decided on the technical invalidity of the contravention claim.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Suspicion of child contact with the father contrary to an injunction.

### Protection Orders

  • Injunction restraining the paternal grandmother from taking the child to visit the father while incarcerated.

### Child Exposure

  • Mother's primary concern was the child's exposure to the father.

### Reasoning

  • The court acknowledged the mother's suspicion as the 'gravamen' of her non-compliance but did not make formal findings of family violence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case highlights the procedural risks of pursuing contravention applications when parenting arrangements have been informally varied, and the court's discretion to apportion costs when an unsuccessful application was nonetheless sparked by the other party's conduct."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • Informal variations to orders (via correspondence) can render formal orders unenforceable in quasi-criminal contravention proceedings.

  • A party's 'wholly unsuccessful' status in litigation is a primary driver for costs, but can be mitigated if their application had 'utility' in resolving broader parenting disputes.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 117 (costs) and s 70NBA (re-determining parenting orders during contravention hearings).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's defense was noted as 'technical' rather than merit-based regarding the child's safety.

  • The grandmother's litigation strategy was described as 'ill-conceived' for choosing contravention over a variation application.

  • The grandmother's financial disclosure regarding her $30,000 legal spend and limited superannuation was accepted by the court to assess capacity to pay.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The paternal grandmother pay the mother’s costs fixed in the sum of $1,440.

  • Payment to be made within four calendar months from 7 December 2018.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied s 117(2A) to determine if circumstances justified departing from the rule that each party pays their own costs.

  • The mother's reliance on Legal Aid and modest means favored a costs order to preserve her funding for the final hearing.

  • The grandmother's age (68) and limited income (pension plus $400/fn) necessitated a long grace period (4 months) for payment.

  • The court reduced the costs award by 50% because the mother's unilateral decision to stop time was the 'catalyst' for the grandmother's 'ill-conceived' legal action.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties entered into a financial agreement under s 90B of the Family Law Act on 26 July 2005, prior to their marriage.

  • The parties were married for approximately 12 years.

  • The husband became bankrupt on 6 November 2012 during the marriage.

  • The husband was discharged from bankruptcy by effluxion of time on 5 November 2015.

  • The parties separated on 10 December 2017.

  • On 3 April 2018, the Court made interim orders in the husband's absence, including property injunctions and a payment of $100,000 to the wife.

  • The husband challenged the Court's jurisdiction, arguing that his obligations under the 2005 financial agreement were contingent debts discharged by his bankruptcy.

  • The wife sought enforcement of the financial agreement under s 90KA of the Family Law Act.

  • The husband asserted that the interim injunctions and the $100,000 payment order paralyzed his property development business.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: B Street unit - Value not specified (Wife granted sole use and occupation)

  • DISPUTED: G Street property - Value not specified (Husband claims injunction prevents business viability)

  • DISPUTED: Husband's property development business interests - Value not specified

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated in the interim judgment

### Contributions

  • The marriage lasted 12 years.

  • The wife was not intimately involved in the day-to-day business dealings of the husband's property development enterprises.

  • The husband managed complex 'deals' involving third-party lenders and his daughter.

### Future Needs

  • The wife sought to enforce the 2005 financial agreement to secure her entitlements post-separation.

  • The wife demonstrated a need for interim financial support, including the payment of outgoings on her residence and motor vehicle expenses.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court sought to balance the wife's right to protect her entitlements under the financial agreement against the husband's need for business agility.

  • The court found it was not just to allow the husband to maintain unfettered control over assets given the breakdown of the marriage and his alleged threats to the wife's rights.

### Orders

  • The Court affirmed jurisdiction to hear the enforcement application.

  • Order 1 (3 April 2018) maintained: Wife to have sole use and occupation of the B Street unit.

  • Order 2 (3 April 2018) maintained: Husband to pay outgoings on the B Street unit.

  • Order 3 (3 April 2018) maintained: Husband to maintain payments on the wife’s motor vehicle.

  • Order 5 (3 April 2018) maintained: Husband to comply with discovery requirements.

  • Order 4(b)(ii) and (iii) (Injunctions) to be varied or set aside: These restricted property dealings on 60 days' notice and limited bank transactions to $5,000/$15,000.

  • Order 6 ($100,000 payment) to be varied or set aside: The court found the source of funds for this payment was unclear on the current evidence.

### Reasoning

  • The Court rejected the husband's jurisdictional argument that the financial agreement was a provable debt in bankruptcy.

  • Under s 90DA of the Family Law Act, a financial agreement has 'no force or effect' until a separation declaration is made; thus, it could not be a provable debt during the husband's bankruptcy (2012-2015) as the parties were still cohabiting.

  • The discharge of bankruptcy under s 153 of the Bankruptcy Act did not release the husband from the agreement because the liability had not yet crystallized.

  • The Court found that the husband's actions in signing a 'Termination Agreement' post-separation satisfied the requirements of a separation declaration under s 90DA(4).

  • Interim orders regarding the unit and car were maintained to preserve the wife's position, but broad injunctions were deemed 'unworkable' for a property development business.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The financial agreement was dormant and lacked legal force during the period of the husband's bankruptcy due to the absence of a separation declaration.

  • The husband's property development business required 'agility,' making broad injunctions against all property dealings counter-productive to the interests of both parties.

  • The husband's failure to provide full discovery left the court with concerns regarding his actual capacity to pay the $100,000 previously ordered.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 90DA Family Law Act: The 'no force or effect' provision was the primary reason the agreement survived the husband's bankruptcy.

  • s 90KA Family Law Act: Provided the court the power to enforce the agreement as if it were an order of the court.

  • Rule 17.02 Family Law Rules 2004: Allowed the court to vary orders made in the husband's absence.

  • s 82 Bankruptcy Act: The court determined the contingent liability was incapable of proper estimation during bankruptcy because the capacity to enforce had not arisen.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The husband failed to provide full and complete discovery as ordered on 3 April 2018.

  • There was no clear evidence identifying the specific source of funds available to satisfy the $100,000 interim payment order.

  • The husband's claims regarding the risk to the G Street property and his broader business empire remained largely untested.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • The wife demonstrated a need for the husband to cover her immediate living expenses (unit outgoings and car payments) pending the final enforcement of the BFA.

### Capacity to Pay
