summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties entered into consent property orders on 19 December 2016.

  • The Respondent failed to comply with property transfer orders within the required 42-day timeframe because she could not secure finance.

  • The Applicant granted the Respondent additional time to comply before filing a s 79A application on 21 September 2017 to vary the orders.

  • On 27 October 2017, the court made orders by consent allowing the Applicant to secure his property, but granted the Respondent a 12-month extension to finance her property instead of ordering an immediate sale.

  • The Applicant sought $3,000 in costs on an indemnity basis for the s 79A proceedings.

  • The Respondent filed a financial statement on 14 November 2017 but failed to file written submissions regarding the costs application.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Property to be transferred to the Applicant - Value not stated

  • AGREED: Property to be transferred to the Respondent - Value not stated

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated in the costs judgment

### Orders

  • The Respondent was granted an additional 12 months to obtain finance and comply with the property provisions of the orders.

  • Orders were made enabling the Applicant to secure in his name the property intended for him under the original orders.

### Reasoning

  • The s 79A application was necessitated by the Respondent's failure to comply with the essential parts of the 2016 consent orders.

  • The court balanced the Applicant's right to his property with the Respondent's need for more time to secure finance, ultimately granting a 12-month extension rather than an immediate sale.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Respondent's inability to secure finance was the sole reason the original consent orders were not executed.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A(1) variation of property orders due to a failure to carry out orders.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The judgment does not specify the valuations of the properties involved or the total net asset pool.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['The judgment reinforces that non-compliance with consent orders is a primary justification for departing from the general rule that each party bears their own costs under s 117.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The litigation was entirely necessitated by the Respondent's failure to comply with previous orders.

  • The Applicant acted reasonably by providing extensions of time before initiating litigation.

  • Both parties had a surplus of income over expenditure, making a costs order financially viable.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117(1) general rule on costs.

  • s 117(2A) factors for costs orders, specifically (c) conduct of parties and (e) failure to comply with previous orders.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Respondent's financial statement contained a calculation error, claiming a $950 weekly surplus when the actual figure was closer to $550.

  • The Respondent's failure to file submissions to resist the costs application undermined her position.

  • The Applicant's evidence regarding his attempts to resolve the matter without litigation was accepted as showing good conduct.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The respondent shall pay the costs of the applicant’s application in a case filed 21 September 2017 as agreed or assessed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge determined that the Respondent's failure to comply with the 2016 orders was sufficient reason to depart from the usual position that each party bears their own costs.

  • The Applicant was not 'wholly unsuccessful' despite not getting the immediate sale order, as he secured the property intended for him.

  • The court rejected the request for indemnity costs because the Applicant did not provide legal arguments or identify specific circumstances justifying such a high threshold.

  • The court noted the Applicant's reasonable conduct in delaying the application to give the Respondent a chance to comply.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were married for 15 years, separated in 2015, and divorced in 2018.

  • There are four children of the marriage: B (aged 15), Z (aged 13), X (aged 9), and Y (aged 7).

  • The child B has expressed suicidal thoughts, is consulting a psychologist, and has not seen the father since 3 April 2018.

  • The child Z has mental health concerns, has been an in-patient at K Hospital, and has not seen the father since March 2018.

  • The children X and Y live with the mother and have supervised time with the father.

  • A sexual incident occurred between B and Z in March 2018, leading to criminal proceedings against B which were resolved via a restorative justice program.

  • The father and B were charged with stalking the mother following an incident on 1 April 2018.

  • A temporary protection order was made in favor of the mother on 10 May 2018.

  • The father filed an application on 25 October 2018 to remove the Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) and restrain the mother's solicitors (KLM Solicitors).

  • The final hearing for parenting orders is scheduled for 11 February 2019.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B (15), Z (13), X (9), Y (7)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of sexual abuse involving B and Z; allegations of family violence by the father; father's stalking charges; B's suicidal ideation.

### Expert Evidence

  • Psychiatrist Dr. Q assessed the parties; Social worker Mr. R prepared a family report; B and Z are seeing psychologists.

### Best Interests

  • The court focused on the ICL's duty to form an independent view of the children's best interests under s 68LA.

### Orders

  • The application filed 25 October 2018 seeking the removal of the independent children’s lawyer be dismissed.

  • The application seeking an injunction restraining KLM Solicitors from acting for the mother be dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The father failed to establish a proper basis for the ICL's removal.

  • The ICL is not obliged to act on the child's instructions or comply with every request from a party.

  • The ICL is actively preparing for the final hearing and has obtained expert reports from Dr. Q and Mr. R.

  • The court found no evidence of bias or failure in the ICL's statutory duties under s 68LA.

  • The father's claim that the ICL or the mother's lawyer would be material witnesses was rejected as baseless.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The ICL's decision not to respond to every piece of 'prolix' correspondence from the father did not constitute a failure of duty.

  • The father's 'demands' were characterized as intemperate and ill-informed.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 68LA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the role and duties of the ICL.

  • Section 60CC regarding the best interests of the children.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The father failed to provide the specific correspondence (letter of 5 October 2018) that he claimed showed ICL bias.

  • No evidence was provided to support the claim that the ICL or mother's solicitor were material witnesses.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Allegation that the father held the mother by the throat.

  • Father and B charged with stalking the mother on 1 April 2018.

### Protection Orders

  • Temporary protection order in favor of the mother and children, varied on 10 May 2018.

### Child Exposure

  • Children exposed to family violence and sexual themes according to B's communication to the father.

### Reasoning

  • Protection from family violence is a matter to be determined at the final trial in February 2019.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The family was described as 'spiralling into crisis' following the March 2018 incident between the children.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Mother's evidence regarding the 'throat holding' incident lacked particularity at the interim stage.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case reinforces the high threshold required to remove an Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) and the court's reluctance to interfere with the ICL's professional judgment in complex, high-conflict parenting matters."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court distinguished between a party's perception of bias and the ICL's statutory obligation to form an independent view.

  • The father's attempts to subpoena the ICL and file complaints with the Legal Services Commission did not automatically render the ICL a 'material witness' or necessitate removal.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 68LA (Role of ICL) acted as the primary shield against the father's application.

  • The interplay between the ICL's duty to minimize trauma (s 68LA(5)(d)) and the father's demands for expanded therapy/contact.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's credibility was undermined by his 'intemperate' and 'ill-informed' demands and the volume of his correspondence (14 emails in one week).

  • The father's reliance on a communication from B (which contained significant vitriol and allegations of abuse against the father) as evidence of the ICL's failure was viewed skeptically by the court.

  • The court noted the father's failure to put key documents (like the 5 October 2018 letter or the subpoena) into evidence, creating a significant evidentiary gap for his own application.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Application in a case filed 25 October 2018 seeking the removal of the independent children’s lawyer and an injunction restraining KLM Solicitors from acting for the mother be dismissed.

  • Costs of and incidental to the application be reserved to the trial.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the principles from Knibbs & Knibbs [2009] FamCA 840, noting that while actual bias is not strictly required for removal, the court must be cautious in complex cases.

  • The court found that the ICL's failure to respond to every email from a 'prolix' litigant is not evidence of professional failure.

  • The judge determined that the ICL's role is to assist the court, not to satisfy the demands of the parents, particularly regarding the organization of family therapy or mediation.

  • The application to restrain the mother's solicitors was dismissed due to a total lack of evidence that they would be material witnesses.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in 2001, married in 2003, and separated in late 2009 or early 2010.

  • The child, X, was born in 2006 and was 12 years old at the time of the hearing.

  • Both parents have histories of mental health challenges, including ADHD and depression; the Father was diagnosed with ADHD in 2004 and the Mother in 2003.

  • In 2012, the Mother's then-partner, Mr. T, attempted to strangle her while the child was in the next room, leading to an AVO.

  • The Father initiated proceedings in September 2015 following concerns about the child's behavior and alleged neglect/exposure to inappropriate conduct in the Mother's care.

  • Interim orders in October 2015 placed the child with the Father with supervised time for the Mother.

  • In March and April 2018, the Mother twice retained the child in breach of orders following the child's allegations of abuse by the Father, which were later found to be coached or unsubstantiated.

  • The child has expressed self-destructive thoughts, including a post-it note stating 'I feel like killing myself' in June 2018.

  • The Mother currently resides in a two-bedroom social housing property as of June 2018.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2006 (12 years old at hearing)

### Safety Concerns

  • Exposure to violence by Mother's former partner Mr. T in 2012.

  • Allegations by Father of child's sexualized behavior possibly stemming from Mother's care.

  • Allegations by Mother of Father's physical and emotional abuse (found unsubstantiated).

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. D (Psychiatrist) provided a report in July 2017 and oral evidence.

  • Dr. D found the Father to be the more stable, protective parent but noted he 'pathologized' some of the child's behaviors.

  • Dr. D described the child as emotionally immature, unregulated, and a 'day dreamer' at risk of poor regulation without stability.

### Best Interests

  • Stability and protection from further conflict-driven trauma were deemed paramount.

  • The court found it in the child's best interests to remain with the Father due to his superior parenting capacity and stability.

### Orders

  • Father to have sole parental responsibility for the child.

  • Child to live with the Father.

  • Graduated unsupervised time for the Mother: 6 months of day-only time (Wednesdays and alternate Saturdays); then moving to alternate weekends (Friday to Monday) by 2020.

  • Specific injunctions against denigrating the other parent.

  • Child to continue counseling with Ms. B at C Group.

  • Child to continue seeing pediatrician Dr. E.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted due to the parties' high conflict, lack of communication, and mutual suspicion.

  • The Father has been the more stable influence since separation.

  • The Mother's actions in 2018 (retaining the child and facilitating police contact for coached allegations) demonstrated a lack of insight into the child's best interests.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Mother's 2018 retention of the child was unjustified and harmful to the child's stability.

  • The Father's concerns about sexual abuse were 'pathologizing' but did not disqualify him as the primary carer.

  • The child's mental health (ADHD and anxiety) requires a highly stable environment which the Father is better equipped to provide.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(a) benefit of meaningful relationship.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from harm (weighted more heavily).

  • s 61DA presumption of equal shared parental responsibility (rebutted).

  • s 60CC(3)(i) attitude to responsibilities of parenthood.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of corroboration for Mother's allegations of Father's physical violence during the marriage.

  • Conflicting interpretations of the child's 'sexualized' behaviors between the Father and the expert Dr. D.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Assault on Mother by Mr. T in 2012.

  • Alleged grabbing and yelling by Father during marriage (belatedly raised by Mother).

### Protection Orders

  • AVO against Mr. T in 2012 for the protection of Mother and child.

### Child Exposure

  • Child was in the next room during the 2012 assault by Mr. T.

  • Child exposed to high-level parental conflict and 'battle' mentality.

### Orders

  • Mother restrained from presenting the child to any unauthorized therapists or practitioners.

  • Injunctions against denigration.

### Reasoning

  • The court did not find an unacceptable risk of violence in the Father's home.

  • The Mother's allegations were inconsistent with her own sought orders (asking for unsupervised overnight time).

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Mother's failure to report physical violence until late in the proceedings undermined the credibility of those claims.

  • The 2012 incident with Mr. T established a history of the child being exposed to harm in the Mother's environment.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2A) greater weight to protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses or medical reports for the Mother's allegations of Father's violence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the court's willingness to rebut the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility in instances of extreme parental conflict and 'coaching' of a child. It highlights the use of graduated time orders to rebuild relationships while maintaining the stability of the primary residence."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Father's stability and commitment to the child's medical/educational needs outweighed his tendency to over-interpret (pathologize) the child's behaviors.

  • The Mother's lack of insight and facilitation of the child's false/coached allegations against the Father posed a psychological risk to the child.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 61DA (presumption) and s 60CC (best interests) resulted in a total shift of responsibility to one parent to ensure decisive management of the child's complex needs.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother was found to be a 'poor witness' who was inconsistent and likely misled the court regarding her past diagnoses and allegations of violence.

  • The Father was found to be a reliable witness despite his slow and 'tangential' responses during cross-examination.

  • The Mother's supporting witnesses were found to be biased and ill-disposed toward the Father, rendering their evidence of little weight.

  • The Father's willingness to correct his own counsel to his forensic disadvantage (regarding Ms. J's income) enhanced his credibility.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous parenting orders discharged.

  • Father granted sole parental responsibility for the child.

  • Child to live with the Father.

  • Mother to have graduated unsupervised time starting with day-only visits, progressing to overnights, and eventually alternate weeks by Term 3, 2020.

  • Father to pay one half of the ICL's costs, fixed at $6,666.50, within three months.

  • Parties granted leave to apply within 28 days regarding the wording of the orders.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the Father's proven track record of providing a stable, functioning home over the Mother's history of housing instability and exposure of the child to a violent partner.

  • The Mother's credibility was significantly damaged by her late-filed, inconsistent allegations of violence against the Father, which the court viewed as forensically motivated.

  • The court adopted the ICL's graduated time proposal to balance the child's need for a relationship with the Mother against the need to prevent further 'retention' incidents and ensure the child's emotional regulation.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were involved in a property settlement dispute that concluded a three-day hearing on 25 October 2018.

  • Orders were made on 29 October 2018 requiring the Respondent to sell the real property at B Street, Suburb C.

  • The Applicant nominated Mr. FF of the FF Group as the estate agent on 19 November 2018.

  • The Respondent failed to formally instruct the nominated agent to place the property on the market.

  • The Applicant filed an application on 3 December 2018 seeking sole conduct of the sale and a warrant for possession.

  • The Respondent filed an affidavit on 9 December 2018 at 11:47 pm, asserting he was awaiting confirmation of the nomination and discussing renovations.

  • The Applicant abandoned her request for renovations to expedite the sale in its current condition.

  • The court found the Respondent had obfuscated and failed to comply with the spirit and letter of the October 2018 orders.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property at B Street, Suburb C - $880,000 (Court-mandated reserve price)

  • DISPUTED: Value of potential renovations - Not quantified as the Applicant abandoned the claim to expedite sale

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated as a total sum, but centered on the $880,000 property value

### Contributions

  • The judgment refers to a prior three-day hearing regarding contributions, but specific domestic or financial percentages are not re-litigated in this enforcement stage.

  • The court noted the ultimate sale price was to be divided on a percentage basis between the parties as determined in the primary judgment.

### Future Needs

  • Not specifically addressed in this enforcement judgment, though the court noted the parties have limited assets but are not impecunious.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court determined it was just and equitable to modify the sale process to ensure the property was sold by February 2019 due to the Respondent's lack of cooperation.

### Orders

  • Paragraph (2) of the orders made on 29 October 2018 is discharged.

  • Mr. FF (of the FF Group) is appointed as the agent for the sale of the Suburb C property.

  • The auction is to be held on 9 February 2019 or 16 February 2019.

  • The reserve price is fixed at $880,000.

  • The contract shall provide for a 60-day settlement.

  • Advertising and sale preparation expenses are capped at $3,000.

  • The agent’s commission is fixed at 1.32 per cent of the gross sale price.

  • The Respondent must sign necessary documents by 4:00 pm on 8 December 2018.

  • The Respondent must make the property available for inspections and the auction.

  • Mr. GG is appointed as the conveyancing solicitor.

### Reasoning

  • The judge found the Respondent's failure to instruct the agent for three weeks after nomination constituted a failure to act 'forthwith' as required by previous orders.

  • The court determined that a 'tightly controlled process' was necessary because the parties are 'very unhappy and mistrusting litigants'.

  • The judge rejected the Respondent's claim that he needed more time to prepare, finding his arguments to be obfuscation.

  • The court decided to fix the sale terms (reserve, commission, dates) directly to eliminate further opportunities for dispute.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Respondent's position was found to be 'untenable' and characterized by 'obfuscation'.

  • The court found that the property valuation had already been established by a single expert witness, Mr. H, making further delays for 'examination' unnecessary.

  • The Applicant's delay in nominating the agent (from late October to mid-November) was noted but did not excuse the Respondent's subsequent three-week inaction.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 regarding the court's power to make property orders.

  • The court's inherent power to vary the machinery of sale orders to ensure the primary judgment is satisfied.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Respondent failed to file a formal response to the Applicant's case, filing only a late affidavit.

  • Conflicting accounts of a conversation with the agent Mr. FF; the court preferred the version provided by the Applicant's counsel.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Reasoning

  • While no specific new incidents were detailed, the court referenced previous findings by Thornton J and Johns J as foundation for the Applicant's concern regarding the Respondent's lack of cooperation.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court acknowledged a history of litigation and 'mistrust' which necessitated judicial intervention in the sale process.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case demonstrates the court's willingness to strip a legal owner of practical control over a sale process when they demonstrate a pattern of obfuscation and non-compliance with previous orders."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the finality of litigation over the Respondent's rights as a legal owner to manage the sale of his property.

  • The Respondent's self-represented status did not shield him from a costs order where his conduct was found to be obstructive.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117 of the Family Law Act 1975: Departure from the principle that each party bears their own costs due to the Respondent's 'untenable' position.

  • s 117(2A): Consideration of the parties' financial circumstances and conduct in awarding costs.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court explicitly found the Respondent's evidence and submissions to be 'obfuscating' and his conduct characterized by a 'lack of cooperation'.

  • The Applicant's counsel's version of events regarding the agent's appointment was preferred over the Respondent's version.

  • The Respondent's history of non-compliance, as noted by previous judges (Thornton J and Johns J), significantly undermined his credibility in the eyes of Justice Cronin.

  • The Respondent's late filing of an affidavit (minutes before midnight) was a procedural concern noted by the court.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Applicant is authorized to attend the auction and participate in all activities.

  • The Applicant has liberty to apply to the Court on short notice regarding any non-cooperation by the Respondent.

  • The Respondent is ordered to pay the Applicant’s costs fixed at $2,270, to be deducted from his share of the sale proceeds.

  • The application filed on 3 December 2018 is otherwise dismissed (specifically regarding the warrant for possession).
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The overarching logic was the necessity to bring a long-running dispute (active since 2015) to a definitive end.

  • The judge balanced the Applicant's right to receive her judgment entitlement against the Respondent's procedural rights, ultimately finding the Respondent's delays justified a more prescriptive judicial approach.

  • The costs order of $2,270 was calculated as compensation for the Applicant having to litigate to achieve a 'correct outcome' rather than as a punishment, using a lower scale of fees.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property orders were made by consent on 17 August 2017.

  • Final parenting orders were made by Hogan J on 16 July 2018.

  • The husband filed an Application in a Case on 30 July 2018 seeking enforcement of personal property orders.

  • The husband filed a further Application in a Case on 3 September 2018 seeking to set aside the 2017 property settlement.

  • The husband sought leave to appeal the 2017 settlement out of time and invoked section 79A(1A) to set aside orders.

  • The husband alleged a marriage contract/financial agreement existed under section 71A that should have prevented court involvement.

  • The Registrar made orders on 4 September 2018 regarding the withdrawal of certain applications, which the husband sought to review.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property at D Street, Suburb E - Value not specified (to be transferred to Wife unencumbered)

  • AGREED: Cash payment from Husband to Wife - $60,000.00 (payable in two $30,000 installments)

  • AGREED: Real property at J Street, Suburb K - Value not specified (subject to default sale provision)

  • AGREED: Real property and assets in [Country B] - Values not specified

  • AGREED: Superannuation, motor vehicles, shares, and bank accounts held in respective names - Values not specified

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated in the judgment

### Contributions

  • The husband claimed his share and entitlement under the 2017 orders were unequal.

  • The husband asserted that the wife's contributions were 'extremely minimal and unjust'.

### Just & Equitable

  • The husband argued the 17 August 2017 consent orders were unjust and sought a re-exercise of discretion.

### Orders

  • The Application in a Case filed 30 July 2018 (as amended 21 September 2018) is dismissed.

  • The Application in a Case filed 3 September 2018 is dismissed.

  • Any Application Initiating proceedings under section 79A must be filed and served by 18 January 2019.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that because final property and parenting orders had already been made, there were no ongoing primary proceedings.

  • An 'Application in a Case' was the incorrect procedural vehicle; the husband must file an 'Application Initiating Proceedings' to invoke section 79A.

  • The court noted the husband's application was 'inelegantly' drafted and failed to specify the particular grounds or facts relied upon for a section 79A claim.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court determined that enforcement of the 2017 orders (regarding personal effects) was inappropriate while the husband was simultaneously attempting to set those same orders aside.

  • The finality of the 17 August 2017 and 16 July 2018 orders meant the litigation had concluded, requiring a fresh initiating application for any further substantive relief.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 79A(1A): The provision the husband sought to use to set aside consent orders.

  • Section 71A: Invoked by the husband regarding the effect of a financial agreement/marriage contract.

  • Section 106A: Referenced in the 2017 orders regarding the execution of documents by a Registrar.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The husband failed to specify which subsection of 79A he relied upon.

  • Lack of particularity regarding the facts and circumstances supporting the set-aside application.

  • The 'imprecise' nature of the order regarding 'personal effects and possessions' made enforcement difficult.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Ages not specified; children identified as [Mr L Akhtar], [Mr M Akhtar], and [Ms N Akhtar].

### Expert Evidence

  • Reference to an Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) who facilitated mediation.

### Reasoning

  • Parenting matters were noted as resolved by final orders made by Hogan J on 16 July 2018.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The conclusion of parenting proceedings reinforced the court's view that no primary proceedings remained on foot.
summary_sections.prenup_postnup:
### Agreement Details

  • Date not specified; referred to as an 'official marriage contact'.

### Reasoning

  • The husband argued that the existence of a marriage contract under section 71A should have prevented the court from making the 2017 property orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court did not rule on the validity of the agreement but noted it as a ground the husband intended to argue in his section 79A application.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 71A: Used to argue the exclusion of the court's jurisdiction.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The actual terms and execution details of the alleged marriage contract were not provided in the judgment.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case clarifies the procedural boundary between 'Applications in a Case' and 'Initiating Applications' following the conclusion of final orders."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • A party cannot seek to enforce consent orders while simultaneously challenging their validity under section 79A.

  • Procedural regularity requires a new initiating application once final orders have been entered and no primary cause of action remains.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 79A: The threshold for reopening final property settlements.

  • Family Law Rules: Governing the form of applications (Initiating vs. In a Case).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility was not directly attacked, but his litigation conduct was characterized as 'inelegant' and procedurally confused.

  • The husband's shifting position regarding the withdrawal of his application (resiling from consent) necessitated a formal dismissal rather than a simple withdrawal.

  • The husband bears a significant evidentiary burden to prove grounds under section 79A, which the court noted had not yet been 'clearly elucidated'.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Application in a Case filed 30 July 2018 as amended by the Amended Application in a Case filed 21 September 2018 is dismissed.

  • Any Application Initiating proceedings under section 79A be filed and served by no later than Friday, 18 January 2019.

  • The costs of both parties of today be reserved.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized procedural correctness, ruling that the husband's attempt to review a Registrar's order via an interim application was inappropriate given the finality of the previous property and parenting judgments.

  • The court found a logical inconsistency in the husband's dual pursuit of enforcement and rescission of the same orders.

  • The dismissal of the current applications was intended to force the husband to clearly articulate his legal grounds (e.g., fraud, duress, or miscarriage of justice) in a proper initiating format.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The father filed an Initiating Application on 23 March 2018 seeking sole parental responsibility and that the child, X, live with him.

  • The child, X, was born in 2012 (also cited as 2013 in the orders).

  • At the time of the application, the child was in the care of Ms. B.

  • Care proceedings were simultaneously pending in the Children’s Court of Victoria.

  • On 28 June 2018, the Children’s Court of Victoria ordered that parental responsibility for X be conferred on the Secretary (child welfare) to the exclusion of all others until 28 June 2020.

  • The Children’s Court Magistrate found the child was at risk of serious harm in the care of either parent.

  • The father was informed of the jurisdictional bar under s 69ZK but sought an adjournment to obtain Legal Aid.

  • On the final hearing date of 20 November 2018, neither the father nor the mother appeared in court.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born in 2012 (approximately 5-6 years old at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • The Children’s Court of Victoria found the child was at risk of serious harm if left in the care of either the father or the mother.

### Expert Evidence

  • Findings and reasons for judgment from the presiding Magistrate of the Children’s Court of Victoria (dated 28 June 2018).

### Best Interests

  • The court determined it could not make orders in the child's best interests due to a lack of jurisdiction under s 69ZK.

### Orders

  • The application initiating proceedings filed 23 March 2018 be dismissed for want of jurisdiction pursuant to section 69ZK of the Act.

  • The Applicant father be restrained from again commencing parenting proceedings in relation to X without providing written consent of a child welfare officer of the relevant State or Territory.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that because the child was under the care of the Secretary via a state child welfare law, s 69ZK of the Family Law Act 1975 prohibited the federal court from making parenting orders.

  • The father failed to provide written consent from the Victorian child welfare authority to proceed with the application.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The existence of the Victorian Children's Court order dated 28 June 2018, which granted parental responsibility to the Secretary until 28 June 2020, was the determinative fact.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 69ZK of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), which limits the jurisdiction of the Family Court when a child is under the care of a state welfare authority.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The father failed to appear at the hearing on 20 November 2018 to argue his position or provide evidence of welfare officer consent.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • The Children's Court of Victoria made findings that the child was at risk of serious harm in the care of the parents.

### Protection Orders

  • State-based care orders (Children, Youth and Families Act 2005 (Vic)) were in place.

### Child Exposure

  • The Magistrate's reasons (Exhibit B) indicated the child was at risk of serious harm, implying exposure to unsafe parenting environments.

### Orders

  • The father is restrained from filing further parenting applications without the written consent of a child welfare officer.

### Reasoning

  • The restraint on future applications was necessary to prevent the father from circumventing state welfare protections and to ensure the child's safety as identified by the Victorian Children's Court.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Children's Court findings were 'adverse' to both parents, establishing a high-risk profile for the child.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) (protection from harm) and s 69ZK (interaction with state welfare laws).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Specific details of the 'serious harm' were contained in Exhibit B but not fully detailed in the ex tempore reasons, though they were sufficient to trigger the jurisdictional bar.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case reinforces the strict jurisdictional boundary between the Family Court of Australia and State-based child welfare systems under s 69ZK of the Family Law Act.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court confirmed that once a state court has granted parental responsibility to a state department (Secretary), the Family Court's power to make parenting orders is effectively suspended unless specific statutory exceptions (like written consent) are met.

  • The father's failure to appear and the existence of adverse findings regarding child safety in a lower court necessitated a dismissal to prevent conflicting orders and protect the child.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 69ZK of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) acts as a mandatory jurisdictional bar in the presence of state care orders.

  • The use of the court's power to restrain a party from filing further applications without specific leave (consent of a welfare officer) serves as a protective measure for children already under state protection.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's credibility was undermined by his failure to appear at the final hearing on 20 November 2018.

  • The Children's Court of Victoria had already made findings adverse to the father's suitability as a carer, identifying a risk of serious harm.

  • The father's application was described as 'inelegantly expressed,' suggesting a lack of legal merit or professional guidance at the time of filing.

  • The father's failure to serve the application on the mother or the carer (Ms. B) by May 2018 indicated procedural non-compliance.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The application initiating proceedings filed 23 March 2018 is dismissed.

  • The proceedings are removed from the active pending cases list.

  • The Applicant father is restrained from commencing further parenting proceedings regarding child X without written consent from a child welfare officer.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was based on the lack of jurisdiction. Since the child was already under the legal care of the Victorian Secretary via the Children, Youth and Families Act 2005 (Vic), the Family Court was legally barred from intervening under s 69ZK.

  • The court noted that the father had been given opportunities to provide the necessary consent or withdraw the application but failed to do so, leading to a dismissal for want of jurisdiction.

  • The adverse findings of the Children's Court regarding the risk of harm to the child were central to the decision to restrain the father from future filings without welfare oversight.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties have been involved in parenting litigation in the Federal Circuit Court at Dandenong since 2009.

  • There are three children of the relationship: X (born 2006), Y (born 2007), and Z (born 2008).

  • On 25 October 2017, consent orders were made for equal shared parental responsibility and a week-about living arrangement.

  • On 6 June 2018, the parties signed a private typewritten agreement (parenting plan) where the children would live with the Mother and spend alternate weekends and holidays with the Father.

  • In late July 2018, the Mother relocated the children from the B Town area to New South Wales without the Father's consent or a court order.

  • The Mother alleged she fled B Town on 29 July 2018 after a man associated with a motorcycle club threatened her with an axe outside her home.

  • On 11 September 2018, the Father obtained ex parte orders from the B Town Magistrates’ Court, which included recovery orders and a grant of sole parental responsibility to him.

  • The Mother filed an application on 9 November 2018 seeking an extension of time to appeal the Magistrate's orders.

  • The Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) provided a report on 15 November 2018 stating the children were not at significant risk in the Mother's care.

  • The Australian Federal Police (AFP) held a recovery order but were unable to locate the Mother until she initiated legal proceedings.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2006) - 12 years old

  • Y (born 2007) - 11 years old

  • Z (born 2008) - 10 years old

### Safety Concerns

  • Father alleged Mother's partner (Mr. C) was violent and inappropriately disciplined the children.

  • Mother alleged Father was violent and made threats to 'bash her head in'.

  • Mother alleged a threat by a motorcycle club member with an axe.

### Expert Evidence

  • DHHS report (15 November 2018) confirmed children were not at significant risk with the Mother and were attending school in NSW with no concerns.

  • Family Report dated 2 July 2012 (noted as outdated and used without permission by the Father).

### Best Interests

  • Court found it was in the best interests to restore the June 2018 status quo pending a full hearing.

  • Stability with the primary carer (Mother) outweighed the immediate enforcement of the Father's weekend time, provided the Mother returned to the B Town area.

### Orders

  • Magistrate's orders of 11 September 2018 discharged.

  • Mother to return children to the B Town area.

  • Mother restrained by injunction from removing children from the B Town area.

  • Recovery order and warrant cancelled.

  • Children to live with the Mother.

  • Children to spend alternate weekends with the Father (Friday after school to Monday morning).

  • Changeovers to occur outside B Town Police Station.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) appointed on an urgent basis.

### Reasoning

  • The Magistrate's ex parte orders were inappropriate given the controversial nature of the facts and the lack of immediate risk evidence.

  • The DHHS report was a critical piece of independent evidence that negated the 'unacceptable risk' claim used to justify the ex parte recovery orders.

  • The June 2018 agreement, although not a registered court order, represented the parties' most recent consensus on the children's best interests.

  • The court utilized s 33B(7) to make interim 'holding' orders to stabilize the children's lives before the matter returned to the Federal Circuit Court.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The DHHS report of 15 November 2018 finding no significant risk in the Mother's care.

  • The existence of the signed 6 June 2018 agreement which the Father failed to fully disclose to the Magistrate.

  • The Mother's relocation to NSW was a breach of a previous injunction but was not sufficient to justify a summary change of residence without a hearing.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC: Best interests of the child as the primary consideration.

  • s 67Z: Obligations regarding reports from state child protection authorities.

  • s 33B(7): Power to make interim orders pending disposal by the Federal Circuit Court.

  • s 68B: Injunctive power for the welfare of children.

  • s 96(4): Requirement for a de novo hearing on appeal from summary jurisdiction.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The truth of the 'axe incident' involving the motorcycle club member remains a disputed factual 'battleground'.

  • Conflicting testimony regarding the Father's alleged violence and the Mother's partner's (Mr. C) behavior.

  • Lack of reasons provided by the original Magistrate for the 11 September 2018 orders.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged threat by a motorcycle club member with an axe on 29 July 2018.

  • Allegations of Father's verbal abuse and threats to 'bash' the Mother's head.

  • Allegations of Mother's partner (Mr. C) using inappropriate physical discipline.

### Protection Orders

  • Reference to previous intervention order proceedings in the State Magistrates' Court.

### Child Exposure

  • Children allegedly reported Father's threats to the Mother.

  • Children were present during the Mother's flight to NSW.

### Orders

  • Injunction restraining Mother from removing children from B Town.

  • Mutual injunctions from October 2017 (noted as still relevant) against denigrating or exposing children to violence.

### Reasoning

  • The court could not make final findings on violence at an interim stage but prioritized the DHHS assessment that the children were safe with the Mother.

  • The injunction for the Mother to return to B Town was balanced against her safety fears, which the court noted appeared to involve a third party rather than the Father directly.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court declined to draw an inference that the Father orchestrated the motorcycle club threat without tested evidence.

  • The lack of police records showing urgency for protection for the Mother undermined her justification for the unilateral relocation.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: Definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b): The need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm from being subjected to, or exposed to, relevant family violence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent witnesses for the axe incident.

  • No police reports confirming the severity of the alleged threats by the Father.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case serves as a significant precedent regarding the misuse of ex parte recovery orders and the weight of independent agency reports in interim parenting disputes.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that the Father's failure to provide a full factual context to the Magistrate (including the June 2018 agreement) rendered the ex parte orders inappropriate.

  • The DHHS report acted as a 'statutory shield' for the Mother, providing the court with a neutral basis to reject the Father's claims of unacceptable risk.

  • The Mother's unilateral relocation, while a breach of prior injunctions, did not automatically disqualify her as the primary carer if the children's best interests favored stability.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 96 (de novo appeal) and s 33B (transfer powers) allowed the Family Court to correct a summary jurisdiction error while ensuring the matter was properly positioned for the Federal Circuit Court.

  • The application of Re K criteria for the urgent appointment of an ICL in high-conflict cases with risk allegations.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother's credibility was questioned regarding her claim that she was unaware of the recovery orders for nearly a month.

  • The Mother's solicitor was heavily criticized for claiming not to know his client's address, with the judge citing R v Bell; Ex parte Lees regarding professional obligations to the court.

  • The Father's credibility was undermined by his selective disclosure to the Magistrate, specifically omitting the positive June 2018 agreement.

  • The 'bizarre' nature of the allegations from both sides suggested a high level of parental conflict and potential coaching or exposure of the children to adult disputes, necessitating the ICL.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Leave granted to the Mother to appeal out of time.

  • Magistrate's orders of 11 September 2018 discharged.

  • Mother ordered to return children to B Town area.

  • Mother restrained from removing children from B Town area.

  • Recovery order and warrant cancelled.

  • Interim residence with Mother; alternate weekend time with Father.

  • Changeovers at B Town Police Station.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer appointed via Victoria Legal Aid.

  • Matter transferred to Federal Circuit Court at Dandenong for hearing on 17 December 2018.

  • Application in a case filed 9 November 2018 otherwise dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The overarching logic was the restoration of the status quo established by the parties' own agreement in June 2018, which the court viewed as the most reliable indicator of the children's best interests prior to the breakdown in communication.

  • Justice Cronin emphasized that the court should not lightly reverse care arrangements (citing Rice v Asplund) and that the Magistrate's decision to do so ex parte was a procedural failure given the controversial facts.

  • The court balanced the Mother's breach of relocation injunctions against the Father's right to a meaningful relationship, concluding that a return to the local area (B Town) was necessary to facilitate the Father's time without uprooting the children from their primary carer.

  • The judge criticized both parties for treating court orders as 'disposable arrangements' and sought to impose a more permanent, supervised structure via the ICL and the upcoming Federal Circuit Court hearing.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were involved in ongoing property settlement proceedings.

  • On 30 August 2017, interim orders granted the husband $100,000 and the wife $50,000.

  • On 28 March 2018, interim consent orders granted each party a further $25,000 from the sale of the former matrimonial home at B Town.

  • The husband applied on 20 September 2018 for a further $100,000 interim settlement, later amended to $27,000 for living expenses and $25,000 for litigation funding.

  • The husband is employed on a fixed-term contract (ending 26 March 2021) earning $109,829 per annum.

  • The husband claimed monthly expenses exceeded his income by $3,770.53 and had an $80,000 overdraft and $25,000 in unpaid legal fees.

  • The wife opposed the application, alleging the husband was profligate and had already received 35% of the asset pool.

  • The wife's health was deteriorating, impacting her ability to manage her business interests.

  • The wife received significant insurance payouts, including trauma insurance ($127,533 and $281,420.07) and a robbery claim ($87,102).
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Joint ANZ Banking Group account - $273,322.71

  • AGREED: Husband's business value - $20,772

  • DISPUTED: Wife's interest in D Pty Ltd and Davids Trust - $810,850 (Expert Mr. E) vs. lower value claimed by Wife due to health/turnover

  • DISPUTED: Wife's trauma insurance payments - $408,953.07 (Husband seeks inclusion; Wife seeks exclusion)

  • DISPUTED: Robbery insurance payout received by Wife - $87,102

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Approximately $664,690 (Wife's estimate) to $1,500,000+ (Husband's estimate including insurance and higher business values)

### Contributions

  • The wife is the primary carer for the children and pays for school fees, clothing, and health insurance.

  • The husband previously operated a business valued at $20,772.

  • The wife manages D Pty Ltd and the Davids Trust, though her capacity is currently compromised by poor health.

### Future Needs

  • Husband claims a deficit of $3,770.53 per month despite a $109,829 salary.

  • Wife has significant health issues and is solely responsible for children's expenses.

  • Husband's employment is fixed-term until March 2021.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court adopted a conservative approach to ensure enough assets remain for a final 'claw-back' if the wife is found to be entitled to a higher percentage at trial.

### Orders

  • The parties to withdraw $40,000 from their joint ANZ account (015-056 299 991 557).

  • The $40,000 to be paid to the Trust Account of Jordan & Fowler for and on behalf of the husband as an interim property settlement.

### Reasoning

  • The court found it reasonable to provide $25,000 for legal fees to ensure the husband remains represented through the final hearing.

  • The court granted an additional $15,000 (totaling $40,000) to assist with the husband's financial position, despite concerns over his spending on a motorbike and motor vehicle finance.

  • A conservative figure was chosen to avoid prejudice to the wife's final entitlement.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's solicitors' willingness to continue representation if current fees were paid was a significant factor in granting litigation funding.

  • The court accepted the single expert's (Mr. E) valuation of $810,850 as a 'minimum value' for the wife's business for interim purposes.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 interim property settlement principles.

  • The court balanced the husband's immediate need for representation against the risk of over-distributing the pool before final orders.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The husband's valuation of the wife's business is still being reviewed by his own valuer.

  • The reasonableness of the husband's monthly expenses was not fully determined.

  • The legal status of the trauma insurance (whether it is property or a financial resource) remains a matter for the final hearing.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Husband claimed a need for $25,000 urgent lump sum maintenance due to a monthly deficit of $3,770.53.

### Capacity to Pay

  • The wife has access to business resources and insurance payouts, but claims her income from the business has ceased since February 2018.

### Income & Expenses

  • Husband: $109,829 income; expenses include motorbike and car finance.

  • Wife: $246,349 taxable income in 2018 (including trust distributions).

### Orders

  • The application for spousal maintenance was effectively dealt with via the interim property settlement order.

### Reasoning

  • The court preferred to characterize the payment as an interim property distribution rather than maintenance to allow for future adjustments.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's financial distress was partly self-inflicted through 'profligate' spending on luxury items like a motorbike.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 (duty to maintain) vs s 79 (property division).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of detailed evidence regarding the necessity of the husband's high monthly outgoings.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case clarifies the court's approach to interim property distributions when one party claims financial hardship despite a high income, particularly emphasizing the importance of maintaining legal representation."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the husband's ability to litigate (litigation funding) over his general claims for living expenses.

  • The treatment of trauma insurance as property vs. resource remains a significant 'battleground' for the final hearing.

  • The court will apply a 'conservative' filter to interim payments to prevent the 'claw-back' problem where a party spends their potential entitlement before final trial.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (interim property) and the court's discretion to ensure a fair trial through legal funding.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility regarding financial need was undermined by his 'profligate' spending on a motorbike and new car while claiming he could not pay legal fees.

  • The wife's claims regarding the decline of her business were noted but not fully tested, as the court relied on the single expert's minimum valuation for the interim stage.

  • The husband's refusal to accept the expert valuation while simultaneously relying on it for his application suggested a tactical inconsistency.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Interim property settlement of $40,000 to the husband from the joint ANZ account.

  • No order as to costs for the interim proceedings.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the husband's immediate liquidity crisis against the wife's concern that he was depleting the pool.

  • The $40,000 figure was a compromise: $25,000 specifically for legal fees to ensure the trial could proceed efficiently, and $15,000 for general relief.

  • The court declined to make a costs order because both parties were partially successful and the husband had significantly reduced his claim at the hearing.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation and married in 1994.

  • The parties separated in January 2015.

  • There are two children of the marriage, born in 2004 and 2007.

  • The husband is a tradesman with experience in large manufacturing; the wife managed financial oversight, IT, and stock control for the family business.

  • The family business, the Millet Group, has operations in Australia and Country J.

  • The parties have significant Division 7A (Div 7A) loan liabilities to the Australian Taxation Office.

  • The investment property at C Street, Suburb D was sold on 10 October 2018, yielding net proceeds of $490,100.12.

  • The husband transferred $192,000 from the Suburb D proceeds to Millet Pty Ltd to offset Div 7A loans prior to the hearing.

  • The property at Q Street, Suburb R is a family beach house held by L Pty Ltd as trustee for the P Trust.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Net proceeds from Suburb D property - $490,100.12

  • AGREED: Millet Group business interests (V Valuation) - $5,284,000

  • DISPUTED: Suburb R property valuation - $1,250,000 (Husband) vs $1,150,000 (Z Valuation)

  • DISPUTED: Husband's residence at Suburb F - Mortgage of $1,160,252

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not finalized, but business and real estate assets exceed $6,000,000

### Contributions

  • Husband managed manufacturing, design, sales, marketing, and employee management.

  • Wife provided financial oversight, account management, IT, website development, and stock control.

  • Husband owned a property in Suburb S at the commencement of cohabitation (purchased for $143,000 in 1993).

### Future Needs

  • The wife seeks to retain the Suburb O and Suburb R properties.

  • The husband argues the wife cannot afford to retain both properties freehold given the overall pool size.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court considered whether an interim sale of the Suburb R property was necessary to satisfy tax debts.

### Orders

  • Parties to sign documents to open a bank account in the name of B Pty Ltd.

  • Net proceeds of sale from Suburb D ($298,100.12 remaining) to be deposited into the B Pty Ltd account.

  • Injunction granted restraining parties from dealing with the Suburb D proceeds pending further order.

  • Husband's application for the interim sale of the Suburb R property is refused.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the remaining proceeds from the Suburb D sale ($298,100.12) plus the $192,000 already paid were sufficient to meet the 2018 Div 7A loan obligations ($464,575).

  • There was no immediate necessity to sell the Suburb R property before the 2019 financial year obligations became due.

  • Selling Suburb R prematurely would trigger a potential Capital Gains Tax (CGT) liability of approximately $583,136, which might be avoided if the wife retains the property in final orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The 2018 tax tranches were covered by the Suburb D sale, removing the 'compelling case' for an interim sale of the beach house.

  • The potential CGT impost of $583,136 was a significant factor in preserving the asset for final distribution.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act 1975 (Property settlement)

  • Division 7A Income Tax Assessment Act 1936 (Tax liabilities influencing cash flow requirements)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Disputed valuation of the Suburb R property ($1.15M vs $1.25M).

  • The net position of the total asset pool was not yet agreed between the parties.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2004, age 14)

  • Y (born 2007, age 11)
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the court's approach to interim property sales where significant corporate tax liabilities (Div 7A) exist. It establishes that the court will not order the sale of a major asset if immediate liabilities can be met by other liquidated funds, especially when a sale would trigger substantial CGT."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The availability of $490,100.12 from the Suburb D sale was the primary reason the court refused the sale of the $1.25M Suburb R property.

  • The court prioritized the preservation of assets for final hearing over the husband's desire to aggressively reduce business interest costs.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (Property division) and the commercial realities of Division 7A tax obligations.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court relied heavily on the financial evidence provided by experts Mr. U (Chartered Accountant) and Mr. V (Valuer).

  • There were no specific adverse credibility findings against either party, though the husband's shift in position regarding the sale of Suburb R (from wanting to retain it to wanting to sell it) was noted.

  • The wife's proactive step of lodging a caveat on the Suburb R property was a key procedural factor in protecting her claim to that asset.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The parties must cooperate to open a bank account for B Pty Ltd.

  • Net proceeds of the Suburb D property sale must be deposited into said account.

  • Injunctions are placed on the Suburb D proceeds.

  • The application for the sale of the Suburb R property is dismissed at this interim stage.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the husband's concern regarding business interest costs against the wife's desire to retain a specific asset (Suburb R).

  • The court determined that since the 2018 tax debt was covered, the parties should have the opportunity to mediate before further assets are liquidated.

  • The potential for the wife to retain Suburb R in a final settlement made an interim sale 'premature' and potentially prejudicial due to CGT.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties separated in January 2015 after 21 years of cohabitation.

  • There are two children of the marriage, aged 14 and 11.

  • The parties operated a business, Millet Pty Ltd (Millet Group), where the wife worked as the Administration Finance Manager until March 2018.

  • On 19 June 2018, consent orders were made for the husband to enable the wife to draw $1,000 per week from the business during an adjournment period.

  • The husband ceased payments around 23 November 2018, asserting he was no longer obligated as the wife had purportedly abandoned her interim claims.

  • The wife applied to reinstate the $1,000 per week payment as interim spousal maintenance.

  • The husband resisted, citing a personal financial deficit and the wife's failure to prove an inability to support herself.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property in Suburb D - Value not specified (Capital payments of $90,000 per annum)

  • AGREED: Real property in Suburb R - Value not specified (Capital payments of $41,000 per annum)

  • AGREED: Cash reserves and superannuation interests - Substantial (Value not specified)

  • DISPUTED: Division 7A loan accounts - Significant issue (Value not specified)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Described as 'substantial' with 'considerable value' but no total figure provided.

### Contributions

  • 21-year marriage with significant financial and non-financial contributions.

  • Wife served as Administration Finance Manager for the family business (Millet Pty Ltd), managing accounts, IT, and website design.

  • Wife has been the primary carer for the children since separation.

### Future Needs

  • Wife remains in the former matrimonial home with the children.

  • Wife has been out of the workforce (outside the family business) for many years.

  • Husband lives in rented accommodation.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted the pool is not yet crystallised but involves high-value assets and complex company loan structures.

### Reasoning

  • The property division was not the subject of final orders in this interim proceeding; however, the court acknowledged the substantial nature of the assets in determining the standard of living for maintenance purposes.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The existence of significant Division 7A loan issues influenced the court's decision to categorize payments as spousal maintenance rather than business drawings.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 factors were noted as context for the overall financial dispute.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the business or the total net asset pool was provided in this interim judgment.

  • The exact status of the Division 7A loan accounts remains a 'significant issue'.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2004, age 14)

  • Y (born 2007, age 11)

### Best Interests

  • The court maintained existing child support and school fee arrangements as being in the children's best interests.

### Orders

  • Husband to pay all tuition fees and fixed charges at AA School and BB School.

  • Husband to pay medical benefit premiums and non-covered medical/dental/orthodontic expenses.

  • Husband to pay child support as assessed (currently $2,441.67 per month).

### Reasoning

  • These orders were largely based on the continuation of consent orders made by Judge Cole on 19 June 2018.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The children's attendance at private schools (AA School and BB School) established a high baseline for their reasonable expenses.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117 of the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 regarding departure from administrative assessment.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The impact of the wife's potential employment on parenting arrangements was not fully explored.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Wife's total weekly expenditure is $3,442.

  • Wife's only income is $561 per week in child support (consumed by children's expenses) and the disputed $1,000 per week payment.

### Capacity to Pay

  • Millet Group forecast income for 2019 is $491,308.

  • Husband claimed a weekly deficit of $2,973, but the court noted this included 'notional capital payments' and significant personal expenditure.

### Income & Expenses

  • Wife's expenses include house repairs ($298), electricity ($200), clothing ($370), and gardening ($154).

  • Husband's expenditure is $5,598 per week against income of $2,625 per week (as per his accountant).

### Orders

  • Husband to pay the wife $1,000 per week by way of spousal maintenance.

  • Payments backdated to 23 November 2018.

  • Order to continue for a period of nine (9) calendar months.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the wife was unable to support herself adequately given the affluent standard of living established during the marriage.

  • The $1,000 figure was deemed a 'reasonable compromise' previously agreed upon by the parties.

  • The nine-month limit was imposed to encourage the wife to explore her earning capacity and seek employment.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court rejected the husband's argument that the wife 'abandoned' her claim, finding instead an informal agreement to continue payments.

  • The husband's claimed financial deficit was undermined by the inclusion of capital payments for properties he would likely retain or benefit from in the final division.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72(1) 'adequately' support herself: The court applied the McCrossen principle that 'adequately' refers to a reasonable standard of living, not a subsistence level.

  • s 75(2)(o): The 'justice of the case' required honoring the informal arrangement.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Wife failed to provide evidence of her efforts to find employment or specific impediments to working.

  • Lack of clarity on whether the $1,000 payments were drawings against equity or maintenance from income.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case reinforces the principle that interim spousal maintenance is determined by the 'adequacy' of support relative to the parties' established standard of living, rather than a mere subsistence level. It also highlights the court's willingness to enforce informal financial arrangements made during litigation."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court's reliance on the transcript of a previous hearing to confirm that the husband's counsel had acknowledged an informal agreement regarding the $1,000 payments.

  • The determination that capital payments on real estate should not be used to artificially deflate a party's capacity to pay maintenance.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 72 (liability to maintain) and s 75(2) (factors), specifically the standard of living (s 75(2)(g)) and the effect of the marriage on earning capacity.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility was slightly impacted by his counsel's 'incorrect' submission that the wife had abandoned her claim, which was contradicted by the court transcript.

  • The wife's failure to address her earning capacity or job-seeking efforts in her affidavit presented a risk to her claim for long-term maintenance, resulting in the nine-month sunset clause.

  • The husband's financial disclosure was viewed through the lens of his control over the Millet Group, with the court looking past 'notional' deficits to his actual ability to fund the payments.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The husband do pay to the wife by way of spousal maintenance the sum of $1,000 per week backdated and effective as and from 23 November 2018 for a period of nine (9) calendar months.

  • The costs of the wife are reserved to the final determination of the proceedings.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the wife's immediate financial need against her obligation to eventually return to the workforce, leading to a time-limited order.

  • The court found that the husband's unilateral cessation of payments was inconsistent with the parties' prior conduct and the spirit of the ongoing litigation.

  • The 'affluent' lifestyle of the parties was a primary factor in determining that $1,000 per week was a reasonable sum for interim support.

  • The court utilized the 'gateway' approach from Hall & Hall to establish the wife's inability to support herself adequately before determining the husband's capacity to pay.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are involved in ongoing parenting litigation regarding four children: B, Z, X, and Y.

  • On 23 August 2018, Senior Registrar Spink made orders requiring the mother to notify the father of changes to B and Z's living arrangements and medical circumstances.

  • The August 2018 orders also provided for the father to have supervised time with X and Y at P Group contact centre.

  • On 24 October 2018, the father filed a Contravention Application alleging two counts of breach by the mother.

  • Count 1 alleged the mother moved child B into her residence on 24 September 2018 without notifying the father or the Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) for 10 days.

  • Count 2 alleged the mother unreasonably withheld consent for activities at the contact centre, leading P Group to withdraw its services on 17 October 2018.

  • The mother applied for summary dismissal of the contravention application under Rule 10.12 of the Family Law Rules 2004.

  • The father appeared via telephone for the hearing on 27 November 2018.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Expert Evidence

  • Correspondence from Ms S of P Group (Exhibit 2) suggested the parties use an alternative service provider because the centre could not monitor certain activities.

### Best Interests

  • The court focused on the legal threshold for contravention rather than a broad best interests assessment in this specific ex tempore judgment.

### Orders

  • The Application – Contravention filed by Mr Myron on 24 October 2018 be dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the father's evidence regarding Count 1 was vague and failed to establish a prima facie case of a 'change in living arrangements' or the timing thereof.

  • Regarding Count 2, the court refused to infer that the mother's conduct caused the contact centre to withdraw services, noting the centre's email cited its own inability to monitor activities.

  • The court emphasized that contravention applications are quasi-criminal and require persuasive evidence.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's affidavit evidence was insufficient to establish when or if a specific change in living arrangements occurred for child B.

  • The correspondence from P Group did not explicitly attribute the withdrawal of services to the mother's lack of cooperation.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 10.12(d) (no reasonable likelihood of success).

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 7.03 (special service requirements for contravention applications).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of specific dates or details regarding the alleged change in B's residence.

  • Incomplete email from P Group (Exhibit 2) which did not support the father's claim of the mother's interference.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case reinforces the high evidentiary burden placed on applicants in contravention proceedings due to their quasi-criminal nature.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • A prima facie case must be established on the face of the evidence before a respondent is required to answer a contravention charge.

  • Inferences of bad faith or non-compliance cannot be drawn from ambiguous third-party correspondence (e.g., contact centre emails).

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 Rule 10.12 regarding summary orders and dismissal of applications with no reasonable likelihood of success.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's evidence was found to be inadequate and vague, particularly regarding the timing and nature of the alleged breaches.

  • The father's absence from the physical courtroom (appearing by telephone) and the incomplete nature of his tendered exhibits (Exhibit 2) hindered his ability to establish a prima facie case.

  • The mother's legal position was preferred as she successfully argued that the father's application failed to meet the basic threshold for a contravention hearing.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Application – Contravention filed by Mr Myron on 24 October 2018 be dismissed.

  • Costs in relation to the applications filed by Mr Myron on 24 and 25 October 2018 and listed for hearing today be reserved to trial.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied a strict standard to the father's affidavit evidence, finding it lacked the necessary specificity to prove a breach of the August 2018 orders.

  • The court determined that the father's reliance on an incomplete email from a service provider was insufficient to prove the mother 'unreasonably prevented' contact.

  • The decision to reserve costs to trial suggests that while the contravention application was dismissed, the court was not yet prepared to make a final determination on the financial consequences of the failed application.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Ms Baxter and Mr Baxter married in New Zealand in 2000.

  • The child, B, was born in 2006 and is currently 12 years of age.

  • Ms Baxter alleges severe family violence by Mr Baxter against her and the child throughout the relationship.

  • The parties divorced on 21 April 2008.

  • The child has not had contact with Mr Baxter since December 2006, when she was less than one year old.

  • Mr Baxter left Australia in 2009 without providing a forwarding address, despite a Departure Prohibition Order.

  • Mr Baxter owes $55,745.14 in unpaid child support as of 19 September 2016.

  • Ms Baxter met Mr Church in early 2014; they began living together in April 2014 and married on 12 July 2014.

  • On 28 November 2016, Judge Howard of the Federal Circuit Court granted Ms Baxter sole parental responsibility.

  • The applicants seek leave under s 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 to commence adoption proceedings in the State court.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, born 2006 (12 years old at the time of judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of severe family violence by the biological father prior to 2007.

### Expert Evidence

  • No external family consultant report mentioned, but the court relied on the affidavit evidence of the Mother and Stepfather.

### Best Interests

  • The court found it is in the child's best interests to formalize the psychological bond with the stepfather, providing legal security and emotional stability.

### Orders

  • Leave granted pursuant to s 60G of the Family Law Act for Mr Church and Ms Baxter to apply for adoption under the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld).

  • Service of the application and supporting material upon Mr Baxter is dispensed with under Rule 7.18.

### Reasoning

  • The judge determined that the biological father has shown a total lack of involvement for a decade.

  • The court noted the child's strong identification with Mr Church as her father and her struggle with him not being 'officially' her parent in social contexts.

  • The court emphasized that adoption would provide the child with a legal family structure in the event of the mother's death, given her only other relative is in Israel.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The biological father's 11-year absence and failure to pay $55,745.14 in child support demonstrated a complete abdication of parental responsibility.

  • Mr Church's proactive involvement, such as relocating his office to be near the child's school, evidenced a deep paternal commitment.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60G(1) and (2) regarding the grant of leave for adoption.

  • s 60CC best interests considerations, specifically the child's views and the nature of the relationship with the stepfather.

  • s 61E and s 65J regarding the termination of existing parental responsibility and parenting orders upon adoption.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The exact current whereabouts of Mr Baxter remain unknown despite CSA efforts.

  • Attempts to serve Mr Baxter via a defunct Facebook page were unsuccessful.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Severe family violence alleged by Ms Baxter against herself and the child during the marriage (pre-2008).

### Child Exposure

  • The child was allegedly exposed to violence in infancy (prior to December 2006).
