### Reasoning

  • The history of violence contributed to the court's willingness to dispense with service and support the severance of the biological father's legal ties.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court accepted the mother's evidence regarding the history of violence as part of the context for the father's long-term absence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) the need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Historical allegations of violence were not contested due to the father's absence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case illustrates the high threshold for granting leave under s 60G, which effectively facilitates the total legal severance of a biological parent's rights in favor of a stepparent. It highlights that long-term abandonment and significant child support arrears are heavy factors in determining the child's best interests."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the 'psychological parenthood' of the stepfather over the biological tie of an absent father.

  • The child's own mature desire to be 'officially' part of the family was a decisive factor under s 60CC(3)(a).

  • The biological father's evasion of the Child Support Agency and the court system justified the dispensation of service.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60G of the federal Family Law Act and the state-based Adoption Act 2009 (Qld).

  • The application of Rule 7.18 of the Family Law Rules to dispense with service in cases of total parental disappearance.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court found Ms Baxter to be a credible witness, accepting her evidence regarding the father's absence and the history of violence.

  • Mr Baxter's credibility was implicitly undermined by his history of non-compliance with child support obligations and his evasion of the Departure Prohibition Order.

  • The court noted that the applicants had exhausted reasonable efforts to locate the respondent, including checking old social media accounts and CSA records.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Service of the Application and supporting material upon the respondent is dispensed with pursuant to Rule 7.18.

  • Leave is granted pursuant to s 60G of the Family Law Act for the Applicants to make an application under the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld) for the adoption of the child B by Mr Church.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was centered on the child's emotional and legal security. The court found that the biological father had effectively 'opted out' of the child's life for over a decade.

  • The court balanced the extreme step of adoption (which ends biological parental responsibility) against the reality of the child's daily life, where Mr Church was the only father figure she knew.

  • The 'good news story' nature of the stepfather's commitment was contrasted against the biological father's history of violence and financial neglect.

  • Dispensation of service was granted because the father had intentionally made himself unfindable, and requiring further search efforts would cause unnecessary delay to the child's stability.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property orders were made on 18 January 2017.

  • The wife brought an application for the enforcement of the 18 January 2017 property orders.

  • The husband indicated an intention to file an application under s 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 to set aside or vary those property orders.

  • The wife filed an interlocutory application for the summary dismissal of the husband's putative s 79A application.

  • On 2 November 2018, the husband appeared with counsel and sought an adjournment of the summary dismissal hearing, which was refused.

  • The court ordered the husband to file written submissions in support of his position by 16 November 2018.

  • The husband failed to file any written submissions by the deadline.

  • On 20 November 2018, the husband's solicitors notified the court that he did not intend to prosecute the s 79A application.

  • The wife sought indemnity costs against the husband from 5 October 2018, the date the husband was originally meant to file his amended application.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Matrimonial assets subject to final orders dated 18 January 2017 - Value not specified

  • DISPUTED: Property in Israel held by the husband - Value and rental income undisclosed

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not specified in this costs judgment

### Future Needs

  • The wife had only recently commenced employment at the time of the costs hearing.

  • The husband had maintained control of all assets subsequent to the 18 January 2017 orders.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court previously determined that neither party was impecunious, though the wife lacked access to her entitled funds.

### Reasoning

  • The husband's conduct in flagging a s 79A application without following through caused the wife to incur 'thrown away' costs.

  • The husband's failure to provide details regarding his property in Israel was noted as a factor in assessing the parties' respective financial positions under s 117(2A)(a).

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband had control of the assets post-judgment, while the wife had limited access to funds.

  • The husband's failure to disclose rental income or details of the Israel property impacted the court's view of his financial conduct.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A (Setting aside property orders)

  • s 117(2A)(a) (Financial circumstances of the parties)

  • s 117(2A)(c) (Conduct of the parties in the proceedings)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No valuation or rental income data provided for the husband's property in Israel.

  • No copy of the wife's costs agreement was tendered to support the indemnity costs claim.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case clarifies the application of s 117(2A) costs orders when a party initiates but fails to prosecute a substantive application (s 79A), leading to wasted legal expenses for the respondent.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's decision to abandon the s 79A application only after the wife had prepared for a summary dismissal hearing constituted conduct justifying a costs order.

  • Indemnity costs were refused because the circumstances, while 'frustrating' and 'unusual', did not meet the 'exceptional' threshold required by the Family Law Rules 2004.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117(1): General rule that parties bear their own costs.

  • Section 117(2): Court's discretion to make costs orders if there are justifying circumstances.

  • Section 117(2A): Mandatory considerations for costs, specifically conduct (c) and financial position (a).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility was undermined by his failure to disclose details regarding his property in Israel and rental income.

  • The husband's litigation conduct was characterized as 'obfuscation', particularly his last-minute withdrawal of the application after seeking an adjournment.

  • The court preferred the wife's account of the procedural history, noting she was forced to answer arguments that the husband never actually filed.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The husband pay the wife’s costs associated with the application for summary dismissal as and from 5 October 2018 by agreement, and in default of agreement, as assessed.

  • The respective applications by way of written submission filed on 30 November and 7 December 2018 are otherwise dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • Justice Cronin found that the husband's 'obfuscation' and failure to file ordered submissions necessitated a departure from the usual rule that each party bears their own costs.

  • The court balanced the husband's right to seek an adjournment against the wife's right to finality and the unnecessary expense she incurred defending a 'putative' application.

  • The judge rejected the husband's argument that conduct prior to 5 October 2018 was irrelevant, noting it 'set the scene' for the subsequent wasted costs.

  • The refusal of indemnity costs was based on the lack of a tendered costs agreement and the finding that the husband's conduct did not reach the level of 'exceptional' misconduct.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The wife filed an application in a case on 21 August 2018, which was effectively an application for summary dismissal.

  • On 24 September 2018, a Registrar informed the wife that her application was unlikely to succeed and reserved the issue of costs.

  • The husband was ordered to file a response and affidavit by 22 October 2018 in preparation for the hearing.

  • The wife did not take further steps to withdraw or amend her application following the Registrar's comments until the morning of the hearing.

  • On 4 December 2018, the wife sought leave to withdraw her application.

  • The husband applied for indemnity costs for the legal work performed in responding to the withdrawn application.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Total assets in the pending property dispute - Over $4,000,000

  • TOTAL NET POOL: $4,000,000+

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted that with over $4 million in assets, both parties are financially secure enough to manage the costs order.

### Reasoning

  • The property division itself was not determined in this judgment; however, the size of the pool was a factor in assessing the parties' financial circumstances under s 117(2A)(a).

### Pivotal Findings

  • The existence of a $4 million asset pool meant that a costs order of $1,500 would not cause financial hardship to the wife.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117(2A)(a) financial circumstances of the parties

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The specific composition of the $4 million pool was not detailed as the property matter was pending.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['The judgment reinforces the principle that while parties generally bear their own costs, a failure to heed court warnings or withdrawing an application at the last minute constitutes conduct justifying a costs order.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's decision to proceed with a summary dismissal application despite being told by a Registrar it would not succeed was a key driver for the costs order.

  • The husband's legal team spent approximately six hours of work that was rendered 'thrown away' by the late withdrawal.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding the general rule and exceptions for costs.

  • Section 117(2A) factors, specifically conduct (c) and success (e).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility as a litigant was undermined by her failure to seek legal advice earlier and her disregard for the Registrar's procedural guidance.

  • The wife was deemed 'wholly unsuccessful' in the context of the specific application withdrawn.

  • The court noted the husband's lawyers' time estimates were undisputed, supporting the quantum of the costs order.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The applicant has leave to withdraw her application in a case filed on 21 August 2018.

  • The response to an application in a case filed on 22 October 2018 is struck out.

  • Paragraph (3) of the Orders made on 24 September 2018 is discharged.

  • The Applicant wife pay the Respondent husband’s costs thrown away this day, fixed in the sum of $1,500 and payable within 90 days.

  • The reasons this day be transcribed and placed on the Court file.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge determined that the wife's conduct as a litigant justified a departure from s 117(1) because she forced the husband to incur costs for a hopeless application.

  • Indemnity costs were refused because the circumstances were not 'exceptional' as required by the authority in Prantage & Prantage [2013] FamCAFC 105.

  • The court rejected the argument that indemnity costs should be awarded simply because practitioners charge above the court scale.

  • The costs were fixed at $1,500, representing approximately six hours of work at the scale rate set out in the Family Law Rules 2004.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties have three children: X (born 2009), Y (born 2011), and Z (born 2012).

  • The children live with the mother, and time with the father has been disrupted by allegations of sexual impropriety.

  • The trial commenced and ran for five days but was adjourned to 4 February 2019 due to judicial unavailability.

  • Two specific allegations were raised: the father exposing himself while urinating in June 2018, and the father tickling child X on her vagina and bottom over her clothing during a movie in July 2018.

  • Child X was interviewed by a senior constable of police on 9 July 2018 regarding the tickling incident.

  • The father denied all allegations during cross-examination.

  • Dr. F assessed the father and found him to be of 'low risk'.

  • The Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) largely supported the father's position for unsupervised time.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (9 years old)

  • Y (7 years old)

  • Z (6 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of sexual impropriety (exposure and inappropriate touching/tickling).

  • Allegations of a 'strident nature' against paternal grandparents.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. F assessed the father as 'low risk'.

  • Ms. E (Family Report writer) to provide an updated report following the interim period.

### Best Interests

  • Court determined it is in the children's best interests to commence an extended relationship period with the father to observe progress.

### Orders

  • Orders of 6 June 2018 discharged.

  • Father to spend unsupervised time with children on specific dates: 8, 15, 16, 22, 29 December 2018; 5, 12, 15, 19, 26, 29 January 2019 from 9:00 am to 7:00 pm.

  • Christmas Day time from 3:00 pm to 8:00 pm.

  • Handover at Suburb B Centre (except Christmas Day at Suburb C and Suburb D Park).

  • Ms. E to undertake an updated family report.

  • Trial adjourned to 4 February 2019.

### Reasoning

  • The court found no 'unacceptable risk' of harm requiring ongoing supervision based on Dr. F's assessment and the father's performance in cross-examination.

  • The court noted the father's partner's support and the fact that the alleged incident occurred in a house full of people, making such behavior unlikely.

  • Overnight time was refused at this interim stage due to a lack of evidence on how the children would handle the transition.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's willingness to undergo close scrutiny in cross-examination bolstered his credibility.

  • Dr. F's 'low risk' assessment was a significant factor in removing the supervision requirement.

  • The court found the incidents described by the children could potentially be 'innocuous' despite the father's total denial.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC: Best interests of the child.

  • s 69ZT: Rules of evidence in children's cases.

  • s 65DA(2) and s 62B: Particulars of obligations and consequences of contravention.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of evidence regarding how the children would cope with overnight stays.

  • Unresolved question as to why the children made the specific allegations if they were not true.

  • Maternal grandparents yet to give evidence.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged sexual impropriety by the father toward the children.

### Child Exposure

  • Alleged exposure of genitals while urinating.

### Orders

  • Handover to occur at a neutral centre (Suburb B Centre).

### Reasoning

  • The court balanced the risk of harm against the benefit of the children having a relationship with their father, finding the risk was not 'unacceptable' at this stage.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court cautioned the mother against 'fanning the fire' of the children's beliefs regarding abuse without a proper basis.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b): The need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm from being subjected to, or exposed to, abuse, neglect or family violence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting accounts between the children's reports to police/experts and the father's denials.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case highlights the court's approach to 'unacceptable risk' in interim proceedings where serious allegations of sexual impropriety are made but not yet fully proven or disproven."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the resumption of the father-child relationship over continued supervision, relying heavily on an expert's 'low risk' assessment and the father's credibility during cross-examination.

  • The decision emphasizes that 'low risk' does not mean 'no risk', but it may be sufficient to move from supervised to unsupervised time if other protective factors (like the presence of a partner or neutral handovers) are in place.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC (best interests) and the evidentiary standards for interim hearings under s 69ZT.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father was found to be a credible witness who subjected himself to rigorous cross-examination.

  • The mother's mental health was flagged as a potential issue to be explored in expert evidence, which might impact the weight given to her reporting of the children's statements.

  • The children's credibility is not directly questioned, but the 'why' behind their statements remains a primary battleground for the remainder of the trial.

  • There is a risk that the mother may inadvertently 'fan the fire' of the children's allegations, which the court identified as a concern for future reporting to authorities.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Interim parenting orders for unsupervised daytime contact on specified dates.

  • Discharge of previous orders from 6 June 2018.

  • Adjournment of final orders application to 4 February 2019.

  • Requirement for parties to file further affidavits by 24 January 2019.

  • Updated family report by Ms. E.

  • Costs of the report to be shared equally by the parents.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to use the adjournment period as a trial phase for unsupervised contact to provide the court and experts with real-world data on the children's reactions.

  • The court balanced the mother's concerns and the children's statements against the father's consistent denials and the lack of corroborating evidence of a pattern of abuse.

  • The refusal of overnight time represents a cautious middle ground, ensuring 'quality over quantity' while maintaining a safety margin until the final expert evidence is heard.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The child, X, was born in 2016.

  • In August 2017, the child suffered serious physical injuries while in the mother's primary care, including a 1mm depressed mildly comminuted fracture to the parietal bone of the skull and an un-displaced left distal radius mid-shaft fracture.

  • The child also presented with extensive bruising to the left cheek, right ear, back, right arm, and leg, as well as a bleeding right nostril.

  • The mother and her then-partner, Mr. B, were charged with cruelty to a child and five counts of assault occasioning grievous bodily harm.

  • The Department of Child Safety removed the child on 31 August 2017 and placed her in foster care.

  • In December 2017, the Department placed the child in the care of the father, who was then living in Darwin.

  • The father moved the child from Queensland to the Northern Territory in June 2018 without seeking formal Departmental approval, though no court order restricted his movement at that time.

  • The mother sought a recovery order to return the child to Queensland to live with her or the maternal grandfather.

  • The maternal grandfather, Mr. Bartlett, intervened seeking that the child live with him in Queensland on an interim basis.

  • The mother's criminal charges were upgraded on 28 September 2018, making her own application for residence unfeasible at the interim stage.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born in 2016 (approximately 2 years old at the time of the 2018 judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • Serious non-accidental physical injuries sustained by the child while in the mother's care.

  • Pending criminal charges against the mother for assault occasioning grievous bodily harm.

  • Allegations of cannabis use by both parents.

  • Historical domestic violence order (2016-2018) against the father by the mother.

### Expert Evidence

  • Ms. C (Departmental Officer): Provided evidence regarding the severity of injuries and supported the child remaining with the father.

  • Dr. D (Senior Registrar): Assessed the child's injuries as likely non-accidental.

  • Ms. F (Family Consultant): Prepared a report dated 5 October 2018; however, the court noted limitations as she had not met the father or seen the child with either parent.

  • Territory Families: Conducted a home visit in July 2018 which raised no concerns regarding the father's care.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that stability in the father's care outweighed the benefit of proximity to the maternal family, given the unresolved safety risks associated with the mother.

### Orders

  • The mother’s application for a Recovery Order is dismissed.

  • The child shall live with the father in the Northern Territory.

  • The child shall spend time and communicate with the mother via FaceTime or other electronic means.

  • The mother is to provide a minute of order by 7 December 2018 identifying proposed physical supervised time in the Northern Territory.

  • The Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) to engage the father regarding supervised time facilitation in Darwin.

  • The father granted leave to appear by telephone for future hearings.

### Reasoning

  • The court applied the 'Goode & Goode' framework for interim proceedings, prioritizing the child's safety under s 60CC(2)(b) over the benefit of a meaningful relationship under s 60CC(2)(a).

  • The severity of the child's injuries (skull and arm fractures) created a 'bold' risk factor that necessitated supervised contact only.

  • The father's relocation to the Northern Territory was not legally restricted at the time it occurred, and disrupting the child's current stable environment was not in her best interests.

  • The court noted that while the mother was the primary carer historically, the pending criminal charges made her an unsuitable residence candidate on an interim basis.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The medical evidence of a skull fracture and mid-shaft radius fracture was a primary driver for finding a risk of harm.

  • The father's immediate travel from Darwin to Queensland when the child was first removed showed a high level of commitment and insight.

  • The lack of evidence of breaches regarding the 2016 domestic violence order mitigated concerns against the father.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 60CC(2)(b): Protection from physical or psychological harm given 'greater weight' than the benefit of a meaningful relationship.

  • Section 60CC(2)(a): Benefit of a meaningful relationship with both parents.

  • Section 67Q: Considerations for Recovery Orders.

  • Constitutional right of movement: Influenced the court's reluctance to use coercive powers to force the father to return to Queensland (citing Sampson & Hartnett).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The medical evidence and mother's explanations for the injuries have not yet been tested via cross-examination.

  • The Family Consultant (Ms. F) had not observed the child with either parent.

  • Conflicting evidence regarding the consistency and quality of telephone contact between the child and the maternal family.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged physical assault of the child resulting in grievous bodily harm (August 2017).

  • Historical domestic violence between the mother and father (2016).

  • Domestic violence order by the mother against her former partner, Mr. B.

### Protection Orders

  • Domestic violence order against the father (expired 2018).

  • Domestic violence order against Mr. B (mother's former partner).

### Child Exposure

  • The child was the direct victim of the alleged violence/assault.

### Orders

  • Mother's time with the child to be supervised by an independent person or commercial organization in the Northern Territory.

### Reasoning

  • The court accepted that the unresolved criminal charges regarding the child's injuries created an unacceptable risk for unsupervised time.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found a 'potential risk' to the child in the mother's unsupervised care due to the nature of the criminal charges.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 4AB: Definition of family violence (applied to the child's injuries).

  • Section 60CG: Court's duty to ensure parenting orders are consistent with safety.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The criminal trial is not expected until mid-to-late 2019, leaving the factual basis of the violence allegations legally undetermined in the interim.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case underscores the primacy of the 'protection from harm' principle in interim parenting disputes where a parent faces serious criminal charges related to the child's physical safety."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the stability of the current care arrangement (father in NT) over the historical primary care (mother in QLD) due to the 'bold' risk of harm presented by the mother's pending criminal charges.

  • The court affirmed that while it has the power to order a parent's return to a jurisdiction, such coercive power should be used sparingly, especially when the parent's move was not in breach of any existing orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) and s 60CC(2)(b), where the latter was given overriding weight due to the severity of the child's injuries.

  • Application of the 'Goode & Goode' principles for interim hearings, emphasizing that the court cannot make final findings of fact on disputed evidence but must manage risk.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's credibility was slightly at risk due to his non-appearance at hearings, though his excuse regarding a cancelled Tiger Airways flight was noted.

  • The mother's credibility regarding the child's injuries is a central issue in the pending criminal proceedings, with the Department noting she provided three different versions of the events.

  • The Family Consultant's report was given limited weight because the consultant had not observed the child with the father, leading to potentially skewed inferences about the father's facilitation of contact.

  • The court found the maternal grandfather to be a 'caring and loving' witness but determined his proposal was a 'temporary' fix that did not address the long-term stability provided by the father.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Recovery order dismissed.

  • Child to live with the father in the Northern Territory.

  • Department of Child Safety, Youth and Women discharged as a party.

  • Mother to provide a minute of order regarding supervised time in Darwin.

  • ICL to facilitate discussions with the father regarding supervised time.

  • Proceedings adjourned for Case Management Hearing on 19 December 2018.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was centered on the child's need for stability and safety. Since the child had been with the father for nearly a year and was progressing well, there was no compelling reason to disrupt that stability to move the child to a grandfather in Queensland, particularly when the mother (the historical primary carer) could not safely care for the child.

  • The court balanced the mother's right to a relationship with the child against the child's right to be protected from the risk of further non-accidental injury.

  • The decision to dismiss the recovery order was based on the fact that the father's relocation to the Northern Territory was lawful at the time and provided a stable environment with extended family support, which the father lacked in Queensland.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The wife initiated property proceedings on 9 August 2018.

  • Parenting orders were previously made by a Registrar on 12 September 2018 by consent.

  • The husband has failed to attend three consecutive court dates: 12 September 2018, 24 October 2018, and 5 December 2018.

  • The husband has failed to file any responding material or financial disclosure.

  • Traditional service via email failed as the husband's account appears closed; communication is currently limited to text messages.

  • The husband is believed to be residing in Western Australia.

  • The wife is the sole legal owner of the primary real estate asset in country Victoria.

  • The parties invested approximately $250,000 into a business in 2017, which the wife asserts is now lost.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Real property in country Victoria - Value described as 'modest' and 'small' (Wife is sole legal owner).

  • DISPUTED: Business investment (2017) - $250,000 (Asserted as lost/nil value).

  • DISPUTED: Husband's Superannuation - Value unknown/unclear.

  • DISPUTED: Truck and Motorbike - Allegedly acquired by husband using joint funds; values unknown.

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not yet determined due to lack of disclosure.

### Contributions

  • The wife asserts sole legal ownership of the country Victoria property.

  • The parties made a joint financial contribution of $250,000 to a failed business in 2017.

### Future Needs

  • Not specifically detailed, though the court noted the wife's need for finality given the husband's lack of participation.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court questioned the necessity of property orders if the husband makes no claim for equitable relief against the wife's sole legal title.

### Reasoning

  • The judge observed that because the wife is the sole legal owner of the real property, she may not strictly require orders unless the husband asserts an equitable interest.

  • The court allowed the wife to seek an undefended hearing due to the husband's persistent failure to file material or attend court.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's failure to attend three court dates (12 September, 24 October, and 5 December 2018) established a basis for an undefended hearing.

  • The $250,000 business loss in 2017 was identified as a significant historical financial event.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79: Alteration of property interests (threshold for proceeding undefended).

  • s 117: Justification for cost orders due to non-disclosure and failure to attend.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the country Victoria property.

  • No evidence regarding the current status or value of the $250,000 business investment.

  • Complete lack of superannuation and asset disclosure from the husband.

  • Unverified values for the truck and motorbike allegedly purchased with joint funds.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Not specified in the text.

### Safety Concerns

  • Not explicitly raised in this procedural judgment.

### Expert Evidence

  • None mentioned.

### Best Interests

  • The court noted the existing consent orders are not being implemented, implying a lack of stability for the children.

### Reasoning

  • The judge noted the parenting orders were made by consent but are not being followed, though the primary focus of this hearing was the property application.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's non-compliance with the 12 September 2018 consent orders suggests a breakdown in the agreed parenting framework.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 61DA: Presumption of equal shared parental responsibility (applied in the prior consent orders).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No evidence explaining why the consent orders are being breached.

  • Lack of information regarding the children's current living situation or welfare.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case highlights the court's procedural flexibility regarding service and the threshold for allowing a matter to proceed on an undefended basis when a party persistently fails to engage."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's repeated failure to attend court or file material despite being aware of proceedings via text message justified a move toward an undefended hearing.

  • The court's willingness to accept service via text message (SMS) reflects a pragmatic approach to non-responsive parties in the digital age.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 regarding service and procedural fairness.

  • s 117(2) regarding the court's discretion to award costs where a party's conduct (non-attendance/non-disclosure) justifies a departure from the standard rule that each party pays their own costs.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility is severely compromised by his failure to attend three court dates and his failure to provide any financial disclosure.

  • The court noted the husband is 'well-aware' of the proceedings due to ongoing text communication, suggesting his absence is a deliberate choice rather than a lack of notice.

  • The wife's evidence was accepted for the purposes of the procedural orders, though the court noted she still bears the burden of proving the husband's knowledge of specific dates to secure the reserved costs.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All extant applications adjourned to the Judicial Duty List on 18 March 2019.

  • Service on the respondent permitted via electronic text message to his mobile number.

  • Wife granted leave to file and serve an Amended Initiating Application and Affidavit by 1 March 2019.

  • Wife granted leave to proceed with her application for alteration of property interests on an undefended basis on the return date, subject to the trial judge.

  • Wife's costs fixed at $1,330, reserved to the return date pending proof of the husband's knowledge of the hearings on 24 October 2018 and 5 December 2018.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to prevent the husband's non-participation from indefinitely stalling the wife's legal rights to finalize property matters.

  • The court balanced the need for procedural fairness (ensuring the husband is aware of the hearing) with the wife's right to progress her application, opting for substituted service via text message.

  • The cost order of $1,330 was intended to compensate the wife for 'wasted' hearings caused by the husband's failure to fulfill his obligations to the court.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in March 2005, married in 2005, and separated in January 2014.

  • A divorce order was granted on 29 April 2015.

  • Final parenting orders were made by consent in the Federal Circuit Court on 13 October 2015.

  • The litigation history is extensive, involving 15 to 20 hearings and over 170 filed documents within three years.

  • The Mother is a law student and former health professional; the Father is a consultant heading the Australian arm of a US-based company.

  • The Mother seeks to relocate with the children to the United States of America.

  • The current proceedings involve cross-applications for contravention of parenting orders, stay of previous orders, and an application to declare the Father a vexatious litigant.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Arrears for medical, dental, and health fees - $527.16

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not specified (proceedings focused on parenting and enforcement)

### Contributions

  • Not addressed in this interim/contravention judgment.

### Future Needs

  • Not addressed in this interim/contravention judgment.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it was not just to proceed with the Mother's claim for $527.16 due to a lack of specific quantum and evidence of offsetting debts.

### Orders

  • The Mother's application to pursue payment of $527.16 for medical/dental fees is struck out due to insufficient evidence and lack of specific quantum.

### Reasoning

  • The Mother failed to establish the precise quantum of the claim, and the Father asserted the debt was offset by monies owed to him by the Mother.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court determined that the financial dispute regarding health fees was too poorly pleaded to be enforceable in the current application.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 (not directly applied), procedural rules regarding the striking out of inadequately pleaded claims.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No specific quantum pleaded for the total arrears.

  • Lack of receipts or clear accounting of payments made versus payments owed.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Two children, aged 8 and 10 at the time of the 2018 hearing.

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleged distress to children caused by Father sending Uber drivers ('strange men') to collect them from school.

### Expert Evidence

  • A previous family report was prepared by Ms N in October 2015.

  • Mother's request for a new family report was dismissed as the trial was not imminent.

### Best Interests

  • The court emphasized that the 'white noise' of constant litigation was contrary to the children's best interests.

### Orders

  • Mother to sign a bond under s 70NEC of the Family Law Act by 14 December 2018 regarding her contraventions.

  • Stay of Bennett J's orders (paragraphs 7, 8, and 20) made on 31 July 2018 pending appeal.

  • Mother's application for a family report is dismissed.

  • Father's breach regarding school notification is found proved but dismissed as trifling/excused.

  • All extant applications (except the stay) transferred to the Federal Circuit Court at Melbourne.

### Reasoning

  • The Mother's excuse that children were 'having a bath' during scheduled Skype calls was rejected; she had a positive obligation to ensure communication occurred.

  • The Mother's contraventions were 'less serious' but required a bond to ensure future compliance (the 'Sword of Damocles' approach).

  • The Father's failure to notify the school was an oversight and excused under s 70NAE.

  • A stay of Bennett J's orders was granted because the balance of convenience favored waiting for the appeal outcome, particularly regarding the 'advanced' parenting course which seemed redundant.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Mother was found to be present during July 2018 communication attempts despite her denials.

  • Mother's obsession with litigation was found to cloud her objectivity and the children's needs.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 70NAC (definition of contravention)

  • s 70NEC (orders for bonds)

  • s 70NAE (reasonable excuse)

  • s 70NBA (power to vary orders in contravention proceedings)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No affidavit or proof of evidence from the school regarding the Father's alleged 'delinquency' in collections.

  • Mother failed to serve annexures to her affidavit on the Father in a timely manner, necessitating an adjournment.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Orders

  • No maintenance orders made.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother referenced past family violence proceedings to the school, which Bennett J previously found was intended to 'embarrass the father'.

### Child Exposure

  • Children exposed to high-level parental conflict and 'toxic' dysfunction.

### Orders

  • Stay of the injunction (Order 8 of Bennett J) which restrained the Mother from denigrating the Father to the school.

### Reasoning

  • The court stayed the injunction pending appeal as the Father could manage the issue himself if the Mother repeated the behavior.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found the Mother used allegations of family violence as a tool for denigration rather than a legitimate safety concern in the context of school communications.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) (protection from harm - considered but found not to justify Mother's breaches).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No evidence provided to support the Mother's claim that the school principal was 'frantic' about the Father's collection methods.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case highlights the court's struggle with high-conflict, self-represented litigants who use contravention applications as a tactical weapon, thereby delaying substantive relief and wasting judicial resources."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court identified a 'white noise' of litigation (97 grounds of appeal, 50 contravention allegations) that masks any legitimate issues.

  • The use of s 70NEC bonds is affirmed as a necessary 'Sword of Damocles' to compel compliance in cases of persistent, 'less serious' breaches.

  • The court clarified that self-represented litigants must still meet the threshold of relevance and admissibility for subpoenas under the Evidence Act 1995.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 102QB (Vexatious proceedings) - threshold for 'frequently' not met despite high volume of applications.

  • s 69ZT (Exclusion of Evidence Act provisions in child-related proceedings) - noted as providing flexibility but not an 'unlimited licence' for irrelevant credit attacks.

  • s 70NEC (Bonds) - used as the primary sanction for non-compliance.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother's credibility was significantly undermined by her 'obsessive' focus and her claim that she did not understand the word 'forthwith'.

  • The Mother's 45-minute cross-examination of the Father on credit failed to establish any relevant dishonesty.

  • The Father was found generally reliable, with his minor breach admitted and explained as an oversight.

  • The Mother's self-representation led to 'obfuscation' and an 'abuse of process' by re-litigating historical issues (some over two years old) that had no practical utility.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Mother to sign a s 70NEC bond by 4:00 pm on 14 December 2018.

  • Stay of paragraphs 7, 8, and 20 of the orders made by Bennett J on 31 July 2018 pending appeal.

  • Mother's application to declare the Father a vexatious litigant under s 102QB is dismissed.

  • Mother's application for review of the Registrar's decision regarding four subpoenas is dismissed.

  • All extant applications (except the stay) transferred to the Federal Circuit Court at Melbourne.

  • Mother's contravention application filed 28 August 2018 is largely dismissed or struck out.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge found the Mother's conduct to be 'obsessive to the point of malevolence,' prioritizing the 'seeking of justice' for past grievances over the current best interests of the children.

  • The Mother's contraventions regarding electronic communication were found to be intentional and without reasonable excuse, as she delegated her 'positive obligation' to ensure communication to the children and her partner.

  • The Father's breach was excused as a minor oversight, contrasting with the Mother's pattern of obstructive behavior.

  • The court transferred the matter to the Federal Circuit Court to consolidate the 'multi-faceted' dispute, including child support and relocation, into a single jurisdiction to prevent further fragmentation.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The Husband (aged 47) and Wife (aged 87) married in 2000 and separated on 18 October 2015.

  • The parties were divorced in March 2017.

  • The parties have one daughter, X, born in 2005.

  • On 23 March 2016, the parties entered into consent orders for property settlement and parenting.

  • The 2016 consent orders allocated approximately $17,934,731 in net assets to the Husband and $20,900,034 to the Wife.

  • The Husband was to receive business interests and real estate in China (valued at $8.8 million), while the Wife received four Sydney properties.

  • The Husband alleges that the transfer of Chinese business interests (the 'BB companies') was 'wholly inefficacious' due to litigation commenced by the Wife's father in China.

  • The Husband filed an application in August 2018 to set aside the consent orders under s 79A or seek a review of the Registrar's decision.

  • The Husband sought interim injunctions to restrain the Wife from selling the Sydney properties and to restrain the 2nd and 3rd Respondents from dealing with Chinese partnership interests.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Four Sydney properties (H Street, Suburb J; K Street, Suburb L; M Street, Suburb N; O Street, Suburb P) - Retained by Wife.

  • DISPUTED: Interests in 'BB companies' (including R Co. Ltd, U Co. Ltd, S Co. Ltd, T Co. Ltd, G partnership, V partnership, W Ltd partnership) - Husband claims transfer failed.

  • TOTAL NET POOL: $38,834,765 (Husband: $17,934,731; Wife: $20,900,034)

### Just & Equitable

  • The Husband argues the original division is no longer just and equitable because the $8.8 million in Chinese assets were not successfully transferred to him.

### Orders

  • The Husband's application for interim injunctions restraining the Wife from dealing with the Sydney properties is dismissed.

  • The Husband's application for interim injunctions against the Second and Third Respondents regarding the Chinese partnerships is dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the Husband failed to establish an evidentiary basis or a specific risk that the Wife intended to dispose of the Sydney assets to defeat his claim.

  • The court applied the principles from Waugh and Waugh [2000] FamCA 1183, requiring evidence of an intention to dispose of assets or conduct likely to defeat anticipated orders.

  • The Husband's reliance on a hearsay statement from the parties' 10-year-old daughter was insufficient to justify an injunction.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court determined that the Husband's 'fears' and a child's hearsay statement did not constitute 'clear evidence' of an intent to dispose of property.

  • The court noted that the Husband's claim of being a victim of 'sharp practice' by the Wife's family is a matter for a final hearing or summary dismissal application, not an interim injunction.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act (setting aside property orders)

  • s 37A(10) Family Law Act (review of Registrar's exercise of power)

  • Rule 7.18 Family Law Rules 2004 (dispensing with service)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent evidence of the Wife's intention to sell the Suburb J property beyond the Husband's affidavit regarding the daughter's comments.

  • No evidence adduced to support injunctive orders against the Second and Third Respondents regarding the Chinese entities.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2005 (aged 13 at the time of judgment).

### Reasoning

  • The court declined to give weight to the child's alleged statement regarding property sales as it was disputed hearsay and insufficient to ground an injunction.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found the child's alleged statement was not proper evidence of the Wife's intent to dispose of assets.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The statement attributed to the child was denied by the Wife.
summary_sections.prenup_postnup:
### Agreement Details

  • 23 March 2016 (Consent Orders).

### Disclosure & Advice

  • The Husband alleges the Wife and her relatives engaged in 'sharp practice' regarding the disclosure and transferability of Chinese business interests.

### Reasoning

  • The court acknowledged the Husband might have a prima facie case for setting aside the orders under s 79A, but this did not automatically entitle him to interim injunctions without proof of risk.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Husband's father-in-law (Mr Wu) was successful in Chinese court proceedings to set aside share transfers that were part of the Australian consent orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The full details of the Chinese court rulings and their impact on the 'BB companies' were not fully determined at this interim stage.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case underscores the high evidentiary threshold required for interim 'freezing' injunctions in property matters, even when a party alleges significant 'sharp practice' or the failure of previous consent orders."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The primary battleground is the enforceability of the 2016 consent orders regarding Chinese assets valued at $8.8 million.

  • The court prioritized the lack of immediate risk to the Sydney properties over the Husband's broader allegations of being defrauded by the Wife's family.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 79A (setting aside orders) and the court's power to grant injunctions under s 114.

  • The application of Rule 7.18 for substituted service in international contexts (China).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court did not make final credibility findings but noted the Husband's evidence regarding the child's statement was 'disputed hearsay'.

  • The Wife's credibility was bolstered at this stage by her direct denial of any intention to sell the properties.

  • The Husband's credibility regarding the 'sharp practice' allegations remains a central issue for the future substantive hearing.

  • The Husband bears a significant evidentiary burden to prove that the 2016 orders were obtained through fraud or are impracticable to carry out.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Personal service of the Further Amended Initiating Application upon the Third Respondent (Mr Cao) is dispensed with.

  • Substituted service on the Third Respondent is to be effected by post to an address in City C, China, and to his attorney at E Legal in China.

  • Paragraphs 2, 3, and 4 of the Husband's Application in a Case (seeking injunctions) are dismissed.

  • The parties are at liberty to pursue remaining relief, including the Wife's application for summary dismissal.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was that interim injunctions are extraordinary remedies that require proof of a specific risk of asset dissipation, which was absent here.

  • The court balanced the Husband's potential prima facie case for a s 79A set-aside against the Wife's right to deal with her property in the absence of proven risk.

  • The court found that the Husband's 'fears' did not override the need for admissible evidence, rejecting the use of a child's hearsay statement as a basis for restraining orders.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties have two children, B and C, born in 2013 (twins), aged approximately 5.5 years at the time of judgment.

  • The children live primarily with the mother in rental accommodation at Suburb P.

  • The father resides at Suburb O.

  • The mother's rental eligibility is contingent on her gross annual income not exceeding $75,000.

  • A dispute arose regarding the children's primary school enrollment for the 2019 school year.

  • The father proposed L School in Suburb Q; the mother proposed M School in Suburb N.

  • The mother attends H Church in Suburb J, which conducts services in her first language and has an associated school, K School.

  • The father sought expanded time during school holidays and specific orders for Christmas and Easter.

  • The mother alleged potential aggression by the father during handovers at private residences.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Personal computer containing financial records - Value not stated

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated

### Future Needs

  • Mother resides in rental accommodation with an income cap of $75,000 per annum to maintain eligibility.

### Orders

  • The father's application for the delivery of a computer was adjourned to allow him to inspect a hard drive provided by the mother containing his 2018 taxation information.

### Reasoning

  • The mother provided a hard drive which she claimed contained all necessary financial information for the father's tax returns.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother acknowledged the computer held financial information necessary for the father's 2018 taxation return.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Full and frank disclosure obligations regarding financial records.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court could not determine if the hard drive provided by the mother was sufficient without the father first inspecting it.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B and C (twins), born 2013 (5.5 years old).

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother expressed apprehension regarding the father's behavior during handovers at residential premises.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family consultant observed a meaningful relationship between the children and the father.

  • Family consultant found no evidence to support allegations that the father presents a risk to the children.

### Best Interests

  • Determined that attending a school (K School) proximal to both parents and aligned with their shared faith was in the children's best interests.

### Orders

  • Parties to have equal shared parental responsibility.

  • Children to spend time with the father on alternate weeks from Wednesday after school to Thursday morning.

  • Children to spend time with the father on alternate weekends from Friday after school to Monday morning.

  • Children to spend one week in each of the first, second, and third term school holidays with the father.

  • Specific Christmas 2018/2019 arrangements: Father to have time from 12:00 pm Christmas Day to 5:00 pm Boxing Day.

  • Easter arrangements: Father to have time from 12:00 pm Easter Sunday to Tuesday morning.

  • Children to be enrolled at K School, Suburb J for the 2019 school year.

  • Mother to inform father of medical emergencies via text message.

  • Mother to provide B’s NDIS funding information and reports to the father.

### Reasoning

  • K School was chosen as a compromise because it is proximal to both parents, avoids city traffic for the father, and is connected to the mother's church (H Church).

  • NAPLAN rankings were dismissed as an arbitrary basis for choosing a primary school for reception-aged children.

  • The court found no reason to rebut the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

  • A transition period for holiday time was deemed appropriate to help the children cope with extended stays.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found the children have a meaningful relationship with the father.

  • The court determined that 'reasonable practicality' regarding travel and school participation was a decisive factor in selecting K School.

  • The court found no evidence of risk posed by the father.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 61DA presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

  • s 60CC best interests of the child.

  • s 65DAA consideration of 'reasonable practicality' for time-sharing arrangements.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting assessments of travel times between Suburb P, Suburb O, and the various proposed schools.

  • Lack of evidence supporting the mother's claim that the father was a risk during handovers.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • No specific incidents of violence were proven; mother alleged potential for aggression.

### Orders

  • Handovers not at school to occur at H Church or the father's home, with the mother not required to engage personally with the father.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the parties had communicated civilly via a communication book and there was rigid compliance with previous orders, undermining the mother's claim of risk.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Counsel for the mother conceded no evidence of risk was available to be presented.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) protection from harm (considered but found no evidence of risk).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No evidence was led to support the mother's apprehension of aggression.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case emphasizes the court's pragmatic approach to schooling disputes, prioritizing 'reasonable practicality' and proximity over academic rankings (NAPLAN) for young children. It also reinforces the application of equal shared parental responsibility where no evidence of risk exists."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the children's connection to their religious community and the practicalities of parental involvement in school activities over the mother's preference for higher-ranked schools.

  • The lack of evidence regarding family violence led the court to maintain standard handover and shared responsibility frameworks.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 61DA (ESPR) and s 60CC (Best Interests) in determining school enrollment and holiday schedules.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility regarding safety concerns was weakened by a lack of evidence and her counsel's concession that no further evidence would be forthcoming.

  • The father was credited for not pursuing a technical breach of previous orders by the mother, showing a focus on the children's needs.

  • The mother's non-compliance with previous handover orders was noted but not penalized, as the court sought a forward-looking solution.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Equal shared parental responsibility ordered.

  • Specific schedule for alternate weekend, midweek, and holiday time established.

  • Children to attend K School in 2019.

  • Medical and NDIS information sharing protocols established.

  • Handover locations specified to include H Church and the father's residence.

  • Application regarding the computer adjourned.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the competing school proposals by selecting a third option (K School) that satisfied the shared religious goals of the parents while being geographically viable for both.

  • The expansion of the father's time into school holidays was justified by the established meaningful relationship and the lack of safety concerns.

  • The court used a 'transition' approach for the 2018/19 Christmas holidays to ensure the children could adjust to longer periods with the father.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The mother applied for a stay of interim orders pending an appeal.

  • The interim orders involved a reversal of residence for two children.

  • The children had lived with the mother in North Queensland since 2012.

  • The interim orders required the children to move from North Queensland to Canberra to live with the father and stepmother.

  • The children had already commenced school in Canberra at the time of the stay application.

  • The oldest child is identified as X.

  • The mother sought to return the children to North Queensland pending the appeal outcome.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Not specified, but X is identified as the oldest child.

### Safety Concerns

  • Fracturing of the relationship between the mother and the oldest child, X.

### Expert Evidence

  • The Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) argued against the stay, highlighting the negative impact of frequent changes in living arrangements.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined it was in the children's best interests to avoid frequent changes in living arrangements (moving back and forth between North Queensland and Canberra).

### Orders

  • The application for a stay of the interim determination is dismissed.

  • The directions listed for 21 May 2018 are vacated.

  • The matter is listed for further directions on 3 May 2018 at 4pm.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the grounds of appeal were not strong, as they primarily challenged the weight given to discretionary factors.

  • Granting a stay would cause the children to move back to North Queensland, only to potentially move back to Canberra shortly after if the final hearing (likely occurring around the same time as the appeal) mirrored the interim result.

  • The 'fractured' relationship between the mother and child X was a significant factor against returning the children to the mother's care at this interim stage.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that the appeal would not be rendered 'nugatory' without a stay, despite the impact of settled arrangements on final proceedings.

  • The existing 'fractured' state of the relationship between the mother and child X was a critical factor in maintaining the father's residence.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Principles from Aldridge & Keaton (Stay Appeal) regarding the onus on the applicant for a stay.

  • Best interests of the child as the paramount consideration in stay applications involving residence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The assessment of the appeal's strength was limited by the reliance on ex tempore reasons from the first instance judgment.

  • Conflicting views on whether the interim change of residence effectively constituted a final decision.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['The judgment reinforces the difficulty of staying interim residence orders when a change has already been implemented and the appeal grounds challenge discretionary weight rather than clear legal error.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the avoidance of 'yo-yo' living arrangements for the children over the mother's long-term history as primary carer.

  • The assessment of the appeal's strength as 'not strong' was a decisive factor in refusing the stay.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Application of Aldridge & Keaton (Stay Appeal) principles.

  • Section 121(9)(g) of the Family Law Act 1975 regarding the approval of the pseudonym 'Lorreck & Watts'.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court did not find the mother lacked bona fides, but suggested her appeal grounds were based on a 'misunderstanding of the reasoning' of the primary judge.

  • The father was treated as a successful litigant entitled to the benefit of the court's orders.

  • The Independent Children's Lawyer's submissions regarding the logistical impact on the children were given significant weight.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The application for a stay is dismissed.

  • The directions listed for 21 May 2018 are vacated.

  • The matter is listed for further directions on 3 May 2018 at 4pm.

  • The mother is granted liberty to attend the next directions hearing by telephone.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the mother's bona fide interest in appealing against the children's need for stability and the father's right to the benefit of the interim judgment.

  • The court rejected the argument that the appeal would be nugatory without a stay, noting that the final hearing would provide a fresh opportunity to determine the children's best interests.

  • The judge emphasized that the 'fractured' relationship with the mother made an immediate return to her care potentially harmful to the children's emotional well-being.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were in a de facto relationship that ended in 1993.

  • The parties entered into a purported Binding Financial Agreement (BFA) which claimed to be under s 90UD of the Family Law Act 1975.

  • The Applicant, represented by Legal Minds, commenced proceedings in the Family Court of Australia in 2015 to set aside the BFA.

  • The First Respondent, represented by Watts McCray, filed a response seeking dismissal and indemnity costs but did not challenge jurisdiction initially.

  • The Family Court's jurisdiction over de facto relationships is generally limited to those ending after 1 March 2009, unless parties 'opt-in' via specific statutory procedures.

  • In 2017, the Court determined it lacked jurisdiction as the 'opt-in' requirements under the 2008 Amending Act were not met.

  • The current proceedings concern whether the solicitors for the Applicant and First Respondent should personally bear the costs of the failed litigation.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Reasoning

  • The court found that the solicitors initiated and maintained property proceedings in a forum that clearly lacked jurisdiction.

  • The Applicant's solicitors (Legal Minds) failed to verify if the mandatory 'opt-in' requirements of item 86A of the 2008 Amending Act were satisfied before filing.

  • The First Respondent's solicitors (Watts McCray) failed to identify the jurisdictional defect in their initial response and persisted in asserting the BFA's validity.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The solicitors failed to answer mandatory jurisdictional questions (Question 26a) on the court initiating forms regarding the date of separation.

  • Legal Minds joined five additional respondents to a proceeding where no underlying jurisdiction existed, exacerbating costs.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Amendment (De facto Financial Matters and Other Measures) Act 2008 (Cth), Schedule 1, Item 86A.

  • Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) ss 31 and 33 regarding jurisdiction.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No evidence that Legal Minds made enquiries of the original drafting solicitor (Ms C) regarding 'opt-in' advice.

  • Conflict between the solicitor's affidavit (Mr K) and the Applicant's trial affidavit regarding the nature of legal advice received at the time of signing the BFA.
summary_sections.prenup_postnup:
### Agreement Details

  • Executed following a 1993 separation (exact date not specified).

### Orders

  • The BFA was effectively set aside/ignored due to lack of jurisdiction in the substantive 2017 judgment.

### Reasoning

  • The agreement failed to meet the requirements of item 86A of Part 2 of Schedule 1 of the Family Law Amendment (De facto Financial Matters & Other Measures) Act 2008 (Cth).

  • There was no written choice to 'opt-in' signed by both parties after receiving specific legal advice on the advantages/disadvantages of that choice.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The BFA recitals were insufficient to constitute a 'written choice' to opt-in to the federal de facto regime.

  • The mandatory nature of the 'opt-in' provisions was strictly interpreted.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Item 86A(5) of Part 2 of Schedule 1 of the Family Law Amendment (De facto Financial Matters & Other Measures) Act 2008 (Cth).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of any signed statement from legal representatives confirming advice on 'opting in' was provided.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case confirms the Family Court's implied power as a Court of Record to order solicitors to pay costs personally, even when the court lacks substantive jurisdiction under the Family Law Act."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court established that the threshold for ordering costs against a solicitor is a 'serious dereliction of duty' or 'conduct that attracts the censure of the court in a serious way,' which is higher than mere negligence.

  • Solicitors have an overriding duty to the court to ensure jurisdiction exists before initiating or defending claims.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The court relied on its status as a 'Court of Record' and the principles in 're P’s Bill of Costs' and 'Jachimowicz' to exercise implied powers.

  • Interplay between the Family Law Act 1975 and the Civil Procedure Act 2005 (NSW) regarding costs jurisdiction.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court found the conduct of both sets of solicitors to be a 'serious dereliction of duty.'

  • Mr K (Legal Minds) was criticized for claiming he formed a view that the 'opt-in' case was arguable, while the Applicant's own evidence stated she never received such advice.

  • Watts McCray's argument that they had no obligation to inform the court of a lack of jurisdiction was explicitly rejected by the judge.

  • The solicitors' failure to accurately complete court forms (Question 26a) was a primary indicator of professional failure.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Legal Minds to indemnify the Applicant in relation to any costs incurred in proceedings NCC1896 of 2015.

  • Watts McCray be restrained from charging the First Respondent in relation to any work done in proceedings NCC1896 of 2015 after 30 September 2016.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge reasoned that it is untenable for a court to be powerless to protect litigants from economic loss caused by lawyers who advise them to initiate proceedings in a court without jurisdiction.

  • Legal Minds' conduct was deemed 'inexcusable' because they ignored the notorious fact that the Family Court only has jurisdiction over de facto separations post-March 2009 unless specific 'opt-in' steps are taken.

  • Watts McCray's conduct was similarly censured because they failed to raise the jurisdictional issue and persisted in defending the BFA's validity even after the defect was apparent.

  • The court balanced the solicitors' duty to their clients against their duty to the court, finding the latter was gravely breached by obstructing the interests of justice through meritless litigation.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • On 9 November 2018, the court made parenting orders following a dispute.

  • The mother had withheld the children from the father for approximately four months, breaching a pre-existing week-about regime.

  • The 9 November 2018 orders granted the mother sole parental responsibility for the children's Australian passports and Country B visas.

  • Paragraph 9 of the 9 November 2018 orders provided the father with 'make-up time' consisting of one extra week on two separate occasions.

  • A dispute arose regarding the interpretation of the 'extra week': the mother argued for a 14-day block followed by her week; the father and ICL argued for a 21-day block (the father's week + the mother's week + the father's next week).

  • The mother contacted the judge's chambers via email without the consent of other parties to seek clarification.

  • The court listed the matter for an ex tempore judgment to clarify the orders and address procedural conduct.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Expert Evidence

  • The Independent Children’s Lawyer (represented by Ms. Palmer) provided an interpretation of the orders that supported the father's position.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that clarifying the make-up time was necessary to ensure the father received the time intended to compensate for the mother's prior withholding of the children.

### Orders

  • The Order made 9 November 2018 is amended to clarify paragraph 9(1)(ii).

  • The children are to spend two blocks of 21 days with the father in each instance before returning to the mother's care.

### Reasoning

  • The judge intended for the father to receive 'make-up time' that effectively utilized the mother's scheduled weeks to compensate for the four months he missed.

  • The 21-day block interpretation (two of the father's weeks plus the mother's intervening week) was the correct execution of the court's intent to provide additional time.

  • The Independent Children's Lawyer's interpretation was preferred as it provided a clear circuit-breaker to the parental dispute.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother withheld the children for four months, necessitating compensatory 'make-up time'.

  • The ambiguity in the original phrasing of 'one extra week' led to conflicting interpretations of the 14-day vs 21-day blocks.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) - general parenting jurisdiction.

  • Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) r 17.02 regarding variation of orders.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The mother's claims regarding requirements from 'Country B authorities' were not supported by admissible evidence, as the judge refused to read her unilaterally submitted documents.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case clarifies the court's approach to 'make-up time' following a significant period of withholding and reinforces strict procedural boundaries regarding ex parte communication with the court."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court confirmed that 'make-up time' should be interpreted in a way that provides a meaningful remedy for the parent who was deprived of time.

  • Unilateral communication with the court (ex parte) is a breach of procedural fairness and will be met with judicial censure.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 121(9)(g) Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding publication under pseudonym.

  • r 17.02A(b) and r 17.02 Family Law Rules 2004 regarding the correction and variation of orders.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was negatively impacted by her unilateral withholding of the children for four months.

  • The mother's procedural conduct was criticized for attempting to influence the court through unauthorized ex parte email communications.

  • The father's position was bolstered by the support of the Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL), whose interpretation was viewed as objective and consistent with the court's original intent.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Order made 9 November 2018 amended to specify 21-day blocks for father's make-up time.

  • The mother is prohibited from contacting the Judge's Chambers without prior consent and agreement from the father and ICL.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge sought to resolve a practical ambiguity in the 9 November 2018 orders that was causing ongoing conflict between the parents.

  • The court adopted the interpretation supported by the ICL to ensure the father received the 21-day blocks (3 weeks) intended to offset the 4-month withholding period.

  • The court prioritized procedural integrity by refusing to read the mother's unauthorized email submissions, emphasizing that all communications must be transparent and agreed upon by all parties.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced their relationship in 2002, married in 2005, and separated on 8 September 2013.

  • There are two children of the marriage: X (born 2008) and Y (born 2011).

  • Consent orders were made on 25 November 2015 for the children to live with the mother and spend alternate weekends with the father.

  • In May 2016, the father retained X, alleging the child was stressed and exposed to the mother's methamphetamine use and domestic violence with her partner, Mr C.

  • The father was arrested in 2017 for aggravated assault on Y; bail conditions initially prevented him from spending time with the children.

  • Y sustained a hairline fracture to her skull while in the care of the father’s partner, Ms G.

  • The mother sought to suspend all time between the children and the father/paternal grandmother based on reports from the children's psychologists.

  • The paternal grandmother sought to be removed as an intervener but remained willing to supervise the father's time.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2008, aged approximately 10)

  • Y (born 2011, aged approximately 7)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of the father choking/strangling Y in May 2017.

  • Mother's previous relationship with Mr C (history of violent offending).

  • Father's partner Ms G (criminal history, drug use, and child protection history).

  • Y's skull fracture while in Ms G's care.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Assessment Report (2 February 2017) recommended equal shared parental responsibility and alternate weekend time.

  • Reports from Ms J (X's psychologist) and Dr K (Y's psychologist) at H Group regarding children's anxiety and fear of the father.

### Best Interests

  • Court found it is in the best interests of the children to retain a relationship with their father, provided risks are managed by supervision.

### Orders

  • Paragraphs 2 and 9 of Orders made 21 December 2017 discharged.

  • Children to spend time with the father from 10:00 am to 3:00 pm on Saturday 15 December 2018 and every fourth week thereafter.

  • Children to spend time with the father from 10:00 am to 3:00 pm on Sunday 30 December 2018 and every fourth week thereafter.

  • Time with the father must be supervised by the paternal grandmother.

  • Handovers to occur at McDonald’s Restaurant, B Street, Adelaide.

### Reasoning

  • The court adopted a conservative interim approach, balancing the need for a meaningful relationship with the father against protection from harm.

  • The judge noted that the children's negative views of the father might be influenced by the high-conflict environment and the maternal family's hostility.

  • The court maintained confidence in the paternal grandmother's ability to supervise, despite the mother's objections.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that the children's relationship with the father was historically strong and should not be severed based on untested interim allegations.

  • The psychologists' reports were treated with caution as they were therapeutic rather than forensic and did not involve the father.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (meaningful relationship vs protection from harm).

  • s 60CC(2A) greater weight given to protection from harm.

  • s 61DA presumption of equal shared parental responsibility (noted as complex in interim proceedings with violence allegations).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No updated forensic family report since February 2017.

  • The psychologists (Ms J and Dr K) did not conduct formal diagnostic assessments or seek input from the father.

  • Conflicting testimony regarding the adequacy of the paternal grandmother's supervision.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Father's arrest for aggravated assault on Y in 2017.

  • Allegations of choking and physical chastisement by the father.

  • Mother's exposure of children to Mr C, a violent offender.

### Protection Orders
