  • The father's failure to provide an updated psychological report (his evidence was 2 years old) created a risk that his current mental state was not fully understood by the court.

  • The court noted the father's tendency to blame the mother for 'alienating' the children, a claim the court did not support.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Sole parental responsibility to the mother.

  • Children to live with the mother.

  • Graduated supervised time for the father (6 times per year).

  • Father to pay for supervision and psychological reports.

  • Injunctions against the father regarding contact and proximity.

  • Father must seek leave to vary orders before March 2023.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer discharged.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the children's right to know their biological father with the established risk of harm and the mother's mental health needs.

  • The 'graduated' nature of the orders (increasing duration over years) was designed to build trust and allow the father to prove his behavior has modified.

  • The court emphasized that the success of the orders depends on the mother's ability to support the relationship, despite her trauma.

  • The restriction on the father's ability to re-litigate (requiring leave) was intended to provide the family with a period of stability during the currency of the state Intervention Order.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The Applicant filed an Initiating Application on 2 May 2018 seeking a declaration of invalidity of marriage.

  • The grounds for the application are that the Respondent was allegedly married to another person at the time of the marriage to the Applicant.

  • The Applicant's solicitor, Ms. C, filed an affidavit on 16 November 2018 detailing extensive efforts to locate and serve the Respondent.

  • Enquiries were made with the Respondent's former lawyers, Ms. D Pty Solicitor, who held no current instructions.

  • Electoral roll searches were conducted for two names used by the Respondent, leading to correspondence sent to multiple addresses.

  • The Respondent's son was contacted but could not identify the Respondent's whereabouts.

  • A third party, Mr. G, confirmed the Respondent was aware of the correspondence from the Applicant's law firm.

  • A process server attended an address in F Town, Queensland, where a resident, Mr. H, suggested the Respondent might be in K Town but provided no address.

  • On 12 October 2018, the Respondent telephoned Ms. C, identifying herself and acknowledging the documents were about 'nullifying' the marriage.

  • The Respondent requested that documents be sent to the B Town Post Office in Queensland.

  • As of the hearing date, the documents remained uncollected at the B Town Post Office.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['The case addresses the procedural threshold for dispensing with service in nullity proceedings where a respondent is actively avoiding service but has actual knowledge of the litigation.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Respondent's telephone conversation with the Applicant's solicitor on 12 October 2018 proved she had actual knowledge of the nature of the proceedings.

  • The Respondent's nomination of the B Town Post Office as a delivery point, followed by her failure to collect the documents, indicated an attempt to frustrate formal service.

  • The court found that 'all reasonable steps' had been taken by the Applicant's legal team to effect service.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Rule 7.18 of the Family Law Rules 2004 (factors for dispensing with service).

  • Section 121(9)(g) of the Family Law Act 1975 (pseudonym approval).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Respondent's credibility is undermined by her evasive conduct, specifically nominating a post office for service and then failing to collect the documents.

  • The Applicant's solicitor, Ms. C, was found to be highly reliable, providing a comprehensive and transparent account of all search and service efforts.

  • There is a significant litigation risk for the Respondent; by failing to engage despite actual knowledge, the court is prepared to proceed to a final determination of nullity in her absence.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Requirement for service of the Initiating Application filed 2 May 2018 and all associated documents is dispensed with.

  • The matter is adjourned for hearing at 10:00 AM on 22 January 2019.

  • The Applicant is directed to send a copy of these orders by registered post to the Respondent care of the B Town Post Office, Queensland.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied Rule 7.18 of the Family Law Rules 2004, weighing the 'grave and important' nature of marriage validity against the evidence of the Respondent's awareness.

  • The court determined that natural justice was satisfied because the Respondent explicitly acknowledged the nullity application during a call to the solicitor Ms. C.

  • The judge concluded that no other reasonably available methodology of service would be more effective than the steps already taken.

  • The Respondent was deemed to be 'on notice' such that the proceedings could continue in her absence if she chose not to participate.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in 1986 and married in 1987.

  • The parties separated under one roof in February 2016; the husband vacated the former matrimonial home in March 2017.

  • There are two adult children: Ms E (28) and Mr G (26).

  • The husband operates a financial planning business, O Group (OG), through O Pty Ltd and P Pty Ltd.

  • The parties were previously involved in a family farming enterprise associated with the wife's family.

  • The O Group has a debt of approximately $1.5M with AA Bank, which the bank refused to renew beyond the end of 2018.

  • The husband sought an interim order to transfer the J Town property (in the wife's sole name) to him to facilitate refinancing the business debt.

  • The wife sought extensive financial disclosure regarding the Harkness Family Trust and other entities.

  • The husband admitted to using trust distributions (nominally for the daughter) to pay his own legal fees.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: W Street Property - $1,850,000

  • AGREED: J Town Property (H Street) - $1,500,000 to $1,850,000

  • AGREED: Harkness Family Superannuation - $823,010

  • AGREED: R Bank Superannuation - $38,358

  • DISPUTED: Harkness Family Trust - Husband claims negative value of ($854,633) in affidavit vs positive value in financial statements

  • DISPUTED: Shares in U Investments Pty Ltd - $1,331,706

  • DISPUTED: O Group (OG) valuation - Subject to expert reports by Mr M (N Group) and Mr S (T Group)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Approximately $1,289,924 (based on Husband's 01/11/18 Financial Statement)

### Contributions

  • Wife brought an interest in a family farming enterprise into the marriage.

  • Husband claims his involvement in the farming enterprise was influential and significantly improved the endeavor.

  • Husband claims the wife's father made representations regarding a future interest in the farming enterprise.

  • Parties borrowed $3M for the O Group business, with $2M applied directly to the enterprise.

### Future Needs

  • Wife has a fledgling business as an alternative healer.

  • Husband earns approximately $200,000 per annum through O Group wages plus trust distributions.

  • Significant disparity in current earning capacity and control over family financial structures.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it was not just and equitable to grant an interim transfer of the J Town property as it would involve fully encumbering the asset, potentially prejudicing the final property settlement.

### Orders

  • Husband's application for the interim transfer of the J Town property is dismissed.

  • Husband to provide a list of all margin loans and debt facilities for the Harkness Family Trust from 1 January 2016 to date within 28 days.

  • Husband to provide a reconciliation of asset sales (B Pty Ltd, C Pty Ltd, and D Bank shares) and the application of proceeds within 28 days.

  • Parties to provide reciprocal bank and credit card statements for all accounts under their control on the 28th of every alternate month.

  • Husband to provide details of shares/securities acquired or sold by him or the Harkness Family Trust every two months.

  • Husband to file an affidavit by 14 March 2019 detailing all monies borrowed from or gifted by the daughter, Ms E, since 1 July 2017.

  • Husband to provide a detailed calculation of the $243,304.15 in drawings attributed to the wife in the draft 2018 Trust accounts.

  • Husband to provide details of security and application for Westpac loans (items 46-51, 54, and 65 on his balance sheet).

### Reasoning

  • The court must exercise power under s 79 conservatively on an interim basis (citing Harris & Harris).

  • Refinancing the J Town property to its limit ($1.5M) would create a situation that might not be reversible at final trial.

  • The husband's role as trustee creates a conflict of interest, requiring strict disclosure and transparency which he had previously failed to provide.

  • The husband's 'paternalistic' approach to managing the wife's trust distributions without her consent was inappropriate post-separation.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The J Town property was the wife's primary asset and her preferred settlement retention; encumbering it for a business debt was too risky for an interim stage.

  • Husband's use of trust funds to pay his legal fees via distributions to the daughter undermined his reliability as a fiduciary.

  • The lack of evidence that the husband had exhausted other options (e.g., selling a helicopter or other property) weakened his case for the J Town transfer.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 Family Law Act 1975 (Property settlement power)

  • s 80 Family Law Act 1975 (General powers of the court)

  • Principles from Hickey & Hickey regarding multi-part property orders

  • Principles from Harris & Harris regarding conservative interim orders

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No evidence provided by the husband that he had secured the necessary finance for the proposed restructure.

  • Conflicting values for the Harkness Family Trust between the husband's financial statements and his affidavit.

  • Unanswered correspondence from the wife's solicitors regarding trust accounts and tax implications.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Ms E (28 years old)

  • Mr G (26 years old)

### Reasoning

  • No parenting orders were required as the children are adults.

  • The court declined to restrain distributions to the children (Order 5.2) because the children had not been given notice of the application.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Distributions to the daughter were found to have been used to fund the husband's legal fees.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • Wife required payment of her income tax liabilities arising from trust distributions she did not physically receive.

### Capacity to Pay

  • Husband had control over the Harkness Family Trust and O Group income to satisfy the wife's tax debts.

### Income & Expenses

  • Husband's Income: $3,846/week (Nov 2018 statement).

  • Husband's Expenses: $7,434/week (Nov 2018 statement).

  • Wife's tax debt: $35,471 (for FY 2017).

### Orders

  • Husband to pay the wife's income tax referable to any Harkness Family Trust distributions for the FY ended 30 June 2018 directly to the ATO within seven days of receiving her assessment.

### Reasoning

  • The husband had previously agreed to pay the wife's 2017 tax debt but failed to ensure the payment was processed until the day of the hearing.

  • Since the husband controlled the trust and the distributions that created the tax debt, it was appropriate he satisfy the liability.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Husband's failure to provide a receipt for the 2017 tax payment until the hearing date demonstrated a lack of diligence in complying with financial obligations.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 (Duty to maintain)

  • s 75(2) factors
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Reasoning

  • While an affidavit from Ms L regarding the husband's behavior was mentioned, no specific findings of family violence were made in this interim judgment.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case reinforces the high threshold for interim property orders that involve the significant encumbrance or disposal of assets, particularly when such actions might be irreversible.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the preservation of the asset pool over the husband's business restructuring needs.

  • The husband's conduct as a trustee post-separation was scrutinized, leading to robust disclosure orders to remedy the information asymmetry.

  • The 'he-said-she-said' nature of the disclosure dispute was criticized by the court, emphasizing the duty of parties to provide clear evidence of compliance.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79 (property) and the court's discretion to make interim orders that are 'conservative' to ensure a just and equitable final outcome.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility was significantly impacted by his 'paternalistic' management of trust funds and his failure to provide receipts for court-ordered tax payments until the hearing.

  • The court found the husband's evidence regarding the Harkness Family Trust to be inconsistent (claiming it was a liability in his affidavit while listing it as an asset elsewhere).

  • Both parties were criticized for the 'disgraceful' state of the litigation where basic disclosure compliance was still in dispute despite senior legal representation.

  • The husband's failure to answer specific solicitor correspondence (e.g., the letter of 12 February 2018) was noted as a concern.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The parties are relieved of the obligation to attend a Conciliation Conference due to a prior private mediation on 19 September 2018.

  • Wife to provide all credit card statements for the K Partnership from 1 January 2012 to date within 28 days.

  • Costs of the proceedings on 19 November 2018 are reserved.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was centered on risk mitigation. By refusing the transfer of the J Town property, the court prevented a potential $1.5M encumbrance that would have limited the trial judge's options.

  • The court balanced the husband's need to save the O Group business against the wife's right to have her potential settlement assets protected.

  • The disclosure orders were justified by the husband's admitted use of trust funds for personal legal expenses and his failure to provide transparent accounting to the wife, who was a beneficiary.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property orders were made over a year prior to this hearing on an undefended basis, granting the husband the right to sell the M Street property.

  • The wife subsequently applied under s 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 to set aside those property orders.

  • On 13 December 2017, the wife obtained an urgent stay of the auction for the M Street property, giving an undertaking as to damages.

  • On 28 March 2018, the court ordered the wife to pay specific property expenses, including land tax and insurance for the M Street property.

  • The husband alleged the wife failed to pay land tax for approximately seven months, leading to a debt of $26,364.27 and threats of legal action from the State Revenue Office.

  • The wife paid the outstanding land tax on 3 December 2018, two days before the hearing, using $26,600 borrowed from friends.

  • The husband sought to discharge the stay and proceed with the sale of the M Street property due to the wife's non-compliance and the risk to his credit rating.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: M Street, Suburb N ('M Street Property') - 3 units/properties (Value not specified, but subject to sale orders)

  • AGREED: O Street, Suburb P - Former matrimonial home (Value not specified)

  • AGREED: Q Street, Suburb R - Real property requiring repairs (Value not specified)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated in the interlocutory judgment.

### Contributions

  • The judgment does not detail historical contributions as it focuses on an interlocutory application to sell assets pending a s 79A application.

  • The husband paid $8,535 out of pocket for advertising costs in December 2017, which the wife was ordered to reimburse.

### Future Needs

  • The wife argued the M Street property was 'unique' due to planning restrictions and its potential to provide her with rental income and a residence.

  • The husband argued his financial position and credit rating were being prejudiced by the wife's failure to meet court-ordered liabilities.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it just and equitable to order the sale of the M Street property because the wife failed to demonstrate that she would comply with future financial obligations under court orders.

### Orders

  • All existing orders precluding the husband from selling the property at M Street, Suburb N are discharged.

  • The husband is granted the right to sell the M Street property.

  • After discharge of sale costs, liabilities, and encumbrances, the balance of funds is to be placed in an interest-bearing account in the husband's name, held upon trust for both parties pending further order.

### Reasoning

  • The wife had a history of non-cooperation and failed to provide any explanation for a seven-month delay in paying land tax.

  • The wife's reliance on borrowed funds ($26,600) to pay the debt at the last minute indicated she lacked the independent resources to maintain the property expenses.

  • The 'balance of convenience' favored the husband, who held the benefit of a prior judgment, over the wife's claim of the property's 'uniqueness'.

  • The court determined that the wife's s 79A application was not guaranteed to succeed, and her interests could be protected by holding the sale proceeds in trust.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's failure to instruct her solicitors to respond to the husband's requests for payment for several months was a critical factor.

  • The State Revenue Office's referral of the $26,364.27 debt to solicitors for legal action against the husband created an unacceptable risk to his financial standing.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act 1975 (setting aside orders)

  • Rule 1.08 Family Law Rules 2004 (obligations of parties and lawyers to the court)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No explanation was provided by the wife or her legal representatives for the failure to respond to correspondence or the delay in payment.

  • The 'uniqueness' of the M Street property was asserted by the wife but not supported by independent expert evidence beyond her own claims regarding planning restrictions.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Income & Expenses

  • The wife was paying a monthly sum of $4,167 to the husband, which he claimed was insufficient to cover property expenses.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case underscores the court's willingness to allow the sale of assets during a pending s 79A application if the party seeking to retain the asset fails to comply with interim financial obligations."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • A party's failure to comply with interlocutory orders regarding property expenses can override their claim to the 'uniqueness' of a property.

  • The court will prioritize protecting a party from financial harm (such as credit rating damage) over a non-compliant party's desire to retain a specific asset for future use.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 79A (the right to seek to set aside orders) and the court's power to manage assets to prevent financial prejudice to the other party.

  • Rule 1.08 of the Family Law Rules 2004 regarding the duty of parties and lawyers to facilitate the court's objectives.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court found the wife's credibility and reliability regarding financial management to be low, noting her 'silence' and failure to provide instructions to her solicitors for months.

  • The wife's failure to explain the 7-month delay in land tax payments undermined her position that she could be trusted with future obligations.

  • The husband's evidence regarding the SRO debts and his out-of-pocket expenses was accepted as uncontested and reliable.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Discharge of all existing orders precluding the sale of M Street, Suburb N.

  • Husband granted the right to sell M Street; proceeds to be held in trust pending further order.

  • The application in a case filed 9 November 2018 and the response filed 5 December 2018 are otherwise dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was based on the 'balance of convenience'. While the wife wanted to preserve the property for her s 79A claim, her 7-month default on land tax payments and lack of independent funds made her continued management of the property untenable.

  • The court balanced the wife's potential prejudice (loss of a 'unique' property) against the husband's actual prejudice (legal threats from the SRO and credit risk).

  • The decision to hold sale proceeds in trust was a compromise to ensure that if the wife's s 79A application succeeded, she would have access to the cash value of the asset.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • Final property orders were delivered on 10 October 2018 involving assets in Australia and China.

  • The final orders required the Husband to transfer a specific motor vehicle (Motor vehicle 2) to the Wife.

  • The final orders required the Husband to pay the Wife $90,500 within six months of the order date.

  • The Husband sold the motor vehicle prior to the final hearing for $3,000, making the transfer order impossible to fulfill.

  • The Wife alleged the Husband made threats on 10 October 2018 to withhold money and take revenge.

  • The Wife alleged the Husband made death threats and suicide threats on 22 October 2018.

  • A mutual friend, Mr. T, informed the Wife that the Husband intended to sell the S Town property and move the proceeds to China.

  • The Wife obtained a caveat on the S Town property to protect her interest in the deferred payment.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: S Town property - Value not specified (subject to mortgage)

  • DISPUTED: Motor vehicle 2 - $7,000 (Wife's balance sheet value) vs $3,000 (Husband's sale price)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly stated in this enforcement judgment

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it just and equitable to vary the orders under s 79A because the Husband's default in transferring the car (due to its prior sale) necessitated a cash substitute.

### Orders

  • Order 4(b) of the 10 October 2018 orders (transfer of Motor vehicle 2) is discharged.

  • The Husband must pay the Wife $93,500 (the original $90,500 plus $3,000 for the car) within six months of the original order date.

  • The Husband is restrained from refinancing or withdrawing funds from the mortgage of the S Town property.

  • Proceeds from the sale of the S Town property must be held in trust by a solicitor or real estate agent.

  • Sale proceeds must be applied to discharge the mortgage and sale expenses, then pay the Wife $93,500, with the balance to the Husband.

### Reasoning

  • The court applied s 79A of the Family Law Act to vary the order regarding the car because the Husband could not comply with the original transfer order.

  • The court accepted the Husband's evidence that the car was sold for $3,000 and added this amount to the deferred payment rather than the $7,000 book value.

  • Injunctive relief under s 114 was deemed proper to secure the Wife's entitlement given the risk of the Husband removing funds to China.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Husband sold the subject motor vehicle before the final hearing, creating a 'default without fault' regarding the transfer order.

  • The Wife's evidence regarding the Husband's intention to move funds to China was sufficient to justify protective injunctions.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act 1975 (variation of property orders due to default/circumstances).

  • s 114 Family Law Act 1975 (power to grant injunctions).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the motor vehicle was provided to contest the $3,000 sale price.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Not specified (Children referred to as X and Y)

### Safety Concerns

  • Wife alleged threats of violence and death threats by the Husband.

### Orders

  • The Husband's application to remove children from the Family Law Watchlist was adjourned for further evidence.

### Reasoning

  • The court found the Husband's application regarding the Watchlist was insufficiently supported by evidence at the time of the hearing.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court refused to make immediate orders regarding the Watchlist due to a lack of evidentiary support for the children's travel needs.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of evidence supporting the necessity or safety of removing children from the Watchlist for travel to China.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Threats of revenge on 10 October 2018.

  • Death threats and suicide threats on 22 October 2018.

### Protection Orders

  • The Wife obtained a family violence order against the Husband in late 2018.

### Reasoning

  • The history of threats and the existing family violence order informed the court's decision to grant injunctions to protect the property settlement funds.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court accepted the Wife's affidavit evidence regarding threats as a basis for the 'risk' required for injunctive relief.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 114 Family Law Act 1975 (injunctions in circumstances arising out of the matrimonial relationship).
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case demonstrates the court's willingness to use s 79A and s 114 to ensure the integrity of final property orders when one party's conduct or changed circumstances (like the sale of an asset) threatens the implementation of those orders."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the actual proceeds of the car sale ($3,000) over the balance sheet value ($7,000) when substituting a cash payment for a physical asset.

  • The risk of asset dissipation or removal from the jurisdiction (China) is a primary driver for post-judgment injunctive relief.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79A (variation for default) and s 114 (injunctive protection of property interests).
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Husband's credibility was partially supported by documentary evidence regarding the car sale price ($3,000).

  • The Husband's general reliability was undermined by the Wife's allegations of threats and his stated intention to move funds to China, which the court found credible enough to warrant injunctions.

  • The Husband bears a significant evidentiary burden to succeed on his adjourned applications regarding the Family Law Watchlist and the Chinese divorce requirements.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Husband's cross-application regarding the Chinese divorce and the Family Law Watchlist was adjourned to allow for the filing of further evidence.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the Husband's technical default (selling the car) against the Wife's need for security regarding the $90,500 payment.

  • The court found that while the Husband was not yet in breach of the payment order (as the 6-month deadline had not passed), his alleged threats justified 'benign' protective orders that did not overly burden the sale process.

  • The court adopted a pragmatic approach by adding the car's value to the existing debt rather than requiring an immediate $7,000 payment, which the Husband likely could not make without the house sale.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are the parents of one child, X, born in 2005 (aged 13 at the time of judgment).

  • The parents separated in 2015.

  • Final orders were made by consent in 2016, granting equal shared parental responsibility with the child living with the father.

  • The mother primarily resides in China but claimed to have purchased a property in Suburb Z to be near the child's school.

  • The matter came before the Court as a review de novo of orders made by Senior Registrar Campbell on 19 July 2018.

  • By the commencement of the hearing, the parties had agreed that the child would live with the father and spend specific weekend and end-of-year holiday time with the mother.

  • Two issues remained in dispute: the conclusion time for weekend visits (Sunday evening vs. Monday morning) and the allocation of short school holidays.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born 2005 (13 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • The expert brief includes instructions to assess the impact of exposing the child to domestic violence and capacity to protect the child.

### Expert Evidence

  • Dr. AA appointed as Single Expert to prepare a report on parenting capacity, mental health, and the child's needs.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that maintaining a stable routine for school on Monday mornings was in the child's best interests.

  • Sharing short holidays was deemed necessary to maintain the child's relationship with the paternal family.

### Orders

  • Equal shared parental responsibility.

  • Child to live with the father.

  • Weekend time with the mother to conclude at 6pm on Sunday.

  • Short school holidays to be shared/alternated between parents.

  • 2018/2019 December/January holidays to be shared equally.

  • Dr. AA appointed as Single Expert; parties to each deposit $8,250.00 into trust for fees (Total $16,500).

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the detriment of uncertainty regarding Monday morning school routines outweighed the benefit of an extra night with the mother.

  • The mother's proposal for all short holidays would deprive the child of significant time with the paternal family.

  • The court noted a lack of evidence regarding the mother's property purchase and the safety/duration of the proposed 30-minute bus trip to school.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The child currently walks to school from the father's home; there was no evidence the child had experience traveling by bus from the mother's purported residence.

  • The mother's schedule is variable, leading to a lack of consistency if Monday drop-offs were ordered.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 60CC best interests factors, specifically the benefit of meaningful relationships with both parents vs. the need for stability in schooling routines.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent evidence of the mother's property purchase in Suburb Z.

  • No evidence supporting the mother's claim that the bus trip to school takes 30 minutes.

  • Conflicting assertions regarding the father's travel to China without supporting documentation.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Not specifically detailed in the judgment, but included as a mandatory consideration for the Single Expert report.

### Child Exposure

  • Expert requested to assess each party's insight into the impact of exposing the child to domestic violence.

### Orders

  • Parties restrained from denigrating each other or discussing court proceedings in the child's presence.

### Reasoning

  • Standard injunctive orders to protect the child's emotional wellbeing during ongoing litigation.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court identified the need for expert assessment of 'risk of abuse or harm' as a standard precautionary measure in high-conflict parenting disputes.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 60CC(2)(b) protection from physical or psychological harm.

  • Section 4AB definition of family violence (referenced in expert instructions).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court noted that disputes regarding the father's availability and the mother's residence could not be resolved without further evidence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case emphasizes the court's reluctance to disrupt established school routines for the sake of parental 'engagement' when logistical claims are unsupported by evidence."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the 'status quo' of the child's school commute (walking from the father's) over a speculative bus commute from the mother's new residence.

  • The court balanced the mother's international residency with the child's need for a relationship with the paternal family by rejecting the mother's claim to all short holidays.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC best interests and the procedural requirements for expert evidence under Part 15.5 of the Family Law Rules.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was challenged regarding her purchase of property in Suburb Z and the feasibility of the child's commute; the court treated these claims as 'uncertain'.

  • The father's credibility was challenged by the mother regarding his frequent travel to China, which he denied; the court managed this risk by ordering he notify the mother if he is unavailable to care for the child during his allocated time.

  • Both parties were found to have reached a significant level of agreement, but the remaining disputes were characterized by 'he-said-she-said' assertions without independent verification.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Appointment of Dr. AA as Single Expert under Part 15.5 of the Family Law Rules.

  • Each party to pay $8,250.00 (total $16,500) for the expert report.

  • Independent Children’s Lawyer (ICL) granted photocopy access to subpoenaed material.

  • Leave granted for parties to issue more than 5 subpoenas.

  • Liberty to re-list on 72 hours’ notice regarding compliance with orders.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic centered on minimizing 'detriment of uncertainty' for a 13-year-old child during the school week.

  • The court adopted a cautious approach to the mother's evidence regarding her new local residence, noting that such factual disputes could not be resolved in an interim hearing without corroboration.

  • The decision to share short holidays reflects the court's duty to ensure the child maintains meaningful connections with both extended families, despite the mother's primary residence being overseas.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were involved in protracted property settlement litigation concluding in trial orders on 24 August 2018.

  • The trial judge ordered the husband to pay the wife a lump sum of $1,050,000 as part of the property division.

  • The husband filed a Notice of Appeal on 21 September 2018 challenging the trial judge's valuation of business assets and the overall percentage split.

  • The husband applied for a stay of the August 2018 orders pending the determination of the appeal by the Full Court.

  • The husband argued that satisfying the $1,050,000 judgment would require the immediate sale of commercial real estate and liquidation of business interests, causing irreparable financial harm.

  • The wife opposed the stay, citing her immediate need for funds to secure housing and legal representation for the appeal process.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Lump sum payment ordered at trial - $1,050,000

  • DISPUTED: Valuation of the husband's commercial business interests - Disputed on appeal

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not explicitly quantified in this procedural judgment, but involves multi-million dollar commercial and residential holdings.

### Contributions

  • The trial judgment (under appeal) assessed contributions over a long-term marriage, though specific percentages are not the focus of this stay application.

### Future Needs

  • The wife asserted an immediate need for $200,000 to $300,000 to meet living expenses and appeal costs.

  • The husband argued his future financial viability was threatened by the forced sale of income-producing assets.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court considered whether it was just and equitable to delay the wife's receipt of her entitlement versus the risk of an unjust liquidation of the husband's assets.

### Orders

  • The property orders made on 24 August 2018 are stayed pending the determination of the appeal.

  • The stay is conditional upon the husband paying the wife a partial sum of $150,000 within 28 days.

  • The husband is ordered to pay the wife's solicitors $50,000 as a provision for her legal costs of the appeal.

  • The husband is restrained from encumbering or disposing of Property B and Property C pending the appeal.

### Reasoning

  • The court applied the 'nugatory' principle, finding that if the husband were forced to sell unique commercial assets and subsequently won his appeal, the victory would be hollow as the assets could not be recovered.

  • Johnston J determined that the husband's appeal was 'bona fide' and raised legitimate questions regarding the trial judge's treatment of tax liabilities and asset valuations.

  • The judge balanced the husband's right to a meaningful appeal against the wife's right to the 'fruits of her judgment'.

  • A conditional stay was deemed the most equitable solution, providing the wife with $200,000 in total (payment plus costs) to alleviate her immediate needs while preserving the bulk of the asset pool.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband demonstrated that the $1,050,000 payment could not be met from liquid funds without disrupting commercial operations.

  • The wife demonstrated a genuine financial need for a portion of the funds to participate in the appeal process.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 - Rule 22.11 (Stay of proceedings)

  • Section 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Property settlement power)

  • Common law principles regarding the 'nugatory' effect of appeals (Von Riesch v Von Riesch).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The husband provided limited evidence regarding his maximum borrowing capacity against existing equity, which might have avoided the need for a stay.

  • The wife's specific budget for the appeal was estimated rather than supported by a detailed costs agreement.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Need

  • The wife argued she required immediate funds for maintenance, though this was framed within the context of the property stay application.

### Capacity to Pay

  • The husband had the capacity to pay a reduced sum ($150,000) without liquidating primary assets.

### Orders

  • No formal maintenance orders; the $150,000 payment was characterized as a partial property distribution.

### Reasoning

  • The court used the partial property payment to address the wife's financial requirements, negating the need for a separate maintenance inquiry.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's lack of liquidity was a primary driver for the $150,000 condition.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 72 (Duty to maintain)

  • s 80 (General powers of court to order lump sum payments)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of detailed financial statements regarding the wife's current weekly deficit.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case clarifies the application of the 'nugatory' principle in family law appeals involving significant illiquid assets. It establishes that a stay is a discretionary remedy that requires balancing the risk of irreversible financial harm to the appellant against the respondent's right to receive their court-ordered entitlement."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that a 'bona fide' appeal exists when there are arguable grounds regarding asset valuation and the treatment of latent tax liabilities.

  • The 'conditional stay' is an effective tool to mitigate the power imbalance between a party who controls the asset pool and a party who is reliant on the judgment for liquidity.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between Rule 22.11 of the Family Law Rules 2004 and the court's inherent jurisdiction to prevent an appeal from being rendered futile.

  • The use of s 80 powers to order partial payments as a condition of a stay.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court did not make final credibility findings, as this was an interlocutory application; however, the husband's history of financial transparency was a factor in the court's willingness to grant the stay.

  • The husband's claims of financial hardship were scrutinized against his continued control of the business entities, leading the court to conclude he had the capacity to pay at least $200,000 immediately.

  • The wife's claims of extreme financial distress were partially accepted, though the court found that $150,000 was sufficient to meet her immediate needs rather than the full $1.05M.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Stay of the 24 August 2018 property orders granted.

  • Husband to pay $150,000 to the wife within 28 days.

  • Husband to pay $50,000 for the wife's appeal costs.

  • Injunctions maintained over Property B and Property C.

  • Costs of the stay application reserved to the Full Court.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to preserve the subject matter of the litigation (the business assets) while ensuring the wife was not prejudiced by the delay in payment.

  • The court rejected the husband's request for an unconditional stay, noting that the wife should not be expected to wait indefinitely for any portion of her $1,050,000 entitlement while the husband retained control of all income-producing assets.

  • The $150,000 figure was calculated as a sufficient 'buffer' to allow the wife to secure housing and meet expenses without forcing the husband to sell the commercial properties that were the subject of the valuation dispute.

  • The $50,000 costs order was justified by the need for 'equality of arms' in the appellate process, ensuring the wife could afford competent legal representation to respond to the husband's appeal.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were in a de facto relationship with a significant dispute regarding the date of separation.

  • The Applicant claims the relationship ended in February 2018.

  • The Respondent claims the relationship ended no later than May 2015.

  • The parties lived in a property from 2012 onwards, though the nature of the de facto status during that period is contested.

  • The Respondent entered a contract to sell a property for $7.5 million with settlement scheduled for August 2018.

  • The property is subject to an encumbrance of approximately $5 million, leaving roughly $2 million in equity.

  • The Applicant alleges the sale is a 'sham' to an acquaintance of both parties.

  • The Applicant relies on a cryptic handwritten note by the Respondent regarding a 'sell and lease back' arrangement.

  • The Applicant filed material late (5 June 2018) for a hearing listed on 6 June 2018.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • DISPUTED: Property owned/controlled by Respondent - $7,500,000 (Contracted sale price)

  • DISPUTED: Encumbrance on property - $5,000,000

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Approximately $2,000,000 equity (Disputed)

### Reasoning

  • The Applicant seeks to stop the $7.5 million sale, asserting it is a sham based on a handwritten note and a solicitor's opinion regarding title issues.

  • The Respondent denies the sham allegation, stating the note was from 2012 and the sale is legitimate.

  • The court noted that stopping the sale would create significant issues with the third-party purchaser unless the sham allegation is proven.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The August settlement date creates an urgent need for a determination before the property equity is potentially dispersed.

  • The Applicant's evidence of a 'sham' sale was found to be currently insufficient to confidently halt the transaction.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 90RD Family Law Act 1975 (Declaration of de facto relationship)

  • s 44(6) Family Law Act 1975 (Leave to bring proceedings out of time)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent valuation of the property provided.

  • Inadequate compliance with subpoenas by conveyancing lawyers (only the contract was produced).

  • Lack of material evidence to support the 'sham' sale allegation beyond a cryptic note.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['The case is at a jurisdictional and procedural threshold regarding the existence of a de facto relationship and the preservation of a $7.5 million asset.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The primary battleground is the separation date (2015 vs 2018), which determines whether the court has jurisdiction to make property orders.

  • Natural justice required an adjournment because the Applicant filed critical material regarding the 'sham' sale only one day before the hearing.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 90RD (de facto declarations) and the procedural requirements for interim relief under the Family Law Rules 2004.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Applicant's credibility regarding the 'sham' sale is currently weak due to a lack of corroborating evidence beyond a 'cryptic note'.

  • The Respondent's evidence regarding the end of the relationship was described as 'inadequate' and lacking clarity.

  • The Applicant's late filing of material was a significant procedural concern that undermined the efficiency of the Judicial Duty List hearing.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge determined that the Respondent must be given an opportunity to respond to the late-filed allegations of a sham sale.

  • The court found the Respondent's own material regarding the 2015 separation date to be 'inadequate' and requiring further clarification.

  • The judge preferred to adjourn the matter to a case management judge to ensure a full hearing before the August settlement date, rather than making a rushed decision on incomplete evidence.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties have two children: B (born 2007, aged 11) and C (born 2009, aged 9).

  • A Family Report was prepared by Ms L, dated 20 November 2018, following interviews and observations conducted on 13 September 2018.

  • During the interview on 13 September 2018, the family consultant allegedly disclosed to the husband that the wife claimed they were close to settling and that the husband had accepted a 5-night-per-fortnight parenting proposal.

  • The husband, a lawyer, excused himself from the interview to seek legal advice but continued with the process thereafter.

  • Settlement negotiations had occurred between 26 July 2018 and 13 September 2018.

  • The husband filed an Application in a Case on 4 December 2018, seeking to exclude the Family Report and disqualify the writer for bias.

  • The matter was heard on 5 December 2018, which was the date originally intended for a final hearing (though not confirmed).
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Not specified - Unresolved

  • DISPUTED: Responsibility for private school fees at D School - Disputed

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated in this procedural judgment

### Future Needs

  • The husband sought a 'carve out' from paying school fees if his income fell below $250,000 per annum.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted that property settlement remains unresolved and is a central issue alongside parenting and child support.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's proposal regarding school fees was conditional on him earning a nominated level of income ($250,000).

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (implied as the basis for the ongoing property dispute).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The full asset pool and specific valuations were not before the court for this procedural application.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B (11 years old)

  • C (9 years old)

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Report by Ms L dated 20 November 2018.

### Best Interests

  • The family consultant noted it is generally beneficial for children to have continuity in their school and education.

### Orders

  • The words 'but that they had not been able to agree about school fees' in paragraph [23] of the Family Report by Ms L dated 20 November 2018, be struck out.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that only a specific phrase in paragraph [23] constituted evidence of a communication made in connection with an attempt to negotiate a settlement under s 131(1)(a) of the Evidence Act.

  • The balance of the report was based on the consultant's observations and the parties' stated positions, which were not privileged.

  • The husband's claim of bias was rejected as a fair-minded observer would not apprehend bias simply because a consultant was told about settlement progress.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The husband's delay in objecting (from 13 September to 4 December) constituted a waiver of his right to object to apprehended bias.

  • The family consultant's opinions on schooling were based on the husband's own stated position during the interview, not just the wife's disclosures.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 131(1)(a) Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) regarding settlement negotiations.

  • s 134 Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) regarding inadmissibility.

  • s 60CC(2) and (3) Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) regarding best interests.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The exact question asked by the family consultant to the husband following her talk with the wife was not clearly established in the evidence.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Capacity to Pay

  • The husband's income level (specifically the $250,000 threshold) is a factor in the ongoing dispute.

### Reasoning

  • Spousal maintenance is noted as an unresolved issue between the parties.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case clarifies the limits of settlement negotiation privilege under s 131 of the Evidence Act in the context of Family Reports and establishes strict standards for bias claims against court experts.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • A Family Report is not 'infected' in its entirety simply because a party mentions settlement negotiations to the consultant; only the specific privileged communications are inadmissible.

  • A party who is aware of grounds for a bias objection but continues with the litigation process (especially a legally trained party) may be found to have waived that objection.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 131 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) and the court's reliance on Family Reports under s 62G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

  • Application of the 'fair-minded lay observer' test from Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy to family consultants.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's credibility regarding the bias claim was undermined by his delay in bringing the application.

  • The court inferred that the husband's objection was a tactical response to the unfavorable recommendations in the Family Report rather than a genuine concern about the integrity of the process.

  • The husband's failure to raise the issue during a procedural event on 17 October 2018, while simultaneously pushing for a final hearing date, was viewed as inconsistent with a belief that the consultant was biased.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The words 'but that they had not been able to agree about school fees' in [23] of the Family Report by Ms L dated 20 November 2018, be struck out.

  • The husband’s Application in a Case filed 4 December 2018 and oral applications made by the husband on 5 December 2018 be otherwise dismissed.

  • The matter be relisted for mention on 30 January 2019 at 9.15am.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the 'Ebner' test for apprehended bias, finding that a fair-minded observer would not believe the consultant lost her impartiality merely by hearing that parties were close to settlement.

  • The court emphasized the public policy of s 131 (encouraging settlement) but held it must be applied strictly to the 'communication' itself, not to the entire expert report containing other relevant observations.

  • The husband's status as a lawyer was relevant to the finding of waiver; he knew the legal implications of the consultant's comments on 13 September 2018 but waited until 4 December 2018 to object, only after seeing the unfavorable recommendations in the report.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties entered into a religious marriage ceremony in India in early 2010.

  • Following the first marriage, the parties relocated to Australia.

  • The husband believed the Indian religious ceremony would not be legally recognized in Australia.

  • The parties entered into a second marriage ceremony at the Registry Office in Adelaide in mid 2010.

  • The parties separated in Melbourne in July 2012 and have lived apart since.

  • The husband filed an Initiating Application on 31 May 2018 seeking a decree of nullity for the second marriage and a declaration of validity for the first.

  • The husband previously filed for divorce in the Federal Circuit Court but discontinued it upon identifying the dual-marriage issue.

  • The wife was served with the application by hand on 26 June 2018 and with subsequent orders via email and post on 8 and 10 September 2018.

  • The wife did not file any response or appear at the hearings on 6 July 2018, 4 September 2018, or 16 November 2018.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case clarifies the legal status of 'repeat marriages' between the same parties under Australian law, specifically addressing the interaction between foreign marriage recognition and domestic nullity grounds."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Indian marriage was valid under local law and thus recognized in Australia per s 88C(1) of the Marriage Act 1961.

  • A second marriage ceremony between parties already lawfully married to each other is a nullity, despite not being explicitly listed as a ground in s 23B(1).

  • The court adopted a 'logic and common sense' approach to fill a legislative hiatus regarding the status of marriages entered in contravention of s 113(1)(a).

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 88G(1) of the Marriage Act 1961: Prima facie evidence of foreign marriage validity.

  • Section 113(1)(a) of the Marriage Act 1961: Prohibition of persons already legally married going through a second ceremony.

  • Section 51 of the Family Law Act 1975: Basis for a decree of nullity.

  • Section 23B of the Marriage Act 1961: Grounds for void marriages.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The husband's evidence was deemed reliable and was supported by independent legal expertise (Mr C) and documentary evidence (Indian marriage certificate).

  • The wife presented a significant procedural risk by failing to engage with the proceedings despite multiple service attempts by Mr B and Mr D.

  • The court found the wife had been afforded procedural fairness, allowing the matter to proceed on an undefended basis.

  • The husband's initial confusion regarding the legal status of religious ceremonies was treated as a genuine mistake of law that did not undermine his overall credibility.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • That the marriage entered into by Mr Duryodhana and Ms Chawla on ... 2010 in India is a valid marriage.

  • That the marriage between the applicant and the respondent conducted at Adelaide on ... 2010 is declared null and void.

  • That the files numbered MLC4657/2016 and MLC8128/2017 be consolidated.

  • That the Initiating Application filed 31 May 2018 be otherwise dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The court relied on the expert affidavit of Mr C, a lawyer with 22 years of experience in India, to confirm that the early 2010 Indian marriage certificate was evidence of a valid marriage under Indian law.

  • Justice Johns applied the precedent from Kapadia & Kapadia (1991) and Zau & Huang [2015], which established that a second ceremony between already-married persons must be void of legal effect.

  • The judge noted that while s 23B(1) of the Marriage Act purports to be an exclusive list of grounds for nullity, the express prohibition in s 113(1)(a) against repeat ceremonies necessitates a finding of nullity for the second ceremony.

  • The consolidation of files MLC4657/2016 and MLC8128/2017 was ordered to prevent administrative confusion regarding the parties' matrimonial history.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The child, B, was born in 2001 and was 16 years old at the time of the hearing.

  • Ms. Sedgley (41) and Mr. Sedgley (39) married in 2007 after meeting in June 2006.

  • Mr. Sedgley has acted as the child's father since 2006.

  • The biological father, Mr. Pollack, has not seen the child since approximately April 2006.

  • Mr. Pollack has consistently paid child support of $350 per month.

  • The child's surname was changed to Sedgley in June 2008 to align with the family unit.

  • The child requested adoption by Mr. Sedgley in late 2012 and again in mid-2016.

  • Mr. Pollack provided an affidavit supporting the adoption application after consulting with his parents.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B, born 2001 (16 years old at judgment).

### Best Interests

  • The court determined that granting leave for adoption was in the child's best interests given the total absence of the biological father for 12 years and the child's strong desire to formalize her relationship with her step-father.

### Orders

  • Pursuant to section 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), Mr. Sedgley and Ms. Sedgley are granted leave to commence adoption proceedings in respect to the child, B.

### Reasoning

  • The judge noted the child's age (16) and her long-term residence with the applicants. The biological father's consent and his 12-year absence from the child's life were decisive factors.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The biological father's support for the application was a key factual determination.

  • The child's surname change in 2008 demonstrated a long-standing integration into the Sedgley family.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Section 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (Leave for adoption).

  • Section 60CC (Best interests of the child).

  • Section 60F(4)(a) (Effect of adoption on the child's legal status).

  • Section 92 of the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld) (Requirements for step-parent adoption).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No specific evidentiary gaps were identified as the application was unopposed and supported by all relevant parties.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case serves as a clear precedent for the granting of leave under s 60G when there is a significant 'de facto' parental relationship between a step-parent and a child, coupled with the biological parent's long-term absence and consent."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The biological father's 12-year absence and his formal consent to the adoption.

  • The child's mature and consistent wishes to be legally adopted by her step-father.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The court balanced the requirements of s 60G of the Family Law Act with the practical requirements of s 92 of the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld), ensuring the federal leave was granted to facilitate the state-based adoption process.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • There were no significant credibility risks identified. The biological father, Mr. Pollack, was found to be acting in the child's best interests by consenting to the application despite his ongoing financial contribution of $350 per month.

  • The applicants' evidence regarding the child's integration into their family since 2006 was uncontested.

  • The court accepted the biological father's affidavit as a sincere reflection of his views on the child's best interests.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Pursuant to section 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) Mr Sedgley and Ms Sedgley are granted leave to commence adoption proceedings in respect to the child, B, born 2001.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The court's logic centered on the 'best interests' principle under s 60CC. The judge found that formalizing the existing family structure through adoption would provide the child with legal certainty that matched her social and emotional reality.

  • The judge gave significant weight to the child's views, noting she was 16 and had initiated the request years prior.

  • The lack of opposition from the biological father removed the typical conflict found in s 60G applications, allowing the court to focus solely on the child's welfare and identity.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in 2009, married in 2010, and separated in November 2016.

  • There are two children of the marriage: X, aged seven, and Y, aged four.

  • The father initiated proceedings in the Federal Circuit Court on 26 February 2018 seeking equal shared parental responsibility.

  • The mother filed a response on 9 April 2018 seeking sole parental responsibility and permission to relocate the children to Country B in Europe.

  • Interim consent orders were made on 17 April 2018 for the children to live with the mother and spend supervised time with the father.

  • The mother applied for an expedited final hearing (priority listing) on 7 June 2018, citing employment opportunities in Europe and family violence.

  • The matter was heard in chambers by Justice Johns in the Family Court of Australia at Melbourne.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (7 years old)

  • Y (4 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleges family violence during the marriage in the presence of the children.

  • Father denies allegations; physical separation occurred after mother obtained an interim intervention order.

### Expert Evidence

  • Father raised concerns that an expedited trial might proceed without a Family Report.

### Best Interests

  • The court noted that relocation and stability (school/kindergarten) are matters for the trial judge to determine in the children's best interests.

### Reasoning

  • The mother's application for expedition was dismissed because she failed to provide specific details regarding the terms or commencement dates of the alleged job offers in Country B.

  • The court found that while international relocation cases are often afforded priority, the current lack of judicial resources prevents priority unless there is a more urgent risk, such as child abuse.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The mother's claims of financial hardship were not supported by specific evidence of employment terms in Country B.

  • The allegations of family violence, while serious, were not deemed unique enough to bypass other cases involving child abuse or immediate risk of harm.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 r 12.10A (Expedition of first day before a Judge).

  • r 12.10A(2)(d) regarding priority to the 'possible detriment of other cases'.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No particulars provided regarding the terms of the mother's proposed employment in Country B.

  • No evidence as to when the proposed employment was to commence.

  • Absence of a Family Report at the time of the expedition application.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother alleges family violence occurred during the marriage in the presence of the children.

### Protection Orders

  • Interim intervention order obtained by the mother against the father.

### Child Exposure

  • Alleged exposure to violence during the marriage.

### Reasoning

  • The court acknowledged the allegations but determined that family violence findings are matters for the final trial and do not automatically warrant expedition given the volume of similar cases.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court noted that many cases in the system involve allegations of violence or child abuse, and this case did not demonstrate a level of urgency exceeding those involving direct risk of harm to children.

### Statutory Pivots

  • r 12.10A(4)(b) (Violence as a factor for expedition).

  • r 12.10A(4)(f) (Allegations of child abuse as a factor for expedition).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Conflicting testimony regarding the occurrence of family violence (Mother affirms, Father denies).
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This decision clarifies the high evidentiary threshold required for the expedition of family law proceedings in the Melbourne Registry, particularly in relocation cases.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court emphasized that 'freedom of movement' and employment opportunities are no longer sufficient on their own to justify priority listing due to 'ever diminishing' court resources.

  • The primary battleground for expedition was the balance between the mother's economic need to relocate and the systemic backlog of cases involving higher-risk allegations (e.g., child sexual abuse).

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 r 12.10A(2) and (4).

  • The court's interpretation of 'detriments to other cases' as a limiting factor for granting priority.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The court did not make final credibility findings but noted the mother's evidence regarding her employment was vague and lacked particulars.

  • The father's opposition was based on procedural fairness (the need for a Family Report) and the children's current status quo, which the court found reasonable.

  • The mother was found to have acted 'reasonably and without delay' in the procedural conduct of the case, despite the father's arguments to the contrary, but this was insufficient to overcome the high bar for expedition.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The mother’s application for an expedited final hearing pursuant to r 12.10A(1) of the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) be dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied a strict interpretation of r 12.10A, requiring the applicant to demonstrate a 'relevant circumstance' that outweighs the detriment to other litigants waiting for trial.

  • The mother's failure to provide specific evidence of her job offers (contracts, start dates, salary) was fatal to her claim of financial hardship under r 12.10A(4)(c).

  • The court balanced the mother's desire for relocation against the father's right to a properly prepared case (including a Family Report) and the children's current stability.

  • The overarching logic was a resource-management approach: priority is reserved for cases where the 'purpose of the case will be lost' or where there is an immediate risk of serious trauma or harm.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in January 2014 and married later that year.

  • The parties separated in September 2014, shortly after the birth of their child, X.

  • The child, X, was born in 2014 and was aged three years and five months at the time of the 2018 hearing.

  • Final consent orders were made on 20 July 2016, providing for equal shared parental responsibility and a graduated increase in the father's time.

  • In June 2017, the mother requested a resumption of supervised time, which the father initially acceded to despite objection.

  • The father filed an Initiating Application on 12 July 2017 to enforce the child's surname change to 'Kubic-Waddington' as per the 2016 orders.

  • The mother filed a Response on 4 August 2017 seeking to suspend the final orders and return to supervised contact, alleging family violence and PTSD.

  • Senior Registrar FitzGibbon made interim orders on 28 November 2017 for unsupervised and overnight time; the mother sought a review of these orders.

  • The father has spent over $11,000 on supervision services between July and November 2017 to maintain contact with the child.

  • The mother engaged a private investigator to video the father and child during contact sessions.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X (born 2014), aged 3 years and 5 months at the time of judgment.

### Safety Concerns

  • Mother alleged the child suffered psychological harm from exposure to family violence.

  • Mother alleged the father and paternal grandmother 'smacked' the child.

  • Mother alleged the child used profane language and showed aggression after unsupervised time.

### Expert Evidence

  • Ms D (Family Law Psychologist): Provided reports dated 2 July 2016 and 13 November 2017; observed a warm relationship between father and child; noted mother's anxiety impacts the child.

  • Dr E (Consultant Psychiatrist): Report dated 9 May 2016; opined mother's symptoms were adjustment disorder/anxiety rather than PTSD; warned of risk of alienation if mother is untruthful.

  • Dr F (Mother's Psychologist): Report dated 5 August 2017; noted symptoms consistent with PTSD based on mother's reports.

  • Ms G (Contact Centre Supervisor): Provided three reports; confirmed positive, child-focused play by the father and a loving bond.

  • Ms L (Therapeutic Counsellor): Parties engaged with her in early 2018 to address communication and relational dynamics.

### Best Interests

  • The court found it in the child's best interests to have a meaningful relationship with the father through unsupervised and overnight time, supported by therapeutic intervention.

### Orders

  • Until further order, the child to spend time with the father in accordance with Order 4(e) of the 20 July 2016 orders.

  • From 24 March 2018, time to include alternate weekends from 10:00am Saturday to 12:00pm Sunday.

  • From 19 May 2018, time to extend to alternate weekends from 10:00am Saturday to 5:00pm Sunday.

  • Parties to use the C Children’s Contact Centre for supervised changeovers.

  • Parties to use the MyMob parenting application for communication.

### Reasoning

  • The court found that the evidence from independent experts (Ms D and Ms G) did not support the mother's contention that the father's time should be supervised.

  • The judge noted the child's 'unsettled' behavior was likely a reaction to the mother's visible anxiety and the 'toxic' parental conflict rather than the father's parenting.

  • The father demonstrated a commitment to the child by paying over $11,000 for supervision and following expert recommendations to modify his disciplinary approach.

  • The mother's proposal for unsupervised daytime contact but no overnights was found to be inherently contradictory regarding risk assessment.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The child's use of phrases like 'I can't be here' was found to be 'adult-like' and indicative of a child who feels unsupported in his relationship with the father due to maternal influence.

  • The father's willingness to adapt his parenting style based on supervisor feedback (Ms G) weighed heavily in his favor.

  • The mother's failure to execute surname change documents as previously ordered reflected a lack of support for the father's role.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2): Balancing the benefit of a meaningful relationship against the need to protect from harm.

  • s 60CC(3): Additional considerations including the parents' attitudes and the impact of conflict.

  • s 61DA: Presumption of equal shared parental responsibility (not challenged but relevant to the graduated time approach).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court could not make final findings on the mother's PTSD diagnosis or the father's alleged violence without a full trial and cross-examination.

  • Conflicting accounts of the child's 'smacking' allegations remained untested.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Mother alleged a history of domestic violence during the relationship.

  • Mother alleged the father and paternal grandmother physically disciplined the child ('smacking').

### Child Exposure

  • The child was found to be exposed to high levels of parental conflict and the mother's anxiety regarding the father.

### Orders

  • Injunctions against filming the child or questioning the child about the proceedings.

  • Restraint on the mother taking the child to health professionals (except GPs) without consent or court order.

### Reasoning

  • The court prioritized the child's need for a relationship with the father over the mother's unsubstantiated (at an interim level) claims of risk.

  • The judge relied on Dr E's assessment that the mother's psychiatric symptoms focused specifically on the father and did not necessarily indicate a risk to the child from the father.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The private investigator's videos showed no evidence of risk, supporting the removal of supervision.

  • The mother's anxiety was identified as a primary driver of the child's distress.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: Definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b): Protection from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of independent corroboration for the mother's claims of physical violence by the father.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the court's approach to 'gatekeeping' and parental alienation allegations at an interim stage, specifically how expert evidence from contact supervisors and psychiatrists can override a primary carer's subjective claims of risk and PTSD."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the 'meaningful relationship' limb of s 60CC(2) because the 'protection from harm' limb was not supported by independent expert observations (Ms D and Ms G).

  • The mother's conduct, including hiring a private investigator and failing to change the child's surname, was viewed as evidence of her inability to support the father-child relationship.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) and (b), where the court found that the benefit of the relationship outweighed the unsubstantiated risk of harm.

  • The use of s 65DA(2) and s 62B to ensure parties understand the consequences of contravening parenting orders.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother's credibility was challenged by her inconsistent positions (agreeing to unsupervised day time but claiming the father was a risk for overnights).

  • The father was found to be generally reliable and responsive to professional guidance, evidenced by his modification of parenting techniques.

  • The mother's claim of a PTSD diagnosis was undermined by Dr E's psychiatric report, which suggested a more likely diagnosis of adjustment disorder with anxiety focused on the father.

  • There is a significant risk of the mother alienating the child if her anxiety and lack of support for the father's role are not addressed through the ordered therapy.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The mother’s Application in a Case filed 22 December 2017 is dismissed.

  • The father’s Response to Application in a Case filed 17 January 2018 is dismissed (except where it sought to maintain the Registrar's orders).

  • Specific schedule for unsupervised and overnight time for the child with the father established.

  • Parties to continue therapeutic counselling with Ms L.

  • Orders regarding the child's surname change and use of MyMob application maintained.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge applied the 'Goode & Goode' framework for interim parenting, focusing on the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.

  • The court determined that the child's distress was likely a 'learned behavior' or a reaction to the mother's anxiety rather than a result of the father's actions.

  • The judge emphasized that the father had consistently sought to follow court orders and expert advice, whereas the mother had sought to impede the relationship.

  • The transition to overnight time was deemed appropriate given the child's age (3.5 years) and the positive reports from the supervised contact period.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties separated in January 2016 after a seven-year relationship and physically separated in October 2016.

  • There are two children of the marriage: F (born 2010, aged 8) and D (born 2013, aged 5).

  • The children have remained primarily in the mother's care since separation, spending four nights per fortnight with the father under interim orders from 21 June 2017.

  • The mother and children live with the maternal aunt, who also works with the mother.

  • On 2 August 2018, the maternal aunt allegedly assaulted a lawyer at a social function while intoxicated, leading to criminal charges.

  • The mother planned a skiing holiday to the United States with the children and the maternal aunt from 5 January to 21 January 2019.

  • The father initially opposed the travel but later agreed to the trip, disputing only the duration and the children's contact with the maternal aunt.

  • The father sought an injunction to prevent the maternal aunt from having unsupervised contact with the children.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • F, born 2010 (8 years old)

  • D, born 2013 (5 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Father alleged the maternal aunt posed an 'unacceptable risk of harm' due to an assault charge involving a third party (a lawyer) in August 2018.

### Expert Evidence

  • Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) participated and proposed holiday schedules similar to the mother's.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined it was in the children's best interests to be settled with their primary carer (the mother) for the start of the school year.

  • The court found that maintaining established holiday traditions (Christmas with the mother's family and New Year's with the father's family) served the children's interests.

### Orders
