  • Children to live with the father from 10am on 20 December 2018 to 10am on 24 December 2018.

  • Children to live with the father from 10am on 28 December 2018 to 10am on 4 January 2019.

  • Children to live with the father from 10am on 22 January 2019 to 6pm on 27 January 2019.

  • Children to live with the mother from 10am on 24 December 2018 to 10am on 28 December 2018.

  • Children to live with the mother from 10am on 4 January 2019 to 10am on 22 January 2019 (covering the US trip).

  • Mother to provide a detailed itinerary and flight information 5 days prior to departure.

  • Mother to provide a mobile number for the father to contact the children at all reasonable times while overseas.

### Reasoning

  • The court rejected the father's proposal for a Christmas Day changeover at 1pm, noting it would cause practical difficulty and require the children to travel several hours by car during celebrations.

  • The court prioritized the mother's primary carer status for the youngest child's first day of school to ensure stability amidst parental acrimony.

  • The court found the father's family traditionally celebrated Christmas on the 22nd or 23rd of December, whereas the mother's family celebrated on the 25th.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father's own evidence showed his family's Christmas traditions occurred before Christmas Day, undermining his claim for time on the 25th.

  • The father's proposal for a 10am handover on the day of the mother's morning flight to the US was found to be practically impossible.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(3) - Additional considerations regarding the nature of relationships and practical difficulties.

  • s 60CC(3)(h) - The lifestyle and background of the children and parents (traditions).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The father failed to provide evidence of where he would be on Christmas Day or where the proposed 1pm changeover would occur.

  • No evidence was provided to link the maternal aunt's alleged assault on an adult to a risk of harm to the children.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged assault by maternal aunt on a lawyer on 2 August 2018 (grabbing throat, squeezing for 3-4 seconds).

### Child Exposure

  • No evidence that the children were present during the alleged assault by the maternal aunt.

### Orders

  • The father's application for an injunction restraining the mother from leaving the children unsupervised with the maternal aunt was dismissed.

### Reasoning

  • The alleged incident did not constitute 'family violence' under the Act as the victim was not a family member.

  • The father's delay in bringing the application (incident in August, application in December) suggested the concern was not genuine.

  • The father's willingness to allow the mother to supervise the aunt implied that any risk was manageable and not 'unacceptable'.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found no nexus between the aunt's behavior toward an adult and a risk of harm to the children.

  • The father's withdrawal of drug testing applications and initial opposition to travel cast doubt on the sincerity of his safety concerns.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 68B - Power of the court to grant injunctions if 'just or convenient'.

  • s 4AB - Definition of family violence (not met in this instance).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of evidence showing the maternal aunt had ever been aggressive toward the children or in their presence.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case emphasizes the court's reluctance to grant restrictive injunctions against third parties (like extended family) without a clear nexus to child safety, especially when the application appears tactical or delayed."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the stability of the primary care arrangement for significant milestones (first day of school) over equalizing time on specific days.

  • The court utilized the parties' established pre-separation and post-separation traditions to resolve holiday scheduling conflicts.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC (best interests) and s 68B (injunctive power), where the court requires a demonstrated risk to justify restraining a parent's discretion in their own home.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The father's credibility regarding safety concerns was undermined by his delay in acting on the maternal aunt's assault charge (waiting 4 months).

  • The father's pursuit and then withdrawal of drug testing orders against the mother suggested a pattern of using litigation for tactical advantage rather than genuine welfare concerns.

  • The mother was found to be the stable primary carer, and the court deferred to her judgment regarding the safety of the children's living environment.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The father's application for an injunction regarding the maternal aunt is dismissed.

  • The father's school term time with the children shall re-commence Friday 8 February 2019.

  • Specific holiday timeframes ordered for December 2018 and January 2019 as detailed in the parenting section.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the father's right to spend time with the children against the practicalities of international travel and the children's need for stability before starting school.

  • The court found that the father's proposal for Christmas Day was disruptive and lacked a basis in the family's actual traditions.

  • The dismissal of the injunction was based on the father's failure to prove that an isolated incident of violence against an adult translated to a risk for the children, combined with his delay in seeking the order.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are engaged in high-conflict litigation involving interim property and parenting disputes.

  • The Husband alleges he was the victim of two assaults, one on 23 April 2018 and another in June 2018.

  • The Husband claims he is incapacitated as a result of the June 2018 assault.

  • The children, B (aged 13) and C (aged 14), currently live with the Husband and spend no time with the Wife.

  • The Wife has sought disclosure regarding the 2016 sale of a property at N Street, Suburb O since October 2017.

  • The Husband has CCTV technology at his residence at L Street, Suburb M, which allegedly captured the April assault.

  • A final hearing is listed for 6 August 2018.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Monies held on trust by K Lawyers - [Value not specified]

  • AGREED: Proceeds from sale of N Street, Suburb O (2016) - [Value not specified]

  • AGREED: Payment to National Australia Bank (NAB) to discharge mortgage - $282,306

  • TOTAL NET POOL: [Not stated]

### Orders

  • Husband and Wife to sign all documents to release monies held on trust by K Lawyers to the Wife as a partial property settlement.

  • Husband to provide the Wife's solicitors with the settlement statement for the sale of N Street, Suburb O within 21 days.

  • Husband to provide the NAB statement reflecting the $282,306 mortgage discharge within 21 days.

### Reasoning

  • The release of trust funds was largely resolved by consent as a partial property settlement.

  • The Husband had conduct of the Queensland property sale and has had notice of the disclosure request for eight months.

  • It is within the Husband's power to obtain the settlement and bank statements directly from the National Australia Bank.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Husband's eight-month delay in providing requested financial documents necessitated a formal order for disclosure to ensure trial readiness.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The court's power to order disclosure to facilitate a just and equitable division under s 79.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Missing settlement statement for the Suburb O property.

  • Missing bank statements confirming the application of $282,306 in sale proceeds.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • B (13 years old)

  • C (14 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Children expressed fear of the Mother to the psychologist Mr J.

  • Husband alleges the Mother or her associates are responsible for physical assaults against him.

### Expert Evidence

  • Mr J (Psychologist) reported the children's fear of the Mother.

  • Ms P (Psychologist) provided a report (11 May 2017) noting that CCTV installation improved the Husband's and children's sense of safety.

### Best Interests

  • It is in the children's best interests for the Mother to be informed of their whereabouts given the Father's claimed incapacity.

### Orders

  • ICL to file and serve a report by Mr J by 20 July 2018, costs shared equally.

  • Husband to advise Wife by email/SMS if children sleep anywhere other than L Street, Suburb M, including details of the location.

  • Extensions of time granted for filing trial material: Husband by 25 June 2018, Wife by 6 July 2018, ICL by 13 July 2018.

### Reasoning

  • The court found it appropriate to ensure the Wife is informed of the children's care arrangements while the Husband claims to be incapacitated.

  • The extension of time for trial material was deemed sensible given the Husband's alleged recent assault.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Husband's claim of incapacity created a factual necessity for transparency regarding the children's actual sleeping locations.

  • The children's expressed fear of the Mother, as reported by Mr J, influenced the maintenance of the current 'no time' arrangement pending trial.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations regarding the children's safety and the benefit of a relationship with both parents.

  • s 60CC(3) additional considerations regarding the children's views and the nature of their relationship with each parent.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Uncertainty regarding the children's current daily care arrangements following the June 2018 assault.

  • The ICL report by Mr J is yet to be completed and served.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Alleged assault on Husband on 23 April 2018.

  • Alleged assault on Husband in June 2018.

### Protection Orders

  • Reference made to a brief of evidence regarding alleged breaches of an intervention order by the Wife.

### Child Exposure

  • Children are aware of the assaults and have attributed blame to the Mother, causing them fear.

### Orders

  • Husband to provide Wife's solicitors with CCTV footage/recordings from the L Street property relating to the 23 April 2018 assault and its aftermath within 21 days.

### Reasoning

  • There is significant distrust between the parties regarding whether the assaults occurred and who was responsible.

  • CCTV footage is likely to shed light on a critical factual issue for the final determination of the case.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The existence of CCTV footage was confirmed by the Husband's own statements to police and the psychologist Ms P's report.

  • The court identified the assault allegations as a 'pivotal factual issue' that could determine the parenting outcome.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) the need to protect children from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The CCTV footage has not yet been produced or reviewed by the Wife or the Court.

  • Conflicting accounts of the assaults (Husband's allegation vs. Wife's denial).
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This interim judgment focuses on ensuring procedural fairness and trial readiness by compelling disclosure and the production of objective evidence (CCTV) in a high-conflict matter involving allegations of violence and financial non-disclosure.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that the Husband's control over financial documents and CCTV evidence required mandatory orders to overcome a history of non-compliance and distrust.

  • The Husband's alleged incapacity necessitated a temporary information-sharing order regarding the children's whereabouts to balance safety concerns with the Mother's right to know the children's location.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC (best interests/safety) and the court's procedural powers to manage evidence and disclosure before a final hearing under s 79.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Husband faces a credibility risk due to his eight-month delay in providing standard financial documents (settlement statements) that were within his power to obtain.

  • There is a significant credibility contest regarding the alleged assaults; the Husband's claims of incapacity are countered by the Wife's total denial of the incidents.

  • The children's alignment with the Father and their fear of the Mother (as reported by Mr J) presents a risk of parental alienation or significant psychological distress that the court must investigate via the ICL report.

  • The Husband's previous reliance on CCTV to prove the Wife's breaches of intervention orders makes his current possession of such footage a critical point of evidentiary accountability.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Release of trust funds to the Wife as partial property settlement.

  • Husband to provide settlement statement for N Street and NAB mortgage discharge statement within 21 days.

  • Husband to provide CCTV footage of the April 2018 assault within 21 days.

  • Husband to notify Wife of children's sleeping locations if away from the primary residence.

  • ICL to file Mr J's report by 20 July 2018; costs shared.

  • Trial material filing dates extended for all parties and the ICL.

  • Paragraph 1 of the Amended Application consolidated with final orders hearing on 6 August 2018; other interim paragraphs dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the 'trial setting' by granting extensions of time while simultaneously forcing the production of long-overdue financial documents.

  • The court adopted a pragmatic approach to the safety allegations by ordering the production of CCTV footage, which serves as an independent evidentiary source to resolve the 'he-said-she-said' dispute regarding the assaults.

  • The notification order regarding the children's sleeping arrangements was a targeted response to the Husband's claim of incapacity, ensuring the Wife is not left in the dark about the children's safety without prematurely altering the current living arrangements.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The proceedings involve a final property adjustment between the husband and wife, which was adjourned part-heard on 6 February 2018.

  • On 26 March 2018, the court ordered the wife to file an amended application if she intended to join additional parties.

  • On 4 May 2018, the wife filed a Further Further Amended Application joining the second and third respondents, seeking orders under s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975 to set aside transactions.

  • The wife failed to comply with multiple court orders (dated 18 May 2018 and 21 August 2018) requiring her to provide particulars of the s 106B claim and an affidavit supporting the joinder.

  • On 21 September 2018, due to the wife's non-compliance, the second and third respondents were discharged as parties to the proceedings.

  • The second and third respondents sought costs against the wife and her legal representative on an indemnity basis.

  • The wife and her legal representative did not file any submissions in response to the costs application.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Not stated in this discrete costs judgment.

  • DISPUTED: Not stated in this discrete costs judgment.

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted that the joinder of the second and third respondents was related to a s 106B application to set aside transactions, but the merits of the property claim were not determined in this costs judgment.

### Reasoning

  • The wife sought to join the second and third respondents under s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975.

  • The wife failed to comply with Rule 6.03(3) of the Family Law Rules 2004, which requires an affidavit setting out facts supporting the addition of parties.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's failure to provide particularity for the s 106B orders sought led to the discharge of the third parties.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 106B Family Law Act 1975 (setting aside transactions)

  • Rule 6.03(3) Family Law Rules 2004 (requirements for joining parties)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife failed to file the required affidavit setting out the facts relied upon to support the joinder of the second and third respondents.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This judgment clarifies the application of s 117 costs orders when a party fails to comply with procedural directions for joining third parties and emphasizes the high threshold for costs against legal practitioners.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife was 'wholly unsuccessful' against the second and third respondents as they were discharged due to her procedural defaults.

  • The wife ignored settlement offers made on 8 June 2018 and 18 July 2018 to abandon the claim with no order as to costs.

  • The court found no evidence of 'improper or unreasonable conduct' by the wife's lawyer to justify a wasted costs order under Rule 19.10.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 117(1) General rule that parties bear their own costs.

  • s 117(2) and (2A) Discretion to award costs based on conduct and success.

  • Rule 19.18 Family Law Rules 2004 regarding the method of calculating costs.

  • Rule 19.10 Family Law Rules 2004 regarding costs against lawyers.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility and reliability were undermined by her persistent failure to comply with court directions and her failure to file submissions in the costs application.

  • The court noted the wife's legal representative did not provide evidence or submissions, but the burden of proof for 'improper conduct' under Rule 19.10 was not met by the respondents.

  • The court found the second and third respondents' quantification of costs to be reasonable and supported by a costs agreement as required by Rule 19.18(3).
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The wife pay the costs of the Second and Third Respondents in the sum of $5,959.25 within two months from 19 December 2018.

  • The application for costs against the wife's legal representative is dismissed.

  • Otherwise, the application for costs be dismissed.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The court determined that 'circumstances justify' a costs order under s 117(2) because the wife failed to comply with multiple court orders and was entirely unsuccessful in her joinder application.

  • The court applied the factors in s 117(2A), specifically noting the wife's conduct in relation to pleadings and her failure to accept reasonable settlement offers.

  • Regarding the quantum, the court accepted the sum of $5,959.25 as 'most reasonable' and fixed it as a lump sum to avoid the delay of formal taxation, citing Beach Petroleum NL v Johnson.

  • The court rejected the application for indemnity costs, noting that such orders are a 'very great departure from the normal standard' and the circumstances did not meet the high threshold of misconduct or ulterior motive described in Colgate-Palmolive.

  • The court refused to make the wife's legal representative jointly liable, ruling that a lawyer is not liable for costs simply because a client's claim is 'doomed to fail' (Lemoto v Able Technical Pty Ltd).

  • The judge emphasized the need for caution in 'hindsight analysis' when evaluating a solicitor's conduct in promoting a client's cause.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties met in mid-2006 and commenced cohabitation shortly thereafter.

  • The Mother had a child, W (born 2002), from a previous relationship.

  • The parties have two children: X (born 2007) and Y (born 2009).

  • The Father had a chronic history of heroin, marijuana, and amphetamine addiction throughout the relationship.

  • The Father attempted suicide in November 2007 in the presence of child W.

  • The parties separated finally in late February 2011 following the Father's relapse into drug use.

  • In March 2011, a 12-month Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (ADVO) was made against the Father.

  • In May 2012, child W (then aged 9) alleged sexual abuse by the Father; the Father was later found not guilty in a criminal trial in late 2013.

  • The children X and Y have had no contact with the Father since April 2012.

  • The Father has an extensive criminal record including dishonesty, drug possession, weapons offences, and break-and-enter, with multiple terms of imprisonment between 2012 and 2015.

  • The Father re-partnered with Ms. F in 2015 and has a child, Z (born 2016).

  • The children have maintained a relationship with the Paternal Grandmother, Ms. B Cantor.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X: 11 years old (born 2007)

  • Y: 9 years old (born 2009)

  • W: 16 years old (Mother's child from previous relationship)

  • Z: 2 years old (Father's child with Ms. F)

### Safety Concerns

  • Father's history of chronic drug addiction and criminal offending.

  • Psychological impact on the Mother and child W if the Father is reintroduced to the household dynamic.

  • Father's Hepatitis C status and lack of treatment.

  • Risk of Father relapsing into illicit drug use.

### Expert Evidence

  • Family Consultant Ms. Q provided reports in 2014 and 2017.

  • Ms. Q identified that the Mother's genuine belief in the abuse of W created a risk of 'emotional dysfunction' in the household if contact was forced.

  • Ms. Q recommended 'recognition time' rather than 'relationship building' time to manage risks.

### Best Interests

  • Determined that a limited 'recognition' relationship with the Father, supervised by the Paternal Grandmother, was in the children's best interests to prevent idealization of the missing parent and maintain cultural links.

### Orders

  • Mother to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Children to live with the Mother.

  • Father to spend time with children 4 times per year (March, June, August, November) for 2 hours (increasing to 3 hours after 12 months).

  • Time to be supervised by Paternal Grandmother, Ms. B Cantor, at her residence.

  • Father permitted to provide up to 2 presents per child for birthdays and Christmas via the Grandmother.

  • Mother authorized to change children's surnames to 'Drummond'.

  • Mother authorized to apply for passports without Father's consent.

  • Mother to engage children in therapeutic psychological services.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted due to family violence and the Father's historical conduct.

  • The court balanced the benefit of a meaningful relationship (s 60CC(2)(a)) against the need to protect the children from psychological harm (s 60CC(2)(b)).

  • The judge found that while the Father had made progress since 2015, his 'egocentric' nature and history required a cautious, supervised approach.

  • The court prioritized the stability of the Mother's household, noting that her parenting capacity would be compromised by high anxiety if contact was more frequent or unsupervised.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Father's rehabilitation since 2015 was noted but viewed with caution due to his lack of ongoing support engagement.

  • The Mother's belief in the abuse of W was found to be genuine, even if the criminal standard of proof was not met, creating a real risk of psychological harm to the household unit.

  • The children's Indigenous heritage (O mob) was a factor in maintaining the paternal family connection.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) (protection from harm) given greater weight than s 60CC(2)(a) (meaningful relationship).

  • s 61DA presumption rebutted by findings of family violence.

  • s 60CC(3)(h) (Indigenous culture) supported ongoing contact with the Paternal Grandmother.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Inconclusive evidence regarding the sexual abuse allegations due to inconsistent police interviews and time delays.

  • Father's true income and employment status were evasive during testimony.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Capacity to Pay

  • Father pays $35.40 per month in child support plus small arrears payments.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Father's suicide attempt in 2007 in front of child W.

  • Father's drug-induced aggression and incoherent behavior (e.g., standing in an esky in underwear with a hose).

  • Father's 2011 charges for being armed with an extendable baton and hunting knife.

  • Theft of Mother's and Paternal Grandmother's property to fund drug use.

### Protection Orders

  • 12-month ADVO granted in March 2011.

### Child Exposure

  • Child W witnessed the Father's suicide attempt and drug-related incidents.

  • Subject children were exposed to the Father's drug-affected states prior to 2012.

### Orders

  • Injunction restraining Father from approaching Mother's residence, school, or workplace (Order 4).

  • Father to undertake random urinalysis drug screening at the request of the ICL (Order 13).

### Reasoning

  • The Father's history of violence and drug use necessitated strict protective boundaries to ensure the physical and psychological safety of the children and the Mother.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Father's past behavior was 'aberrant and reprehensible'.

  • The risk of relapse into drug use remains a lifelong concern for the Father.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB (definition of family violence).

  • s 68B (injunctive power for personal protection).

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of recent drug testing results prior to the final hearing led to the inclusion of random testing orders.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["The case establishes the threshold for 'recognition time' in instances where a long-term absence and a history of severe drug use/criminality make a standard meaningful relationship impossible or unsafe. It highlights the court's willingness to protect the primary carer's psychological stability as a prerequisite for the children's well-being."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that forcing a relationship beyond 'recognition time' would likely cause the Mother's household to become 'emotionally dysfunctional'.

  • The Father's recent stability (2015-2018) was sufficient to avoid a 'no-contact' order but insufficient to grant unsupervised or frequent time.

  • The Paternal Grandmother's neutrality and good relationship with the Mother were essential to the feasibility of the supervised time orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) and (b), where the 'protection from harm' mandate effectively capped the 'meaningful relationship' objective at a minimal level.

  • Rebuttal of s 61DA presumption based on a history of family violence and drug-related instability.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Father was found to be 'egocentric' and 'self-focused', with his evidence on employment and income described as 'evasive'.

  • The Mother was viewed as a reliable and protective parent, though her belief in the sexual abuse of W was noted as a significant driver of her anxiety.

  • The Father's partner, Ms. F, was found to be 'responsive and thoughtful', though her own history of drug and alcohol issues necessitated caution.

  • The Paternal Grandmother was described as an 'impressive witness' whose cultural knowledge and reliability were central to the court's trust.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Sole parental responsibility to the Mother.

  • Children to live with the Mother.

  • Supervised time for the Father 4 times per year.

  • Injunctions against the Father regarding the Mother's home and school.

  • Children's surnames changed to Drummond.

  • Passport application rights granted to the Mother.

  • ICL appointment continued for 12 months.

  • Father to undergo random urinalysis drug testing.

  • Costs submissions invited from the ICL.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to provide the children with 'identity' and 'truth' regarding their Father without destabilizing their primary residence.

  • The court determined that a 'no-time' order might lead the children to idealize the Father, whereas limited supervised contact allows them to form a realistic view of him as they mature.

  • The 14-year-old age threshold was discussed as a point where children might independently seek out a parent, justifying a structured reintroduction now.

  • The name change and passport orders were granted to reflect the reality of the children's lives and the Father's long-term absence and minimal financial contribution.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties separated in 2007 after a five-year relationship.

  • There are three children: X (born 2003), Y (born 2004), and Z (born 2005).

  • The litigation history spans 11 years with multiple consent orders (2007, 2008, 2014) and five Family Consultant reports.

  • The Mother is in a long-term relationship with Mr. Spence (aged 29), who has an intellectual disability and a history of aggressive behavior.

  • The Father is in a relationship with Ms. C (aged 32), whose five other children are all in foster care.

  • In April 2017, the child Y ran away and expressed suicidal ideation, leading to hospitalisation and Departmental intervention.

  • At the time of trial, X lived with the Mother, Y lived with the Paternal Grandparents, and Z lived with the Father.

  • The Father conceded he preferred the children to live with the Paternal Grandparents as he struggled to manage their care.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X: 15 years old (born 2003)

  • Y: 13 years old (born 2004)

  • Z: 12 years old (born 2005)

### Safety Concerns

  • Mr. Spence (Mother's partner) used corporal punishment and has significant anger management issues.

  • Ms. C (Father's partner) is deemed a risk to children; her own children are in state care.

  • Father's immaturity and history of volatile confrontations (e.g., spear gun incident).

### Expert Evidence

  • Five Family Consultant reports over 11 years.

  • Family Consultant expressed concern regarding the 'fragmentation of the siblings'.

  • Medical certificates for Ms. C regarding her inability to attend court.

### Best Interests

  • Stability and protection from the volatile dynamics of the parents' partners.

  • Maintaining sibling relationships through overlapping visitation.

  • Respecting the maturity and clear views of the eldest child, X.

### Orders

  • X to live with Mother; Mother to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Y to live with Paternal Grandparents; Grandparents to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Z to live with Paternal Grandparents; Grandparents to have sole parental responsibility.

  • Y to spend alternate weekends and half school holidays with Mother.

  • Z to spend alternate Saturdays (day only) with Mother to coincide with Y's time.

  • Father's time with Y and Z as agreed with Paternal Grandparents.

  • Changeovers to occur at McDonald's, Suburb B.

  • Restraints on Mr. Spence and Ms. C being in the presence of Y and Z unsupervised.

### Reasoning

  • The presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was rebutted due to the total breakdown in communication and high conflict.

  • X's maturity and strong views justified her remaining with the Mother despite the risks posed by Mr. Spence.

  • The Father's inability to provide full-time care necessitated Y and Z living with the Grandparents to ensure stability and safety.

  • Splitting the siblings was necessary because Y and Z required a level of supervision and protection from Mr. Spence that X did not.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Father's admission that he told Z it was better to live with the Grandparents.

  • Mr. Spence's admission of using corporal punishment and his history of 'anger anxiety'.

  • The finding that the Grandparents were the most likely to ensure compliance with court orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations (meaningful relationship vs protection from harm).

  • s 60CC(3)(a) views of the children (weighted heavily for X).

  • s 61DA rebuttal of the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • No independent medical evidence regarding the Father's self-diagnosed autism.

  • Conflicting accounts of the incident where Y ran away and alleged the Grandmother denigrated the Mother.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Father attended Mother's home with 8-10 friends and a spear gun to confront Mr. Spence.

  • Mr. Spence brandished a baseball bat and axe during a neighborhood dispute.

  • Mr. Spence used a spatula to hit the children as discipline.

### Protection Orders

  • Final ADVOs made 22 June 2017 for the protection of the Father and Ms. C from Mr. Spence.

### Child Exposure

  • Children exposed to 'overt and subtle criticism' of family members by all adults.

  • Z witnessed/participated in a physical altercation with Mother and Mr. Spence over an Xbox.

### Orders

  • Father restrained from contact with Mr. Spence.

  • Changeovers at a neutral public location (McDonald's).

  • Intervener to provide Mr. Spence with a referral to a family service.

### Reasoning

  • The court found a high level of mutual hostility and a history of reactive violence between the male figures in the children's lives.

  • The safety of the children required strict supervision of the parents' partners due to their respective histories.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The ADVOs against Mr. Spence established a pattern of aggressive behavior that necessitated protective restraints.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2)(b) the need to protect children from physical or psychological harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of specific findings on the severity of the 'carpet burns' Z allegedly suffered during the Xbox incident.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the court's willingness to split a sibling group and grant parental responsibility to grandparents when both parents' households are compromised by volatile partners and a long-term inability to co-parent."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The total breakdown of the parental relationship over 11 years rendered equal shared parental responsibility impossible.

  • The 'fragmentation of siblings' was a necessary evil to balance the eldest child's autonomy against the younger children's need for protection from a volatile step-father.

  • The Paternal Grandparents were identified as the only parties capable of providing a stable, conflict-free environment for the younger children.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC(3)(a) (child's views) and s 60CC(2)(b) (protection from harm) led to the divergent orders for X versus Y and Z.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother was found to be generally loving but overwhelmed and unable to protect the children from Mr. Spence's discipline.

  • The Father was viewed as immature and evasive, particularly regarding his capacity to care for the children without his parents.

  • Mr. Spence was found to be a significant risk factor due to his lack of insight into his aggressive behavior.

  • The Paternal Grandparents were viewed as intelligent and capable but were criticized for potentially undermining the children's relationship with the Mother.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All prior parenting orders discharged.

  • X to live with Mother (Sole Parental Responsibility).

  • Y and Z to live with Paternal Grandparents (Sole Parental Responsibility).

  • Specific visitation schedules for Mother with Y and Z.

  • Restraints on partners (Mr. Spence and Ms. C) regarding unsupervised time.

  • Ban on corporal punishment.

  • ICL to explain orders to the children.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge prioritized the children's safety and stability over the traditional preference for children to live with a parent.

  • The Mother's partner, Mr. Spence, was a primary risk factor due to his intellectual disability and aggressive discipline, which Y and Z could not manage but X could.

  • The Father's partner, Ms. C, posed a risk to children, and the Father's own dependency on his parents made him an unsuitable primary carer.

  • The Grandparents were the 'rescuers' who, despite some denigration of the Mother, offered the most reliable care for the younger children.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced a relationship in early 2014 and separated on 6 June 2015.

  • The child, X, was born in late 2014 and was nearly four years old at the time of the trial.

  • The mother is a New Zealand citizen who has lived in Melbourne for many years; her primary support network (parents and best friend) resides in City B, New Zealand.

  • The father is a consultant who has been primarily based in Sydney since early 2015, working on a major project with an annual income of approximately $413,800.

  • The mother was a consultant in Melbourne with an income of approximately $300,000, though her contract ended on 30 September 2018.

  • The mother suffered significant health issues in late 2015, requiring surgery, during which her mother travelled from New Zealand to provide sole care for the child.

  • The father rented a property in Melbourne in June 2018 and furnished it by August 2018 to oppose the relocation application.

  • The mother applied to relocate the child's residence from Melbourne, Australia to City B, New Zealand.

  • A Family Report was prepared by Ms E, a family consultant, on 28 December 2017, recommending against relocation.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • X, born in 2014 (3 years and 11 months at judgment).

### Safety Concerns

  • The court noted this was a rare case with no issues of abuse, neglect, or family violence.

### Expert Evidence

  • Ms E (Family Consultant) recommended against relocation, suggesting the father relocate to Melbourne and the child spend 5 nights per fortnight with him.

  • Ms E expressed concern that the mother had 'limited insight' into the impact of geographical separation on the father-child bond.

### Best Interests

  • The court found it was in the child's best interests to relocate with the mother to ensure her happiness and contentment as the primary carer, which directly impacts the child's wellbeing.

### Orders

  • Equal shared parental responsibility (by consent).

  • The child shall primarily live with the mother.

  • The mother is permitted to relocate the child from Melbourne to City B, New Zealand on or after 31 January 2019.

  • The father to spend time in block periods: one 4-day/3-night block every 2 months in New Zealand.

  • The mother to bring the child to Sydney for three 5-day/4-night blocks per year.

  • Half of all school holidays (Terms 1, 2, 3, and Summer) to be spent with the father.

  • Facilitation of telephone/FaceTime communication at least twice per week.

### Reasoning

  • The judge departed from the Family Consultant's recommendation, finding the consultant underestimated the mother's isolation and the father's 'Sydney-centric' history.

  • The court found the father's move to Melbourne was tactical and 'tenuous', occurring only months before the trial.

  • The mother's happiness and psychological welfare are critical to the child's development (citing East & Loewe [2015]).

  • The mother has a proven track record of facilitating the father's relationship despite the distance between Sydney and Melbourne, suggesting she will continue to do so from New Zealand.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The father prioritised work over parenting for 3.5 years, staying in Sydney while the mother struggled as a sole carer in Melbourne.

  • The mother's support network in Melbourne was 'of limited compass' compared to her extensive family and friend network in City B.

  • The child has a 'secure attachment' to the father that was developed while they lived in different cities, proving the relationship can flourish despite distance.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(a) - Benefit of meaningful relationship with both parents.

  • s 60CC(3)(d) - Likely effect of changes in circumstances (mother's happiness).

  • s 65DAA(2) - Substantial and significant time considerations.

  • s 92 Constitution - Freedom of movement considerations.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The Family Consultant's report was nearly a year old at the time of the hearing.

  • The father had not made formal job applications in Melbourne despite claiming a desire to live there permanently.
summary_sections.spousal_maintenance:
### Income & Expenses

  • Mother's former income: $300,000/year.

  • Father's income: $413,800/year ($2,400/day as a contractor).

### Orders

  • No spousal maintenance orders made.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Reasoning

  • The court explicitly found no issues of family violence or safety concerns.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC(2)(b) - Protection from harm (found not applicable).
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case reinforces the principle that relocation is not a 'special category' of case but a standard best-interests evaluation where the primary carer's psychological wellbeing is a heavy factor."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritised the 'happiness and contentment' of the primary carer (Mother) over the Family Consultant's recommendation for geographic proximity.

  • The father's late-stage attempt to establish a Melbourne residence was viewed as a litigation tactic rather than a genuine long-term commitment, given his 'Sydney-centric' career and family ties.

  • The child's existing secure attachment to the father, maintained despite the father living in Sydney for most of the child's life, served as evidence that the relationship could survive international relocation.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 60CC(2)(a) (meaningful relationship) and s 60CC(3)(d) (effect of change).

  • Application of s 65DAA regarding the reasonable practicability of substantial and significant time in an international context.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The mother was found to be a 'careful and thoughtful witness' whose evidence was 'generally frank and reliable'.

  • The father was found to be 'generally truthful' but prone to 'dissembling and obfuscation' regarding his commitment to living in Melbourne.

  • The father's complaints about the mother's parenting (e.g., childcare days, medical appointments) were characterized as 'facile' and 'fabricated' for the purpose of litigation.

  • The Family Consultant's evidence was questioned because she had not observed the parties' tested evidence over the full trial period and her report was nearly a year old.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • All previous parenting orders discharged.

  • Mother permitted to relocate child to City B, New Zealand on or after 31 January 2019.

  • Equal shared parental responsibility ordered by consent.

  • Specific travel and communication regime established for the father's time with the child.

  • Neither party to move the child's residence from City B without consent or court order.

  • All other extant applications dismissed.

  • Certification that it was reasonable to engage senior counsel.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge balanced the father's right to a relationship with the child against the mother's right to freedom of movement and her need for a support network.

  • The court found that forcing the mother to remain in Melbourne, where she felt isolated and overwhelmed, would negatively impact her parenting capacity and, by extension, the child's best interests.

  • The father's high income ($413k+) made the financial burden of international travel for contact 'not a significant concern', facilitating a robust long-distance parenting plan.

  • The court rejected the Family Consultant's view that the mother lacked insight, finding instead that the mother had been the one 'doing the hard yards' and facilitating the father's bond for years.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The Mother seeks international relocation of the children to Country B.

  • The trial was listed to commence on 10 December 2018 in Townsville with an estimated hearing time of five days.

  • Mid-trial, the family report writer, Ms. C, testified that she required psychiatric evidence of both parents to properly assist the Court.

  • The Father disclosed in his affidavit (filed 16 November 2018) a history of protracted childhood sexual abuse by a teacher from age 10, involving alcohol and drug grooming.

  • The Mother alleges the Father has continued problems with illicit drug use in his adult life and during the relationship.

  • The paternal grandmother's affidavit (16 November 2018) described the children as an 'anchor' and 'stabilising influence' for the Father, expressing concern he would not cope if they relocated.

  • The Mother applied for an adjournment of the trial; the Father and the Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) opposed the application.

  • The proceedings commenced in July 2017 and were transferred to the Family Court on 3 October 2017.

  • The Father is privately funding the litigation, while the Mother's funding for psychiatric assessments is uncertain.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Safety Concerns

  • Exposure to family violence during and after the relationship

  • Father's history of childhood sexual abuse and grooming

  • Father's alleged adult illicit drug use

  • Father's psychiatric stability and potential for relapse if relocation is granted

### Expert Evidence

  • Ms. C (Family Report Writer) testified that psychiatric assessments of both parents are a necessary precondition to her formulating recommendations for the Court.

### Best Interests

  • The Court must balance the benefit of meaningful relationships against the need to protect children from harm under s 60CC.

### Reasoning

  • The Court cannot determine the children's best interests without the psychiatric evidence requested by the family report writer.

  • Psychiatric health is a central factor in assessing the risk parents pose to the children's emotional health.

  • The potential prejudice of delay to the Mother's relocation claim is outweighed by the need for a complete evidentiary basis for final orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The paternal grandmother's affidavit (16 November 2018) suggested the Father might not cope or communicate with children if they relocate, identifying them as his 'anchor'.

  • The Father failed to disclose his childhood trauma to the family report writer during interviews, only revealing it in his affidavit.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC best interests considerations

  • s 60CC(2) primary considerations regarding meaningful relationships and protection from harm

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of psychiatric reports for both parents

  • Uncertainty regarding Legal Aid funding for the required assessments

  • Conflicting evidence regarding the Father's adult drug use
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Common ground that children were exposed to arguments and conduct satisfying the definition of family violence during and after the relationship.

### Child Exposure

  • Children were exposed to parental arguments and conduct during and after the relationship.

### Reasoning

  • Psychiatric assessments are required to inform the risk assessment regarding exposure to violence and emotional harm.

### Pivotal Findings

  • Father's history of childhood grooming and abuse is relevant to his current psychiatric health and risk profile.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB definition of family violence

  • s 60CG obligations regarding family violence findings

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Lack of independent psychiatric evaluation to determine the impact of past trauma on current parenting safety.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This judgment underscores the Court's priority on comprehensive expert evidence over procedural speed in relocation matters involving complex mental health histories and family violence."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The Court found that the interests of justice required an adjournment because the family report writer (Ms. C) could not fulfill her duty to the Court without psychiatric assessments of the parents.

  • The Court noted the 'counterintuitive' positions where the Mother sought delay despite her relocation claim, and the Father opposed it despite the evidence potentially supporting his case.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC best interests mandate

  • s 60CG court obligations regarding family violence findings
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Father's credibility is potentially compromised by his failure to disclose significant childhood trauma to the family report writer during interviews, despite including it in his affidavit.

  • The Father's intemperate communications about the ICL and the report writer suggest a potential lack of insight or emotional regulation issues.

  • There is a significant litigation risk regarding the funding of the court-ordered psychiatric assessments, as neither party has clear resources and Legal Aid funding is uncertain.

  • The Mother's willingness to accept delay in her relocation application to ensure proper evidence was presented suggests a focus on the children's best interests.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The further hearing of the trial is adjourned to 9:30am on 17 June 2019 in Townsville with an estimated hearing time of 4 days.

  • The matter is listed for a Trial Management Hearing at 2:15pm on 21 May 2019 by Global Meet teleconference.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The primary logic was that the Court cannot make final parenting orders in the children's best interests if the primary expert (Ms. C) feels unable to provide recommendations due to missing psychiatric data.

  • The Judge balanced the 6-month delay against the necessity of assessing the Father's psychiatric health, especially given the grandmother's evidence that the children are his 'anchor' and he might not cope with their relocation.

  • The Court determined that while delay is generally decried, it is not determinative when the safety and emotional health of children are at stake and require expert assessment.

  • The Judge noted that the Father's history of childhood abuse and alleged drug use required professional psychiatric context to properly evaluate the risk of harm under s 60CC.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties are engaged in Family Law property proceedings following the breakdown of their marriage.

  • The husband’s parents were joined as interveners in the proceedings.

  • A dispute arose regarding the appointment of a single expert witness to value specific real property and water rights.

  • On 13 December 2017, the court directed solicitors to agree on a single expert or provide brief written submissions.

  • The wife filed six pages of submissions and an affidavit on 19 December 2017 with ten pages of annexures.

  • The husband filed nine pages of submissions and an affidavit with twenty-one pages of annexures.

  • The court found the volume of material filed for a procedural expert appointment to be disproportionate and unnecessary.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Land, building, and water rights at C Street, D Town, New South Wales - Value TBD

  • AGREED: Land, building, and water rights at E Street, D Town, New South Wales - Value TBD

  • DISPUTED: Valuation costs - $12,100 (Mr G) vs $19,250 (Mr H)

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not yet determined

### Just & Equitable

  • The court determined that appointing the more cost-effective qualified expert was just and equitable to preserve the parties' funds.

### Orders

  • B Valuers be appointed as a single expert witness to value land, buildings, and water rights at C Street and E Street, D Town, NSW.

  • The husband to provide access for the expert to the properties.

  • Parties to issue a joint letter of instruction within 21 days.

  • Costs of the single expert report to be paid by F Pty Ltd.

  • Solicitors are restrained from charging their respective clients legal fees for the preparation and filing of submissions and affidavits regarding the expert appointment.

### Reasoning

  • Both proposed valuers (Mr G and Mr H) were found to be sufficiently qualified.

  • The court preferred Mr G (B Valuers) because his fee of $12,100 was approximately $7,000 less than Mr H's fee of $19,250.

  • The court applied the principle of proportionality, finding the extensive affidavit material filed by both parties to be excessive for a simple procedural issue.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The $7,000 price difference between the two qualified experts was the primary factor in the selection of Mr G.

  • The legal work performed by the solicitors in preparing the submissions was deemed 'well beyond what was necessary or proportionate'.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules regarding the appointment of a single expert witness.

  • The court's inherent power to manage costs and ensure litigation is conducted proportionately.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The actual market value of the properties and water rights remains unknown pending the expert report.

  • The court noted it did not invite affidavit material for this procedural step, rendering much of the filed evidence redundant.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case serves as a significant procedural warning regarding the 'over-lawyering' of simple interim issues and the court's willingness to use cost-restraint orders to enforce proportionality."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found that filing nearly 15 pages of submissions and over 30 pages of affidavit material for a valuer appointment was a breach of the duty to conduct litigation efficiently.

  • The selection of an expert can be determined primarily on cost when qualifications are equal.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) regarding expert witnesses and the overarching purpose of the rules to facilitate the just resolution of disputes as quickly and inexpensively as possible.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • While the credibility of the parties' substantive claims was not assessed, the court implicitly criticized the professional judgment of the legal practitioners for both sides.

  • The excessive filing of documents suggested a lack of cooperation or an overly adversarial approach to a standard procedural step.

  • The husband's solicitor was specifically noted for asserting the judge should read a series of documents that the judge deemed unnecessary.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Appointment of B Valuers as single expert for NSW properties.

  • Costs of the expert to be borne by F Pty Ltd.

  • Restraint on solicitors from charging fees for the specific submissions and affidavits related to the valuer appointment due to lack of proportionality.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge emphasized that the court did not invite affidavit material for the appointment of a valuer and that the material provided was 'well beyond what was necessary'.

  • The decision to restrain solicitors from charging fees acts as a punitive measure to protect the parties' asset pool from being depleted by disproportionate legal costs.

  • The court balanced the need for expert evidence with the need for financial economy, choosing the valuer who offered a $7,000 saving for the parties.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in December 2007 and married in 2008.

  • The parties have four children: D (born 2008), E (born 2009), B and C (born 2012).

  • The parties separated in February 2011 and divorced on 29 June 2014.

  • Consent orders were made in 2013 for equal shared parental responsibility and a week-about arrangement for the boys.

  • Interim orders were made on 7 July 2016 continuing equal time for the boys and increasing time for the girls.

  • Between February and May 2016, the children were separated (boys with father, girls with mother) due to mutual allegations of abuse and neglect.

  • The Mother applied to suspend the 2016 orders, seeking supervised time for the Father based on new allegations of sexualized behavior and drug use.

  • The Father cross-applied for the children to live with him, alleging the Mother was coaching the children and fabricating allegations for financial gain.

  • A final trial was scheduled for 3 April 2018.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • D (born 2008 - 9 years old)

  • E (born 2009 - 8 years old)

  • B (born 2012 - 5 years old)

  • C (born 2012 - 5 years old)

### Safety Concerns

  • Allegations of Father drawing a 'smiley face' on his penis and showing children.

  • Allegation of Father inserting a finger into B's bottom while applying cream.

  • Allegation of Father inhaling powder through a 'popper straw' witnessed by D.

  • Allegation of Father causing bruising to D.

  • Mother's history of alcohol abuse (noted as in remission).

### Expert Evidence

  • Mr. F (Family Report Writer): Noted intense parental conflict; recommended children live with Mother to provide stability and respite.

  • Dr. DD (Psychiatrist): Identified Father as having narcissistic and obsessional traits, seeing things only from his perspective.

  • Ms. EE (Psychologist): Reported children's disclosures of sexualized behavior and drug use by Father.

  • Dr. CC (Psychiatrist): Agreed by parties to assess D for Autism Spectrum Disorder.

### Best Interests

  • The court determined the children's best interests required 'respite' from the ongoing, unrelenting conflict between the parents.

### Orders

  • The children to live with the Mother.

  • The Father to spend supervised time with the children every Sunday from 9:00 am to 5:00 pm.

  • Supervision to be provided by Ms. BB or a person nominated by the ICL.

  • Father permitted to send one letter, card, or gift per week via the Mother's postal address.

  • Mother restrained from taking children to health professionals (except emergencies or Dr. CC) without Father's written agreement.

  • Mother restrained from facilitating children's attendance with Police or Child Safety unless required by law.

### Reasoning

  • The court found it impossible to make final findings on the abuse allegations at an interim stage but adopted a cautious approach.

  • The primary objective was to provide the children with a period of respite from exposure to conflict.

  • Supervision was deemed necessary to prevent further involvement of the children in the dispute and to protect the Father from potentially unfounded allegations.

  • The court preferred the Mother as the primary carer pending trial to maintain stability, noting she was historically the primary carer for the girls.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The children were found to be 'caught in the middle' of 'warring parents' and were carrying the burden of the dispute.

  • The risk of contamination of evidence was high due to the children being interviewed by multiple parties (Police, Child Safety, Ms. EE, Mr. F).

  • The Father's admitted history of 'vile and abusive' text messages to the Mother.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 60CC: Best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.

  • s 60CC(2)(b): The need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm.

  • s 60CG: Ensuring parenting orders do not expose persons to an unacceptable risk of family violence.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • Ms. EE's report was criticized for being in narrative form and failing to detail specific questions and responses.

  • Conflicting accounts of whether the Mother initiated a child support re-assessment on the day she withheld the children.

  • Lack of independent evidence regarding the Father's alleged drug use beyond D's report to Ms. EE.
summary_sections.family_violence_safety:
### Incidents

  • Father admitted to sending vile and abusive text messages to the Mother.

  • Allegations of sexualized conduct by the Father toward the children.

  • Allegations of physical abuse (bruising) toward D.

### Protection Orders

  • Protection orders exist against both parties for the protection of the other.

  • A protection order exists against the Mother's mother where the Father is the aggrieved.

### Child Exposure

  • Children exposed to 'intense, overt parental conflict and mistrust.'

  • Children pleaded for 'peace' during interviews with the Family Report writer.

### Orders

  • Injunctions restraining the Mother from facilitating children's attendance with police or child safety unless required by law.

  • Supervised time for the Father on Sundays.

### Reasoning

  • The court aimed to shield the children from further 'unnecessary investigations' and the trauma of being interviewed repeatedly about allegations.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found the parents were using 'small pockets of information' from the children to make complaints to authorities.

  • The children's psychological well-being was severely compromised by the ongoing litigation and conflict.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 4AB: Definition of family violence.

  • s 60CC(2): Primary considerations regarding protection from harm.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The court noted a high risk of 'contamination' of the children's evidence due to the Mother's involvement and Ms. EE's questioning methods.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case highlights the court's priority on providing children with 'respite' from high-conflict litigation, even when allegations of abuse cannot be definitively proven at an interim stage."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritized the children's immediate psychological need for 'peace' over the parents' claims for equal time.

  • The expert evidence from Dr. DD regarding the Father's narcissistic traits and Mr. F regarding the children's distress were decisive in shifting the arrangement from equal time to supervised time.

  • The risk of 'investigative trauma' led to injunctions against the Mother to prevent further unnecessary police/child safety involvement.

### Statutory Pivots

  • The interplay between s 60CC (best interests) and the court's power to make interim orders to manage risk and conflict pending a full trial.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The Mother's credibility was questioned due to a history of 'dubious' accusations and the potential coaching of children.

  • The Father's credibility was undermined by his admitted 'vile' text messages and Dr. DD's assessment of his narcissistic personality traits.

  • Ms. EE (Psychologist) was viewed as having lost objectivity and becoming an 'advocate' for the Mother.

  • The court noted a significant risk of 'contamination' of the children's evidence, making their 'disclosures' difficult to rely upon without cross-examination at trial.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Suspension of paragraphs 17 and 18 of the 3 December 2013 Order.

  • Suspension of paragraphs 2, 3, and 4 of the 7 July 2016 Order.

  • Trial listed for 3 April 2018 for 4 days.

  • Father to pay setting down and hearing fees by 6 March 2018.

  • Specific directions for filing affidavits of evidence-in-chief and reply material.

  • Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) to notify Dr. DD and Mr. F of trial dates.

  • Compliance hearing listed for 29 March 2018.

  • Ongoing duty of full and frank disclosure pursuant to Chapter 13 of the Family Law Rules 2004.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge's overarching logic was to stabilize the children's environment before the final trial.

  • The court balanced the Father's right to a relationship with the children against the risk of psychological harm caused by the 'warring' parents.

  • The decision to impose supervision was a risk-management strategy to protect the children from potential abuse and the Father from further allegations.

  • The court emphasized that interim proceedings are confined to issues requiring determination prior to a proper trial, rather than making final findings of fact.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties entered into consent property orders on 11 July 2017.

  • The wife applied to set aside the 11 July 2017 orders pursuant to s 79A of the Family Law Act 1975.

  • The wife alleges a gross underestimation of the property pool and asserts fraud or abuse of process regarding the making of the consent orders.

  • The wife claims she was unaware that the documents she signed were consent orders and seeks to disassociate herself from them.

  • The parties' adult son, Mr B Seaton, was approached to provide evidence regarding the circumstances of the consent orders.

  • Mr B Seaton refused to provide evidence voluntarily, citing concerns of self-incrimination, and sought a certificate under s 128 of the Evidence Act 1995.

  • A subpoena was issued for Mr B Seaton to attend court on 27 November 2018.

  • The court considered whether to compel the son's evidence and provide statutory protection.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Not specified in this interlocutory judgment.

  • DISPUTED: List of assets and liabilities in the 11 July 2017 application for consent orders - Wife alleges gross underestimation.

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not stated; wife alleges significant property was omitted or undervalued.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court noted the significant financial impact on both parties regarding the application to set aside the 2017 orders.

### Orders

  • Mr B Seaton to file and serve an affidavit on or before 17 December 2018.

  • The affidavit must address the adequacy of the list of assets and liabilities in the 11 July 2017 consent order application.

  • The affidavit must respond to the wife's assertions regarding the circumstances leading to the 11 July 2017 orders as set out in the affidavits of Mr C (filed 19 November 2018) and the wife (filed 23 November 2018).

### Reasoning

  • The court determined that the son's evidence is important to the resolution of the s 79A application.

  • Justice Benjamin held that given the significant financial impact on both parties, it is appropriate for the material to be filed to ensure justice.

  • The court acknowledged that the son would only provide evidence if compelled by subpoena and protected by a s 128 certificate.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The wife's allegations of fraud and gross underestimation of assets are sufficient to warrant the compulsion of evidence from a third party (the son).

  • The son's reluctance to testify without protection suggests his evidence may involve admissions of illegal behavior or abuse of process related to the 2017 orders.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act 1975: The underlying basis for the litigation (setting aside orders for fraud/miscarriage of justice).

  • s 128 Evidence Act 1995: The mechanism used to compel testimony while protecting the witness from self-incrimination.

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The wife's evidence regarding fraud and non-disclosure remains 'wholly untested'.

  • The son's evidence has not yet been filed or subjected to cross-examination.

  • No independent valuation of the disputed property pool was provided in this judgment.
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ['This case addresses the procedural intersection of financial non-disclosure allegations and the privilege against self-incrimination for non-party witnesses.']

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court prioritizes the 'interests of justice' in uncovering potential fraud over a witness's desire to remain silent, provided statutory protections (s 128) are available.

  • The use of a s 128 certificate is a critical tool in s 79A applications where family members may have been complicit in financial misrepresentations.

### Statutory Pivots

  • Interplay between s 79A of the Family Law Act (substantive right to set aside orders) and s 128 of the Evidence Act (procedural protection for witnesses).

  • Rule 19.50 of the Family Law Rules 2004 regarding the certification of costs for Senior Counsel in complex evidentiary matters.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility is at risk as her claims of being unaware of the nature of consent orders are 'wholly untested' and contradict the existence of signed legal documents.

  • The husband faces significant credibility risk if the son's compelled evidence corroborates the wife's allegations of a 'gross underestimation' of assets.

  • The son's credibility will be a central issue, as his need for a s 128 certificate implies his prior actions or statements regarding the 2017 orders may have been legally compromised.

  • The court noted that the son's evidence will be 'untested' until cross-examination, indicating a cautious approach to the reliability of all three primary actors.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • The Court issues a certificate in favour of Mr B Seaton pursuant to s 128 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth).

  • Mr B Seaton is ordered to file and serve an affidavit by 17 December 2018 addressing asset adequacy and the circumstances of the 11 July 2017 consent orders.

  • Costs of all parties are reserved.

  • Affidavits to be served upon the legal practitioners for the applicant and respondent.

  • Certified pursuant to Rule 19.50 of the Family Law Rules 2004 that it was reasonable to engage Senior Counsel and Counsel.
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • Justice Benjamin's overarching logic was that the court cannot properly determine a s 79A application involving allegations of fraud without the evidence of key participants, even if those participants require legal immunity.

  • The court balanced the husband and wife's right to a fair trial (which requires all relevant evidence) against the son's right to avoid self-incrimination.

  • The decision to grant the s 128 certificate was a pragmatic step to unlock 'untested' evidence that is central to the wife's claim of gross financial underestimation.

  • The court avoided making premature findings of fact, emphasizing that the son's evidence would still be subject to cross-examination and judicial scrutiny.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties were married for a long duration and have significant financial assets.

  • Consent orders regarding property were previously made in the Sydney Registry of the Family Court.

  • The wife filed an application under s 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 to set aside the consent orders.

  • The wife alleges the consent orders were obtained through misleading and deceptive conduct by the husband and/or other family members.

  • The wife seeks to subpoena the parties' adult son, Mr. B Seaton, to provide evidence regarding the alleged concealment of the nature and effect of the consent orders.

  • Mr. B Seaton, through his solicitor, indicated an unwillingness to provide information unless protected by a certificate under s 128 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth).

  • The wife asserts that the son is caught 'between a rock and a hard place' regarding his testimony.

  • The court previously ordered the anonymisation of the proceedings to prevent identification in Tasmania.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • AGREED: Matrimonial assets - Value described as a 'considerable amount of money'

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Not specified in this interim judgment

### Just & Equitable

  • The wife contends that the existing consent orders are not just and equitable due to alleged misleading and deceptive conduct.

### Reasoning

  • The court noted the wife's reliance on her Notice of Facts, Issues and Contentions alleging deception.

  • The judge acknowledged hearsay evidence in the wife's affidavit regarding steps taken to conceal the effect of orders.

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court found there are 'sensible reasons' for the witness (the son) to be required to give evidence sooner rather than later to resolve the s 79A dispute.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 79A Family Law Act 1975 (setting aside orders)

  • s 128 Evidence Act 1995 (privilege against self-incrimination)

### Evidentiary Gaps

  • The specific nature of the 'misleading conduct' remains a matter of disputed testimony and hearsay at this stage.

  • The full extent of the asset pool is not detailed in this procedural judgment.
summary_sections.children_parenting:
### Child Ages

  • Adult son (Mr. B Seaton)
summary_sections.overall_impact_analysis:
### Overall Impact Analysis

  • ["This case clarifies the procedural requirements for obtaining a s 128 certificate in the Family Court, specifically addressing the necessity of 'compulsion' for the privilege against self-incrimination to apply to evidence-in-chief."]

### Pivotal Findings

  • The court adopted the reasoning from CFMEU v ABCC (2018) and Field & Kingston (2018), moving away from the earlier Ferrall decision.

  • The judge determined that a witness can object to giving evidence-in-chief under s 128 if they are compelled by subpoena.

  • The court affirmed its inherent power to control its own processes by ordering the early filing of affidavit material to ensure procedural fairness.

### Statutory Pivots

  • s 128 Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) - The primary mechanism for witness protection against self-incrimination.

  • s 79A Family Law Act 1975 - The underlying substantive application regarding the setting aside of property orders.

  • Rule 19.50 Family Law Rules 2004 - Justification for Senior Counsel costs.
summary_sections.general_credibility_risk:
### General Credibility & Risk

  • The wife's credibility on the s 79A application is yet to be tested, as her current evidence includes hearsay regarding the husband's alleged deception.

  • The husband's credibility is under challenge due to allegations of misleading and deceptive conduct in obtaining the original consent orders.

  • The adult son is viewed as a neutral but reluctant witness whose evidence is critical to the 'misleading conduct' claim.

  • The court noted that the husband's solicitor did not take technical objections to the wife swearing affidavits in an anonymised name, suggesting a focus on substantive rather than procedural disputes.
summary_sections.outcome_orders:
### Final Orders

  • Proceedings adjourned to 9:30 am on 6 December 2018 at Hobart.

  • Leave granted for the wife to issue a subpoena to Mr. B Seaton to attend court on 6 December 2018.

  • Leave granted for the husband's solicitor to attend by video-link from Sydney.

  • Mr. B Seaton ordered to file any affidavit in support of a s 128 Evidence Act certificate four days prior to 6 December 2018.

  • Mr. B Seaton directed to file evidence-in-chief by affidavit on or before 18 December 2018.

  • Husband to file a Response to the wife's Notice of Facts, Issues and Contentions within 48 hours of the son's affidavit or 19 December 2018.

  • Costs of both parties reserved.

  • Certification that it was reasonable to engage Senior Counsel (Rule 19.50).
summary_sections.reasons_rationale:
### Reasons & Rationale

  • The judge reasoned that the wife's application to set aside orders based on deception required the son's evidence to be secured promptly.

  • The court followed the modern interpretation of s 128, which requires a witness to be 'compellable' (e.g., via subpoena) before a certificate can be granted, even for evidence-in-chief.

  • The judge balanced the son's need for protection against self-incrimination with the wife's right to pursue her s 79A claim.

  • The use of video-link and specific filing timelines was deemed necessary to manage the interstate nature of the parties (Sydney/Tasmania) and the sensitivity of the family dynamics.

summary_sections.facts:
### Facts

  • The parties commenced cohabitation in 2010 and married in 2011.

  • Separation occurred on 1 September 2018.

  • The Wife is 52 years old, a health professional, and has been unemployed for four years.

  • The Husband is 50 years old, a manager, and is currently employed in the Middle East.

  • The primary asset is a property at D Street, Suburb E, Queensland, purchased in 2012.

  • The property was initially in the Husband's name; a transfer of a one-half interest to the Wife was signed in 2016 but never registered.

  • The Wife alleges a history of family violence, including physical assault and controlling behavior.

  • The Husband cancelled the Wife's airline ticket to the Middle East and blocked her access to savings and credit cards upon separation.

  • The Wife discovered the D Street property was listed for sale on realestate.com.au on 23 November 2018 without her knowledge.

  • The Wife identified suspicious transfers by the Husband: $267,000 in July 2017 and $270,000 in September 2017 to a UK account.

  • The Husband claimed via text on 2 December 2018 that the mortgage balance is $604,000, whereas the Wife believed it was $90,000 a year prior.
summary_sections.property_division:
### Asset Pool

  • DISPUTED: D Street Property, Suburb E - Wife estimated $850,000 equity based on $90,000 mortgage; Husband claims $604,000 mortgage balance.

  • DISPUTED: ANZ Account Transfer - $267,000 (July 2017).

  • DISPUTED: G Bank Account (UK) Transfer - $270,000 (September 2017).

  • TOTAL NET POOL: Undetermined at this interim stage.

### Contributions

  • The parties together constructed a dwelling on the D Street land starting in 2013.

  • The Wife deposes that the Husband executed a transfer of 50% interest to her in 2016, though unregistered.

### Future Needs

  • Wife is currently unemployed (4 years) and 52 years old.

  • Husband is employed as a manager in the Middle East.

### Just & Equitable

  • The court found it proper to grant an injunction to preserve the property interests pending a full hearing.

### Orders
